source.thread:
Dev Patel: Anyone familiar w/ usage pipeline + streaming cancels? Northwind AI says billed tokens > expected. Hypothesis: retries + our attempt-level request_id. SUP-1842.

Ava Chen: UI groups by request_id (attempt). Customers often assume its stable across retries. Maybe we need copy + docs update.

Ben Carter: Any chance completion tokens get double-counted on stream reconnect? Customer uses 15s timeout + retry once.

Dev Patel: I checked 3 sample req ids. completion tokens == sum of streamed chunks. prompt counted once per admitted attempt. On retry, both attempts show up. No evidence of double-counting so far.

Priya Natarajan: +1 to comms. Also maybe recommend idempotency key usage (if supported) or avoid auto-retry on timeouts.

Dev Patel: Ill add a note to KB + propose UI hint: Retries are separate billable attempts.

source.messages:
Ethan Park: Question for folks thinking about route-level enablement: should the route config allow setting an explicit quant profile ID (ex: `qp_llama3_70b_int8_v3`) OR do we only allow aliases like `recommended` / `latest`?

Mina Haddad: My gut says explicit IDs only. Aliases feel like a footgun unless we define *exactly* when they resolve. Determinism + audit gets messy if `recommended` changes out from under you.

Ravi Menon: +1. Policy engine needs deterministic behavior. If alias resolves at request time, then two requests with same route config could apply different profiles depending on publish cadence.

Benji Okafor: From API ergonomics POV customers will ask for “just give me the best one”. Aliases are attractive. But we can do it in Console UX without storing it as alias maybe?

Jordan Lee: The determinism requirement is pretty strict. If we support `recommended`, I’d prefer it resolves at *write time* (set route config), and we store the concrete profile id. That keeps routing/runtime deterministic and audit diffs meaningful.

Ethan Park: Right, the field is currently drafted as `quantization_profile_ref`. We’ve got `default` (meaning baseline precision) and then a ref. If we allow alias, the question is whether we persist `alias:recommended` or persist resolved id.

Priya Natarajan: We should avoid “silent change” semantics. Customers will be unhappy if output shifts because we published a new recommended profile and their route updated implicitly.

Chloe Martin: Console team concern: if a user clicks “Enable recommended profile”, they’ll expect it to track updates. But if we store resolved id, then “recommended” is basically a convenience selector, not an ongoing policy.

Mina Haddad: Convenience selector is fine. If we want tracking, that’s a different feature (and needs explicit consent + change control). Also for compliance customers, implicit changes are a no-go.

Benji Okafor: Another option: allow alias in API but require a `pinned: true/false` flag? If pinned false, then it’s dynamic. But that’s basically v2.

Ravi Menon: Dynamic alias means policy engine has to re-resolve on each config refresh and we need to answer “which version was applied for request X” reliably. That is doable if runtime logs include resolved `quant_profile_id`, but still breaks “same config => same behavior”.

Jordan Lee: Also impacts rollback. If alias points to v4 and quality regresses, you need to repoint alias globally or coordinate. With explicit IDs, you can just flip route back to baseline or prior known-good.

Ethan Park: How do we handle multi-region / dedicated pools? If someone sets `recommended`, it might differ per GPU SKU. Alias might resolve to different profile ids depending on region/hardware.

Mina Haddad: Exactly. “Recommended” is not a single thing unless scoped by model + hardware class + max context regime. If we do alias, we’ll need to define the resolution key.

Chloe Martin: Console can still show “Recommended for your current deployment (H100 class)” and store the resolved id. If user later changes region/tier and that profile becomes incompatible, policy should fallback to baseline and show warning.

Priya Natarajan: That sounds aligned with guardrails. Store explicit id in route config. Policy engine checks compatibility; if incompatible, fallback. No dynamic alias.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: Works for API too: accept `quantization_profile_ref` as either a concrete id OR a special token `recommended` in the *request*, but the server will translate it to concrete id before persisting. We just need to document that behavior.

Ravi Menon: Important: we should be explicit that `recommended` is “resolve now” not “follow updates”. And the audit log should include both (requested alias + stored id) maybe.

Jordan Lee: +1. For audit: record `requested_ref=recommended`, `resolved_profile_id=qp_...v3`, `resolution_scope=model+variant+gpu_class`.

Ethan Park: ok. I’ll update the config service spec accordingly: route settings accept `default` | `profile_id` | `recommended` (server-resolved), but storage is always `default` or concrete `profile_id`.

Chloe Martin: Can we also add a UI string like “This will pin the current recommended profile version; future updates won’t apply unless you change it.”? Otherwise people will assume it auto-updates.

Priya Natarajan: Yes, and that message is important for enterprise change control.

Mina Haddad: Also: if a profile gets deprecated/blocked, we should return a clear error on enable and/or automatically fallback with a big warning. Prefer blocking enable.

Ravi Menon: For existing routes pointing at deprecated id, fallback + metric `quant_profile_resolution=fallback_deprecated` + alert.

Benji Okafor: I’ll sync with Jordan on error codes and how the API surfaces “alias resolved” vs “explicit id accepted”.

Jordan Lee: I’ll drop a note in the ADR draft section on alias semantics: allowed as input, never persisted; deterministic storage; no silent upgrades.

Ethan Park: Cool. Action items: Benji update OpenAPI + client types, Chloe/Tessa align on Console copy, Jordan add to ADR. Thanks all.

Chloe Martin: :white_check_mark: will add to Console ticket notes.

Priya Natarajan: :thumbsup:

source.text:
Marissa Cole: now that route experiments is GA-ish (at least rollout plan is locked), can we list the post-GA followups in one place? mostly: (1) stat sig / analysis improvements, (2) possible custom metric ingestion roadmap. dropping notes here so we don’t lose them.

Dylan Brooks: +1. also would be good to capture what we *won’t* do (so this doesn’t turn into “console is an analytics product”).

Jada Williams: agreed. right now results API is “basic deltas + guardrails + trend charts”. we should be explicit what analysis is MVP vs future.

Logan Wright: starting list (telemetry/analytics angle):
- significance testing: we currently show deltas without claiming significance. followup: add optional “confidence” signal based on sample size / variance.
- custom metrics ingestion: today only Redwood-defined metrics + eval streams. followup: explore limited customer-defined metrics, but need tight constraints (cardinality, units, aggregation windows).

Allison Grant: if we touch significance, we need to avoid people reading it as “ship/no ship button”. also sequential testing / peeking is a whole can of worms.

Sean Gallagher: + also on-call perspective: anything that affects guardrail/auto-stop must be explainable + debuggable. if we add stats, it should not gate auto-stop (or if it does, needs a kill switch + clear failure modes).

Marissa Cole: let’s break down into sections + propose owners. I’ll turn into Linear tickets after.

Jordan Lee: on significance: do we want frequentist (t-test-ish) vs bayesian? because the data is not i.i.d. and we have heavy tails on latency + retries.

Mei Lin: from datasets perspective, we have per-variant rollups at {route, experiment_id, variant, model, region, time_bucket}. anything more advanced needs raw-ish distributions or at least histogram sketches. we do not store full raw request events for long.

Caleb Johnson: and we’ll need to be careful about metric definitions:
- latency: are we doing end-to-end request duration, or model inference only? we have both in OTel spans.
- cost: token accounting can be delayed for streaming; we’ve already seen backfills.
if we compute stats, we need to be consistent about which signal we’re using.

Jonas Weber: quality angle: if we do stats for eval scores, we need to account for sampling (evals are not on 100% traffic) + prompt-set drift. otherwise we’ll get false “improvements”.

Selene Huang: also if we add “significance” to quality, we need guardrails for regression alerts: ex. require minimum N prompts, minimum coverage per variant, and show “insufficient data” explicitly.

Dylan Brooks: proposal: Phase 1 post-GA = “decision support” only:
- show “insufficient data” vs “directional” vs “stable” labels
- do *not* show p-values in UI
- keep doc text very explicit: not a guarantee, don’t use for compliance decisions

Marissa Cole: yes. we can also add a tooltip / docs link. customers keep asking “is this stat sig?” but we can answer with “not yet; here’s how to interpret.”

Logan Wright: concrete followups for significance:
1) Add sample size + variance indicators per metric (N, stddev / IQR) in results API (or derived fields).
2) Consider lightweight bootstrapped CI for deltas (esp latency percentiles are gnarly though).
3) Explore histogram sketches (t-digest / HDR hist) so we can estimate p95/p99 variance better.

Allison Grant: +1 on histogram sketches. otherwise people will do math on percentiles and it’ll be wrong.

Sean Gallagher: note: anything adding histograms increases ingestion/storage cost. we should bound it: maybe only for routes w/ active experiments, and only for the top-level route template (avoid raw path cardinality).

Caleb Johnson: custom metric ingestion followups (constraints-first):
- define allowed metric types: gauge/counter only? no distributions initially.
- require customer-provided unit + description (ms, %, $)
- strict dimension allowlist (no arbitrary tags). at most {experiment_id, variant_id, route_id, region} + one customer dimension?
- minimum reporting interval (1m / 5m) and max series.

Jordan Lee: how would customers report? header-based? SDK call? OTel?

Logan Wright: I’d lean “OTel bridge” long-term but that’s huge. near-term: Redwood API endpoint where customer posts time-bucketed metrics keyed by {experiment_id, variant_id} (and we validate it matches our known experiment + variant). keeps us out of the request path.

Dylan Brooks: endpoint approach sounds feasible but needs auth + tenancy + retention policies. also question: how do they compute variant_id client-side? they only know what headers we return.

Jada Williams: if we go that route, we probably need:
- stable response headers already include experiment_id + variant_id (we have those)
- docs: “collect metrics in your app, aggregate, then upload per time window”
- but this becomes “bring your own analytics” and may not work for all customers.

Marissa Cole: also, we should decide what problem we’re solving. most asks I’ve heard:
- “conversion rate” / “task success rate”
- “human rating score”
- “downstream error rate” (like tool call success)
Those are not infra metrics.

Selene Huang: and those are easy to game / misinterpret. we need disclaimers + maybe a template: “custom metric must be monotonic + clearly defined”.

Jonas Weber: alternative: let them attach eval suites (Redwood-managed) as their “quality” metric, instead of ingesting their own. that’s closer to what we can trust/standardize.

Mei Lin: if we do customer metric upload, we should treat it as “external metric” dataset. do not mix with guardrails/auto-stop initially. display-only.

Sean Gallagher: +1000. external metrics must not page on-call.

Connor O’Brien: can we capture these as post-GA tickets with rough sequencing? release train planning will want a bucket.

Marissa Cole: yup. rough ticket list (draft):
- PostGA-1: Results UI/Docs: add “insufficient data” states + explanation of deltas (owner: Marissa/Dylan)
- PostGA-2: Results API: add sample size/variance fields + metric definition versioning (owner: Logan/Mei)
- PostGA-3: Telemetry: evaluate histogram sketch for latency distributions during experiments (owner: Caleb/Allison; include cost estimate)
- PostGA-4: Analysis: explore lightweight CI / bayesian-ish approach, but keep out of UI until validated (owner: Mei + Jordan)
- PostGA-5: Custom metrics discovery: customer interviews + constraints doc + proposal (owner: Marissa + Logan)
- PostGA-6: “External metric upload” spike (authz, schema, retention) (owner: Logan/Caleb)
- PostGA-7: Quality: expand eval integration + sampling guidance so customers can use Redwood evals instead of custom metrics (owner: Jonas/Selene)

Dylan Brooks: looks good. add note: anything touching “significance” must go through design review + legal-ish copy review (we don’t want implied guarantees).

Allison Grant: and SRE review for anything that changes telemetry volume.

Marissa Cole: ack. I’ll file these + link here. if anyone thinks of more, add to thread.

Sean Gallagher: last ask: when we file PostGA-3, include explicit rollback plan + a feature flag. we don’t want a repeat of the staging metrics blowup.

Marissa Cole: yep, will do.

Sean Gallagher: + also on-call perspective: anything that affects guardrail/auto-stop must be explainable + debuggable. if we add stats, it should not gate auto-stop (or if it does, needs a kill switch + clear failure modes).

Marissa Cole: let’s break down into sections + propose owners. I’ll turn into Linear tickets after.

Jordan Lee: on significance: do we want frequentist (t-test-ish) vs bayesian? because the data is not i.i.d. and we have heavy tails on latency + retries.

Mei Lin: from datasets perspective, we have per-variant rollups at {route, experiment_id, variant, model, region, time_bucket}. anything more advanced needs raw-ish distributions or at least histogram sketches. we do not store full raw request events for long.

Caleb Johnson: and we’ll need to be careful about metric definitions:
- latency: are we doing end-to-end request duration, or model inference only? we have both in OTel spans.
- cost: token accounting can be delayed for streaming; we’ve already seen backfills.
if we compute stats, we need to be consistent about which signal we’re using.

Jonas Weber: quality angle: if we do stats for eval scores, we need to account for sampling (evals are not on 100% traffic) + prompt-set drift. otherwise we’ll get false “improvements”.

Selene Huang: also if we add “significance” to quality, we need guardrails for regression alerts: ex. require minimum N prompts, minimum coverage per variant, and show “insufficient data” explicitly.

Dylan Brooks: proposal: Phase 1 post-GA = “decision support” only:
- show “insufficient data” vs “directional” vs “stable” labels
- do *not* show p-values in UI
- keep doc text very explicit: not a guarantee, don’t use for compliance decisions

Marissa Cole: yes. we can also add a tooltip / docs link. customers keep asking “is this stat sig?” but we can answer with “not yet; here’s how to interpret.”

Logan Wright: concrete followups for significance:
1) Add sample size + variance indicators per metric (N, stddev / IQR) in results API (or derived fields).
2) Consider lightweight bootstrapped CI for deltas (esp latency percentiles are gnarly though).
3) Explore histogram sketches (t-digest / HDR hist) so we can estimate p95/p99 variance better.

Allison Grant: +1 on histogram sketches. otherwise people will do math on percentiles and it’ll be wrong.

Sean Gallagher: note: anything adding histograms increases ingestion/storage cost. we should bound it: maybe only for routes w/ active experiments, and only for the top-level route template (avoid raw path cardinality).

Caleb Johnson: custom metric ingestion followups (constraints-first):
- define allowed metric types: gauge/counter only? no distributions initially.
- require customer-provided unit + description (ms, %, $)
- strict dimension allowlist (no arbitrary tags). at most {experiment_id, variant_id, route_id, region} + one customer dimension?
- minimum reporting interval (1m / 5m) and max series.

Jordan Lee: how would customers report? header-based? SDK call? OTel?

Logan Wright: I’d lean “OTel bridge” long-term but that’s huge. near-term: Redwood API endpoint where customer posts time-bucketed metrics keyed by {experiment_id, variant_id} (and we validate it matches our known experiment + variant). keeps us out of the request path.

Dylan Brooks: endpoint approach sounds feasible but needs auth + tenancy + retention policies. also question: how do they compute variant_id client-side? they only know what headers we return.

Jada Williams: if we go that route, we probably need:
- stable response headers already include experiment_id + variant_id (we have those)
- docs: “collect metrics in your app, aggregate, then upload per time window”
- but this becomes “bring your own analytics” and may not work for all customers.

Marissa Cole: also, we should decide what problem we’re solving. most asks I’ve heard:
- “conversion rate” / “task success rate”
- “human rating score”
- “downstream error rate” (like tool call success)
Those are not infra metrics.

Selene Huang: and those are easy to game / misinterpret. we need disclaimers + maybe a template: “custom metric must be monotonic + clearly defined”.

Jonas Weber: alternative: let them attach eval suites (Redwood-managed) as their “quality” metric, instead of ingesting their own. that’s closer to what we can trust/standardize.

Mei Lin: if we do customer metric upload, we should treat it as “external metric” dataset. do not mix with guardrails/auto-stop initially. display-only.

Sean Gallagher: +1000. external metrics must not page on-call.

Connor O’Brien: can we capture these as post-GA tickets with rough sequencing? release train planning will want a bucket.

Marissa Cole: yup. rough ticket list (draft):
- PostGA-1: Results UI/Docs: add “insufficient data” states + explanation of deltas (owner: Marissa/Dylan)
- PostGA-2: Results API: add sample size/variance fields + metric definition versioning (owner: Logan/Mei)
- PostGA-3: Telemetry: evaluate histogram sketch for latency distributions during experiments (owner: Caleb/Allison; include cost estimate)
- PostGA-4: Analysis: explore lightweight CI / bayesian-ish approach, but keep out of UI until validated (owner: Mei + Jordan)
- PostGA-5: Custom metrics discovery: customer interviews + constraints doc + proposal (owner: Marissa + Logan)
- PostGA-6: “External metric upload” spike (authz, schema, retention) (owner: Logan/Caleb)
- PostGA-7: Quality: expand eval integration + sampling guidance so customers can use Redwood evals instead of custom metrics (owner: Jonas/Selene)

Dylan Brooks: looks good. add note: anything touching “significance” must go through design review + legal-ish copy review (we don’t want implied guarantees).

Allison Grant: and SRE review for anything that changes telemetry volume.

Marissa Cole: ack. I’ll file these + link here. if anyone thinks of more, add to thread.

Sean Gallagher: last ask: when we file PostGA-3, include explicit rollback plan + a feature flag. we don’t want a repeat of the staging metrics blowup.

Marissa Cole: yep, will do.

source.text:
Benji Okafor: seeing flaky behavior in the promote endpoint under retries. In the e2e test we sometimes “double-promote” from stage 1 -> 2 -> 3 when Console retries after a 502. We need to lock down idempotency + optimistic concurrency before beta.

Benji Okafor: current behavior: `POST /v1/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollout_plans/{plan_id}:promote` is not idempotent. If Console times out but server commits, retry does another promote.

Jordan Lee: +1, we should treat promote like a write action that must be at-most-once per client intent.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
1) require `Idempotency-Key` header for action endpoints (`promote`, `abort`, `hold`, `force_promote`).
2) require an expected version for optimistic concurrency (either `If-Match: W/"plan_version"` or explicit `expected_plan_version` in body).
3) server stores the idempotency record keyed by `(plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key)` and returns the same response on replay.

Jordan Lee: if same idempotency key is reused w/ different payload (e.g. different `reason` or `requested_stage_index`), return 400.

Ethan Park: +1 overall. I’m a little worried about “required” idempotency key for internal callers. Are we ok with making it mandatory now or do we do “strongly recommended + enforce for Console”?

Jada Williams: Console will do retries automatically (our gateway + browser retry on flaky networks). If it’s not mandatory, we’ll miss it in some path and we’ll be back here.

Chloe Martin: fwiw Console backend can always send one. We can generate a UUID per button click and persist it client-side while the request is in-flight.

Benji Okafor: +1 for mandatory. The failures we saw were from the backend job that replays on 5xx.

Jordan Lee: also: optimistic concurrency alone doesn’t solve the retry case. If request #1 succeeded and bumped `plan_version`, request #2 (retry) might use a stale version and fail with 409, which is “safe”, but the UI shows an error even though it promoted.

Jordan Lee: idempotency gives us “retry returns success with same result” vs “retry returns conflict”. Much nicer.

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Ethan Park: what’s the exact contract? Example?

Jordan Lee: something like:
```
POST ...:promote
Idempotency-Key: 6b10f5d4-0b18-4d90-bb5a-9b3a86d7a0c9
If-Match: W/"17"

{ "reason": "canary looks good" }

200
{ "plan_id": "rp_123", "previous_stage": 1, "current_stage": 2, "plan_version": 18 }
```

Jordan Lee: If client retries with same key, we return the same 200 payload (even if If-Match is stale / missing on retry).

Jordan Lee: If client sends different key but stale If-Match, return `409` with current plan state:
```
409
{ "error": "version_conflict", "current_plan_version": 18, "current_stage": 2 }
```

Benji Okafor: question: should idempotency bypass authz? (i.e., if user A promoted, and user B replays the same key, do we return the cached response?)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: pls do NOT bypass authz. Cached result should still require caller to be authorized for the action (at least “can view plan”); otherwise you can leak that a promote happened + stage index.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’d suggest: store `actor_id` / `actor_type` with idempotency record and require the replaying request to match the same actor, or at minimum same org + same permission level. Strongest is “must be same actor”.

Jordan Lee: +1 on same actor. If Console retries, it’s same service principal anyway.

Chloe Martin: careful: Console backend uses service-to-service token, but the end-user actor is in `X-Redwood-Acting-User`. We sometimes replay with the same service principal but a different acting user depending on session refresh.

Chloe Martin: could we scope “same actor” to the service principal + org, and treat `acting_user` as audit-only?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok with that as long as:
- the cached response does not get returned to a different org
- and we still log the actual actor for the *first* commit
- replays should not emit additional audit events

Benji Okafor: +1, main need is preventing double side-effects.

Priya Natarajan: for v1: enforce that the idempotency record is scoped to `(org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)` where `auth_principal_id` is the service principal (Console backend / operator tooling). Don’t include acting user in the match requirement.

Priya Natarajan: Kaitlyn can we live with that for beta? If yes, we’ll document.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, with the constraints above. Also ensure the response doesn’t include anything beyond what the principal could fetch via GET anyway.

Ethan Park: storage details: do we need a new table?

Benji Okafor: I can add `rollout_action_requests` (or similar) with:
- org_id
- plan_id
- action_type
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal_id
- request_hash
- status (in_progress|completed|failed)
- response_body (or key fields)
- created_at, completed_at
Unique index on (org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)

Jordan Lee: +1. For “in_progress”: if two requests race with same key, second should block or return 409/202.

Jordan Lee: I prefer: second returns `409 idempotency_in_progress` w/ `Retry-After: 1` so client can poll. But Console probably just retries anyway.

Chloe Martin: Console backend can handle `409 idempotency_in_progress` and retry with backoff. Alternatively we can return `202`.

Ethan Park: 202 semantics are confusing here. I’d do 409 w/ specific code.

Benji Okafor: ack.

Benji Okafor: also, how do we prevent *different keys* from double-promoting concurrently? E.g. two operators click promote at same time.

Jordan Lee: that’s optimistic concurrency: the action must be evaluated against a known plan_version inside a transaction.

Jordan Lee: DB transaction: `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` on plan row, verify expected_plan_version (or If-Match), compute next stage, write plan, insert audit event, then commit. If expected version mismatch: 409.

Ethan Park: I’m leaning toward body field instead of If-Match just to keep it consistent with our other internal APIs.

Jada Williams: Console prefers If-Match, but we can do either. We already parse ETags on some endpoints.

Benji Okafor: if we do body field, can we still return ETag on GET? would be nice.

Jordan Lee: can do both: accept either `If-Match` OR `expected_plan_version`. If both present and disagree => 400.

Priya Natarajan: +1 accept either. Console can move to If-Match, internal tooling can keep body param.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: make sure the idempotency key can’t be replayed forever. TTL?

Benji Okafor: I was thinking keep records 7d for debugging/audit correlation.

Jordan Lee: 7d is fine, but also cap table growth. Maybe 24h for “dedupe guarantee” and best-effort after? But semantics get weird.

Ethan Park: for action endpoints, we really only need short window. But support will love having it around.

Priya Natarajan: decision: keep for 7d, but only guarantee replay for 24h. After 24h, server *may* return 404 for the idempotency replay and client should treat it as non-retriable. (Console retries will all be within minutes anyway.)

Chloe Martin: can we avoid the “may” language and just keep full replay for 7d? Implementation is the same if we store response.

Benji Okafor: storing response body for 7d is ok, it’s small.

Jordan Lee: I’d rather keep deterministic: if record exists, replay forever until GC. So “guarantee” equals retention. If we retain 7d, we guarantee 7d.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: retention for idempotency record is not audit log retention, but ok. Just ensure it doesn’t include sensitive payload beyond what’s needed.

Priya Natarajan: ok, simplify: guarantee for 7d. We can tune later.

Ethan Park: edge case: first request fails after partially executing (e.g. audit insert succeeded but plan update failed). Idempotency record should mark failed and allow retry?

Jordan Lee: action must be atomic. Plan update + audit insert in same txn. If txn rolls back, idempotency record should not be marked completed.

Benji Okafor: but idempotency record insert needs to happen before we start, otherwise races.

Jordan Lee: pattern:
1) start txn
2) insert idempotency row with status=in_progress (unique constraint)
3) lock plan row
4) validate + perform action
5) update idempotency row status=completed + store response
6) commit

Jordan Lee: if txn fails, idempotency row insert rolls back too.

Benji Okafor: makes sense.

Jada Williams: also note: promote is not instantaneous from data plane perspective, but control plane state change is. So idempotency is about control plane state transition, not “all pods updated”.

Ethan Park: yup.

Chloe Martin: can we include `resulting_action_id` in response so Console can fetch gate evidence / timeline by action id?

Benji Okafor: good idea. Will generate `ra_...` action id. Also helps correlate to audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please ensure audit event includes:
- action_id
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal
- acting user (if present)
- old_stage/new_stage
- reason
But again: replay should NOT emit audit twice.

Jordan Lee: +1. We should also include `idempotency_replayed=true` flag in response headers? maybe too much.

Priya Natarajan: no need. But add a debug log line when replay occurs.

Ethan Park: so final behaviors:
- same key => same response (200 or 409?), no extra side effects
- different key + same expected version => one wins, other 409
- missing key => 400
- missing expected version => ???

Jordan Lee: missing expected version should be 400 too. Otherwise two parallel promotes from different clients can both succeed sequentially, which is unexpected.

Jada Williams: agree. Promote should be explicit about what you think current stage/version is.

Benji Okafor: ok: require BOTH `Idempotency-Key` AND expected version.

Priya Natarajan: decision: required for all action endpoints.

Priya Natarajan: Benji can you capture in the PR description + update the API docs in the service doc? (Also ping Connor so QA knows what to test.)

Benji Okafor: yep. I’ll add test cases:
- retry same key returns same `action_id` + stage
- concurrent promote with different keys -> one 200, one 409
- replay with different payload -> 400

Chloe Martin: I’ll update Console backend to always send both headers. For expected version we’ll use ETag from GET plan.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please add one more test: replay with different auth principal should NOT return cached response (should 409/404). We can decide exact code, but shouldn’t leak.

Jordan Lee: suggest 409 `idempotency_key_conflict` if key exists for different principal within same org/plan. That’s safe.

Benji Okafor: ok.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: thanks all

Priya Natarajan: :thumbsup: ship it, but behind the feature flag until Console is updated.

Benji Okafor: (deploy bot voice) will do.

Chloe Martin: :eyes: I’ll coordinate rollout order with release train so we don’t break existing clients.

3) server stores the idempotency record keyed by `(plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key)` and returns the same response on replay.

Jordan Lee: if same idempotency key is reused w/ different payload (e.g. different `reason` or `requested_stage_index`), return 400.

Ethan Park: +1 overall. I’m a little worried about “required” idempotency key for internal callers. Are we ok with making it mandatory now or do we do “strongly recommended + enforce for Console”?

Jada Williams: Console will do retries automatically (our gateway + browser retry on flaky networks). If it’s not mandatory, we’ll miss it in some path and we’ll be back here.

Chloe Martin: fwiw Console backend can always send one. We can generate a UUID per button click and persist it client-side while the request is in-flight.

Benji Okafor: +1 for mandatory. The failures we saw were from the backend job that replays on 5xx.

Jordan Lee: also: optimistic concurrency alone doesn’t solve the retry case. If request #1 succeeded and bumped `plan_version`, request #2 (retry) might use a stale version and fail with 409, which is “safe”, but the UI shows an error even though it promoted.

Jordan Lee: idempotency gives us “retry returns success with same result” vs “retry returns conflict”. Much nicer.

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Ethan Park: what’s the exact contract? Example?

Jordan Lee: something like:
```
POST ...:promote
Idempotency-Key: 6b10f5d4-0b18-4d90-bb5a-9b3a86d7a0c9
If-Match: W/"17"

{ "reason": "canary looks good" }

200
{ "plan_id": "rp_123", "previous_stage": 1, "current_stage": 2, "plan_version": 18 }
```

Jordan Lee: If client retries with same key, we return the same 200 payload (even if If-Match is stale / missing on retry).

Jordan Lee: If client sends different key but stale If-Match, return `409` with current plan state:
```
409
{ "error": "version_conflict", "current_plan_version": 18, "current_stage": 2 }
```

Benji Okafor: question: should idempotency bypass authz? (i.e., if user A promoted, and user B replays the same key, do we return the cached response?)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: pls do NOT bypass authz. Cached result should still require caller to be authorized for the action (at least “can view plan”); otherwise you can leak that a promote happened + stage index.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’d suggest: store `actor_id` / `actor_type` with idempotency record and require the replaying request to match the same actor, or at minimum same org + same permission level. Strongest is “must be same actor”.

Jordan Lee: +1 on same actor. If Console retries, it’s same service principal anyway.

Chloe Martin: careful: Console backend uses service-to-service token, but the end-user actor is in `X-Redwood-Acting-User`. We sometimes replay with the same service principal but a different acting user depending on session refresh.

Chloe Martin: could we scope “same actor” to the service principal + org, and treat `acting_user` as audit-only?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok with that as long as:
- the cached response does not get returned to a different org
- and we still log the actual actor for the *first* commit
- replays should not emit additional audit events

Benji Okafor: +1, main need is preventing double side-effects.

Priya Natarajan: for v1: enforce that the idempotency record is scoped to `(org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)` where `auth_principal_id` is the service principal (Console backend / operator tooling). Don’t include acting user in the match requirement.

Priya Natarajan: Kaitlyn can we live with that for beta? If yes, we’ll document.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, with the constraints above. Also ensure the response doesn’t include anything beyond what the principal could fetch via GET anyway.

Ethan Park: storage details: do we need a new table?

Benji Okafor: I can add `rollout_action_requests` (or similar) with:
- org_id
- plan_id
- action_type
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal_id
- request_hash
- status (in_progress|completed|failed)
- response_body (or key fields)
- created_at, completed_at
Unique index on (org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)

Jordan Lee: +1. For “in_progress”: if two requests race with same key, second should block or return 409/202.

Jordan Lee: I prefer: second returns `409 idempotency_in_progress` w/ `Retry-After: 1` so client can poll. But Console probably just retries anyway.

Chloe Martin: Console backend can handle `409 idempotency_in_progress` and retry with backoff. Alternatively we can return `202`.

Ethan Park: 202 semantics are confusing here. I’d do 409 w/ specific code.

Benji Okafor: ack.

Benji Okafor: also, how do we prevent *different keys* from double-promoting concurrently? E.g. two operators click promote at same time.

Jordan Lee: that’s optimistic concurrency: the action must be evaluated against a known plan_version inside a transaction.

Jordan Lee: DB transaction: `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` on plan row, verify expected_plan_version (or If-Match), compute next stage, write plan, insert audit event, then commit. If expected version mismatch: 409.

Ethan Park: I’m leaning toward body field instead of If-Match just to keep it consistent with our other internal APIs.

Jada Williams: Console prefers If-Match, but we can do either. We already parse ETags on some endpoints.

Benji Okafor: if we do body field, can we still return ETag on GET? would be nice.

Jordan Lee: can do both: accept either `If-Match` OR `expected_plan_version`. If both present and disagree => 400.

Priya Natarajan: +1 accept either. Console can move to If-Match, internal tooling can keep body param.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: make sure the idempotency key can’t be replayed forever. TTL?

Benji Okafor: I was thinking keep records 7d for debugging/audit correlation.

Jordan Lee: 7d is fine, but also cap table growth. Maybe 24h for “dedupe guarantee” and best-effort after? But semantics get weird.

Ethan Park: for action endpoints, we really only need short window. But support will love having it around.

Priya Natarajan: decision: keep for 7d, but only guarantee replay for 24h. After 24h, server *may* return 404 for the idempotency replay and client should treat it as non-retriable. (Console retries will all be within minutes anyway.)

Chloe Martin: can we avoid the “may” language and just keep full replay for 7d? Implementation is the same if we store response.

Benji Okafor: storing response body for 7d is ok, it’s small.

Jordan Lee: I’d rather keep deterministic: if record exists, replay forever until GC. So “guarantee” equals retention. If we retain 7d, we guarantee 7d.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: retention for idempotency record is not audit log retention, but ok. Just ensure it doesn’t include sensitive payload beyond what’s needed.

Priya Natarajan: ok, simplify: guarantee for 7d. We can tune later.

Ethan Park: edge case: first request fails after partially executing (e.g. audit insert succeeded but plan update failed). Idempotency record should mark failed and allow retry?

Jordan Lee: action must be atomic. Plan update + audit insert in same txn. If txn rolls back, idempotency record should not be marked completed.

Benji Okafor: but idempotency record insert needs to happen before we start, otherwise races.

Jordan Lee: pattern:
1) start txn
2) insert idempotency row with status=in_progress (unique constraint)
3) lock plan row
4) validate + perform action
5) update idempotency row status=completed + store response
6) commit

Jordan Lee: if txn fails, idempotency row insert rolls back too.

Benji Okafor: makes sense.

Jada Williams: also note: promote is not instantaneous from data plane perspective, but control plane state change is. So idempotency is about control plane state transition, not “all pods updated”.

Ethan Park: yup.

Chloe Martin: can we include `resulting_action_id` in response so Console can fetch gate evidence / timeline by action id?

Benji Okafor: good idea. Will generate `ra_...` action id. Also helps correlate to audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please ensure audit event includes:
- action_id
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal
- acting user (if present)
- old_stage/new_stage
- reason
But again: replay should NOT emit audit twice.

Jordan Lee: +1. We should also include `idempotency_replayed=true` flag in response headers? maybe too much.

Priya Natarajan: no need. But add a debug log line when replay occurs.

Ethan Park: so final behaviors:
- same key => same response (200 or 409?), no extra side effects
- different key + same expected version => one wins, other 409
- missing key => 400
- missing expected version => ???

Jordan Lee: missing expected version should be 400 too. Otherwise two parallel promotes from different clients can both succeed sequentially, which is unexpected.

Jada Williams: agree. Promote should be explicit about what you think current stage/version is.

Benji Okafor: ok: require BOTH `Idempotency-Key` AND expected version.

Priya Natarajan: decision: required for all action endpoints.

Priya Natarajan: Benji can you capture in the PR description + update the API docs in the service doc? (Also ping Connor so QA knows what to test.)

Benji Okafor: yep. I’ll add test cases:
- retry same key returns same `action_id` + stage
- concurrent promote with different keys -> one 200, one 409
- replay with different payload -> 400

Chloe Martin: I’ll update Console backend to always send both headers. For expected version we’ll use ETag from GET plan.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please add one more test: replay with different auth principal should NOT return cached response (should 409/404). We can decide exact code, but shouldn’t leak.

Jordan Lee: suggest 409 `idempotency_key_conflict` if key exists for different principal within same org/plan. That’s safe.

Benji Okafor: ok.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: thanks all

Priya Natarajan: :thumbsup: ship it, but behind the feature flag until Console is updated.

Benji Okafor: (deploy bot voice) will do.

Chloe Martin: :eyes: I’ll coordinate rollout order with release train so we don’t break existing clients.

source.messages:
Logan Wright: quick review request on PR feedback for checklist progress API perms + audit logging. PR-28431 is getting a lot of :eyes: from security + support. Can we align on the model today so Chloe/Jada can push updates?

github-bot: PR #28431 “add-checklist-progress-api” updated
- requested review: @loganw @chloe.m @marcus.lin
- latest commit: `f6a1c4d` “wire authz + add audit_log skeleton”

Chloe Martin: yep. main open questions from comments:
1) should Console call `GET /v1/onboarding/checklist/state` as end-user or as console-service?
2) do we allow *any* write endpoint besides the internal event ingest?
3) audit logging: what’s minimum required fields + where does it land

Jada Williams: +1 and there’s also a comment about us accepting `tenant_id` in the request body for the “mark step complete” endpoint (draft). reviewer said that’s a footgun.

Dev Patel: from Support side: we need the override but it has to be safe + auditable. We’ve had a bunch of internal requests like “customer says they did step X but state is stuck”, and we end up escalating to eng.

Marcus Lin: adding security lens: principle is “derived tenant from auth, never caller-provided”, and “human overrides require explicit reason + immutable audit trail”. Also: keep payload PII-free.

Logan Wright: ok, proposing:
- read state: Console backend calls as console-service (service-to-service) *but* scoped to the end-user’s tenant based on their session. (So the browser never talks to progress-service directly.)
- write events: only `POST /internal/v1/onboarding/events` from gateway/control plane w/ mTLS + service auth.
- support override: separate endpoint `POST /v1/onboarding/checklist/admin/override` behind `support_ops` role. no customer tokens.

Nadia Rahman: re: read state, can we do it as end-user JWT? would be nice but we’d then need to expose progress-service publicly + deal with CORS, WAF rules, etc. I’d rather keep it behind console backend.

Dylan Brooks: +1 keep it behind console backend. also lets us evolve without breaking the browser clients.

Chloe Martin: ack. so for the console-service call: we still need to ensure console can’t fetch arbitrary tenants. easiest is require `X-RW-Tenant-Id` header from console, but then validate it against the authenticated console session claims.

Jada Williams: right now in PR it’s `tenant_id` query param. I can change to “no tenant_id param; tenant resolved by auth middleware” for all non-admin endpoints.

Marcus Lin: strongly prefer “no tenant_id param” where possible.
- if you *must* pass tenant, make it an internal header and verify it server-side.
- public APIs: tenant must come from token claims.

Logan Wright: for non-admin read endpoint, we can have:
- Console sends `Authorization: console-service` (service token)
- plus `X-RW-Actor-User-Id` + `X-RW-Actor-Tenant-Id` coming from console after validating session
- progress-service enforces: actor tenant id is required and becomes the tenant scope. no ability to query others.

Chloe Martin: that works. We already have that pattern for a couple internal services.

Dev Patel: admin override: can it be limited to internal staff only + require a reason string? we’ll use that in support notes.

Jada Williams: current draft has `reason` optional. I can make it required + min length.

Marcus Lin: yes, make it required. also include “ticket reference” field (Jira/Slack link). optional is ok but helpful.

Nadia Rahman: audit logging: where do we want it? we have the standard audit log pipeline for console/admin actions. should we send to that or write to a local DB table?

Logan Wright: minimum viable:
- append-only table `onboarding_checklist_audit_log`
- also emit to our audit log stream (same sink as other admin actions) for retention/search
If only one for MVP, I’d pick stream (so Support can find it) + store request_id in DB.

Chloe Martin: constraints: emitting to stream is easy, but we still want local for debugging + unit tests. I’ll do both but keep DB minimal.

Jada Williams: what fields are required? PR comment asked for “who/what changed state” + “before/after”.

Marcus Lin: required for human override:
- ts
- actor_type (support_user | system)
- actor_id (internal user id) and actor_email optional (if that’s allowed; email is borderline PII but internal)
- tenant_id
- action (override_mark_complete, override_reset, override_note)
- step_id
- from_status -> to_status
- reason
- source_ip + user_agent (optional but useful)
- request_id / trace_id
For system event ingestion we can log less, but should still have trace_id + dedupe key.

Nadia Rahman: for system writes, we should log:
- event_name
- producer_service
- idempotency_key
- tenant_id
- step_id
- resulting transition
(no payload)

Logan Wright: agree. and importantly: do not log prompt text, api keys, model inputs, etc.

Dylan Brooks: one more: permissions for “admin override” endpoint. We need to define `support_ops` vs `console_admin`.

Dev Patel: support leadership asked for “Support + CS” eventually. for MVP I’m ok with Support only. If we can gate on an allowlist group, even better.

Chloe Martin: I can wire it to our internal RBAC groups: `support_oncall` and `support_leads`. We’ll start with `support_leads` only to reduce blast radius.

Logan Wright: sounds good. also add a rate limit on admin override (per actor) so it can’t be spammed.

Jada Williams: PR feedback also asked for a “dry run” mode. do we need that?

Dev Patel: not necessary. we just need a “note” field and audit log.

Marcus Lin: +1 no dry run.

Nadia Rahman: last detail: should admin override allow setting arbitrary state or only monotonic transitions?

Chloe Martin: my instinct:
- allow `mark_complete` (incomplete->complete)
- allow `reset` (complete->incomplete) but only with reason + ticket ref
- do not allow setting “in_progress”/intermediate states. keep state machine small.

Logan Wright: perfect. that matches the race-condition fix we did elsewhere (monotonic for system, explicit reset for humans).

Jada Williams: ok action items for PR-28431 update:
- remove tenant_id query/body from non-admin endpoints; derive scope from auth middleware
- add admin override endpoint behind RBAC group `support_leads`
- make `reason` required; add optional `ticket_ref`
- implement append-only audit log row + emit audit event (no payload)
- add unit tests for authz + audit emission

Marcus Lin: please tag me on the updated diff; I’ll do a quick privacy check once you land it.

Dev Patel: also loop me in when override is ready; we’ll draft the Support macro with “what reason to enter + where to paste ticket link”.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: thanks all. Jada/Chloe ping when updated; I’ll resolve the PR comments + we can unblock merge.

github-bot: Chloe Martin pushed 3 commits to PR #28431
- `1c2b9d0` “derive tenant scope from auth; remove tenant_id params”
- `7a0e4aa` “add admin override (support_leads) + required reason”
- `d13fe21` “emit audit events + append-only audit table”

Chloe Martin: updates are in. remaining TODO is rate limit on override (I can do next PR if it’s too much churn now).

Logan Wright: next PR is fine. I’ll approve w/ note. Marcus can you take another look?

Marcus Lin: looking now.

Marcus Lin: reviewed. audit fields look good. small ask: ensure `ticket_ref` is length-limited + validated to avoid someone pasting a full customer payload by mistake.

Jada Williams: good call. I’ll cap at 256 chars and strip newlines.

Dev Patel: works for us.

Logan Wright: once that’s in, we’re good to merge.

github-bot: PR #28431 merged into `main`

- append-only table `onboarding_checklist_audit_log`
- also emit to our audit log stream (same sink as other admin actions) for retention/search
If only one for MVP, I’d pick stream (so Support can find it) + store request_id in DB.

Chloe Martin: constraints: emitting to stream is easy, but we still want local for debugging + unit tests. I’ll do both but keep DB minimal.

Jada Williams: what fields are required? PR comment asked for “who/what changed state” + “before/after”.

Marcus Lin: required for human override:
- ts
- actor_type (support_user | system)
- actor_id (internal user id) and actor_email optional (if that’s allowed; email is borderline PII but internal)
- tenant_id
- action (override_mark_complete, override_reset, override_note)
- step_id
- from_status -> to_status
- reason
- source_ip + user_agent (optional but useful)
- request_id / trace_id
For system event ingestion we can log less, but should still have trace_id + dedupe key.

Nadia Rahman: for system writes, we should log:
- event_name
- producer_service
- idempotency_key
- tenant_id
- step_id
- resulting transition
(no payload)

Logan Wright: agree. and importantly: do not log prompt text, api keys, model inputs, etc.

Dylan Brooks: one more: permissions for “admin override” endpoint. We need to define `support_ops` vs `console_admin`.

Dev Patel: support leadership asked for “Support + CS” eventually. for MVP I’m ok with Support only. If we can gate on an allowlist group, even better.

Chloe Martin: I can wire it to our internal RBAC groups: `support_oncall` and `support_leads`. We’ll start with `support_leads` only to reduce blast radius.

Logan Wright: sounds good. also add a rate limit on admin override (per actor) so it can’t be spammed.

Jada Williams: PR feedback also asked for a “dry run” mode. do we need that?

Dev Patel: not necessary. we just need a “note” field and audit log.

Marcus Lin: +1 no dry run.

Nadia Rahman: last detail: should admin override allow setting arbitrary state or only monotonic transitions?

Chloe Martin: my instinct:
- allow `mark_complete` (incomplete->complete)
- allow `reset` (complete->incomplete) but only with reason + ticket ref
- do not allow setting “in_progress”/intermediate states. keep state machine small.

Logan Wright: perfect. that matches the race-condition fix we did elsewhere (monotonic for system, explicit reset for humans).

Jada Williams: ok action items for PR-28431 update:
- remove tenant_id query/body from non-admin endpoints; derive scope from auth middleware
- add admin override endpoint behind RBAC group `support_leads`
- make `reason` required; add optional `ticket_ref`
- implement append-only audit log row + emit audit event (no payload)
- add unit tests for authz + audit emission

Marcus Lin: please tag me on the updated diff; I’ll do a quick privacy check once you land it.

Dev Patel: also loop me in when override is ready; we’ll draft the Support macro with “what reason to enter + where to paste ticket link”.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: thanks all. Jada/Chloe ping when updated; I’ll resolve the PR comments + we can unblock merge.

github-bot: Chloe Martin pushed 3 commits to PR #28431
- `1c2b9d0` “derive tenant scope from auth; remove tenant_id params”
- `7a0e4aa` “add admin override (support_leads) + required reason”
- `d13fe21` “emit audit events + append-only audit table”

Chloe Martin: updates are in. remaining TODO is rate limit on override (I can do next PR if it’s too much churn now).

Logan Wright: next PR is fine. I’ll approve w/ note. Marcus can you take another look?

Marcus Lin: looking now.

Marcus Lin: reviewed. audit fields look good. small ask: ensure `ticket_ref` is length-limited + validated to avoid someone pasting a full customer payload by mistake.

Jada Williams: good call. I’ll cap at 256 chars and strip newlines.

Dev Patel: works for us.

Logan Wright: once that’s in, we’re good to merge.

github-bot: PR #28431 merged into `main`

source.body:
Ethan Park: Locking the final list of “sensitive admin workflows” + mapping to `event_type` + required fields. This is the list we’re gating Phase 2 coverage on (Hosted/Dedicated/Private parity). Please reply w/ :thumbsup: if your area looks correct, or call out gaps by EOD.

Ethan Park: Canonical required fields (per schema v1) for *all* event_types below:
- `schema_version`, `event_id`, `occurred_at`
- `event_type`, `event_category` (admin|security|policy|deployment)
- `tenant_id` (+ `project_id` when scoped)
- `actor_type` (user|service|system), `actor_id`, `actor_display_name` (if user)
- `impersonated_by_actor_id` (nullable)
- `source_ip` (nullable for system), `user_agent` (nullable)
- `request_id` and/or `trace_id` (one required; both preferred)
- `outcome` (success|failure) + `error_code` (if failure)
- `resource_type`, `resource_id` (or `resource_name` if ID not available), `resource_scope`
- `change_summary` (required for updates; redacted + cardinality-safe)

Ethan Park: Workflow -> event_type -> extra required fields -> owner
```
1) Create role
   event_type: rbac.role.create
   extra required: role_id, role_name, permissions_summary
   owner: Benji (RBAC) / Ethan (control plane)

2) Update role (permissions/name)
   event_type: rbac.role.update
   extra required: role_id, before_permissions_summary, after_permissions_summary
   owner: Benji

3) Delete role
   event_type: rbac.role.delete
   extra required: role_id, role_name
   owner: Benji

4) Add user/service account to role (binding create)
   event_type: rbac.binding.create
   extra required: principal_type, principal_id, role_id, scope (tenant|project)
   owner: Benji

5) Remove user/service account from role (binding delete)
   event_type: rbac.binding.delete
   extra required: principal_type, principal_id, role_id, scope
   owner: Benji

6) Create API key
   event_type: credential.api_key.create
   extra required: key_id, key_name, key_type (user|service), expires_at (nullable)
   notes: NEVER log key material; allow only last4 if needed (prefer none)
   owner: Kaitlyn (auth/cred lifecycle)

7) Rotate API key
   event_type: credential.api_key.rotate
   extra required: old_key_id, new_key_id, key_name
   owner: Kaitlyn

8) Revoke API key
   event_type: credential.api_key.revoke
   extra required: key_id, key_name, revoke_reason (enum)
   owner: Kaitlyn

9) Create / update routing policy (fallbacks, allow-lists, model pinning)
   event_type: policy.routing.update
   extra required: policy_id, policy_name, diff_summary, rollout_mode (immediate|canary|scheduled)
   owner: Jordan (policy engine) / Diego (cross-deploy parity)

10) Quota override (tenant/project)
   event_type: admin.quota.override
   extra required: quota_metric (enum), before_value, after_value, expires_at (nullable)
   owner: Amara (API gateway) / Ethan (control plane)

11) Create audit log export destination
   event_type: audit.export_destination.create
   extra required: destination_id, destination_type (splunk_hec|sentinel|datadog|s3|webhook), destination_region, auth_type (token|oauth|keyvault_ref)
   notes: destination_url MUST be normalized (host only) OR hashed; no secrets
   owner: Kevin (exporter)

12) Update audit log export destination (incl enable/disable)
   event_type: audit.export_destination.update
   extra required: destination_id, diff_summary, enabled (bool)
   owner: Kevin / Chloe (console backend)

13) Delete audit log export destination
   event_type: audit.export_destination.delete
   extra required: destination_id, destination_type
   owner: Kevin / Chloe

14) Test audit log export destination ("send test event")
   event_type: audit.export_destination.test
   extra required: destination_id, destination_type, test_result (success|failure)
   owner: Kevin / Chloe

15) Update audit log retention window
   event_type: data.retention.update
   extra required: retention_days_before, retention_days_after, scope (tenant|project)
   owner: Chloe (console) / Ethan (control plane)

16) Update data residency region / routing constraint
   event_type: data.residency.update
   extra required: region_before, region_after, scope
   owner: Ethan

17) Private: install
   event_type: private.deployment.install
   extra required: deployment_id, install_id, operator (if present), package_version
   owner: Diego (parity) + Private team (tracked separately)

18) Private: upgrade
   event_type: private.deployment.upgrade
   extra required: deployment_id, upgrade_id, from_version, to_version, operator (if present)
   owner: Diego + Private team

19) Private: rollback
   event_type: private.deployment.rollback
   extra required: deployment_id, rollback_id, from_version, to_version, operator (if present)
   owner: Diego + Private team

20) Private: apply config (includes export config + retention/residency settings)
   event_type: private.config.apply
   extra required: deployment_id, config_change_id, diff_summary
   owner: Diego + Private team
```

Naomi Feldman: This matches what we’ve been telling customers (esp export + retention/residency). One nuance: for #9 policy.routing.update, can we guarantee `diff_summary` is bounded + not free-form? We should call out it must be “field allowlist diff” not a raw JSON diff.

Jordan Lee: +1. We’re generating diff from a structured allowlist (e.g. `fallback_enabled`, `allowed_models_added_count`, etc.). Will not dump full policy. I’ll add “diff_summary MUST be generated from allowlisted keys only” to the policy engine implementation notes.

Allison Grant: For the “extra required fields” list, let’s be careful about adding new top-level fields vs packing into `change_summary`. Our standard is: stable schema fields stay stable; any service-specific fields should be prefixed (e.g. `rbac_role_id` instead of `role_id`) OR go into `extensions.<namespace>.*`.

Ethan Park: Yep. The block above is conceptual — actual emission should follow the flattened standard:
- use `resource_type/resource_id` for the primary object
- put additional IDs into `extensions.*` with namespacing
We’ll keep `change_summary` required and keep extra IDs in `extensions.<service>_*`.

Benji Okafor: RBAC list looks good. I’ll align naming to:
- `resource_type=rbac_role`, `resource_id=<role_id>`
- `extensions.rbac.principal_id`, `extensions.rbac.principal_type`, `extensions.rbac.role_permissions_delta`
Also confirming we’ll populate `impersonated_by_actor_id` for support/admin impersonation.
(reactions: :thumbsup: x3)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Cred lifecycle (#6-8) ok. We’ll set:
- `resource_type=api_key`, `resource_id=<key_id>`
- `extensions.credential.key_type`, `extensions.credential.expires_at`
No last4. We can include `key_name` if it’s user-provided but cap length + character set.

Amara Diallo: Quota override is correct. One question: do we also need an event when a quota override *expires* (system action)?

Naomi Feldman: Good catch. Yes, that’s a “sensitive effect” even if it’s system-initiated. Can we add:
- `admin.quota.override.expire` (actor_type=system)
Same required fields + before/after.

Ethan Park: Agree. Adding #21:
```
21) Quota override expiration
    event_type: admin.quota.override.expire
    actor_type: system
    extra required: quota_metric, before_value, after_value, override_id
    owner: Amara / Ethan
```

Kevin Osei: Export destination workflows look right. For URL handling we’re doing `extensions.audit_export.destination_host` (hostname only) + optional `destination_host_hash`. No full URL, no headers, no tokens.
Also: for `audit.export_destination.test` we should emit the test event itself *as audited* (i.e. audit log about the test), but the payload sent to SIEM should be a separate “synthetic” event that is clearly labeled and does NOT include real tenant activity.

Chloe Martin: Console backend: I’m good w/ #12-14 + #15. One dependency: actor attribution when Console calls control plane on behalf of a user. We’ll ensure we pass end-user identity and not “console-service” as actor.

Diego Santos: Private items (#17-20) are in parity checklist. We can’t always get operator identity (depends on installation method), but we’ll populate `actor_type=system` and put operator into `extensions.private.operator` when it exists. Also we should add `extensions.private.command`? (like `upgrade`/`rollback`)

Allison Grant: Please no raw command strings. If you need it, make it enum: `operation` = install|upgrade|rollback|config_apply. Cardinality budget will thank you.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard, this list is perfect as the “expected event_types” source of truth. Please keep it stable + versioned. If we add #21 we need to update the expected list in telemetry.

Sahana Iyer: CI linting: once we finalize the enum list (incl #21), I’ll add it to the registry and make unknown `event_type` fail builds. Also adding a check that updates (PATCH/PUT) must have `change_summary` non-empty.

Ethan Park: Cool. I’ll post the final list in the taxonomy JSON and tag owners in Linear. Any objections to the naming `admin.quota.override.expire`? (vs `admin.quota_override.expired`)

Naomi Feldman: Prefer consistent verb at end (create/update/delete/expire). `admin.quota.override.expire` is fine.

Amara Diallo: Works for me.

Ethan Park: Finalizing then. Thanks all — this is the launch gating list. If you discover a missing workflow later, we’ll add via the exception process (time-boxed waiver) but default is “must emit”.
(reactions: :white_check_mark: x6)
