```
   owner: Kevin / Chloe

15) Update audit log retention window
   event_type: data.retention.update
   extra required: retention_days_before, retention_days_after, scope (tenant|project)
   owner: Chloe (console) / Ethan (control plane)

16) Update data residency region / routing constraint
   event_type: data.residency.update
   extra required: region_before, region_after, scope
   owner: Ethan

17) Private: install
   event_type: private.deployment.install
   extra required: deployment_id, install_id, operator (if present), package_version
   owner: Diego (parity) + Private team (tracked separately)

18) Private: upgrade
   event_type: private.deployment.upgrade
   extra required: deployment_id, upgrade_id, from_version, to_version, operator (if present)
   owner: Diego + Private team

19) Private: rollback
   event_type: private.deployment.rollback
   extra required: deployment_id, rollback_id, from_version, to_version, operator (if present)
   owner: Diego + Private team

20) Private: apply config (includes export config + retention/residency settings)
   event_type: private.config.apply
   extra required: deployment_id, config_change_id, diff_summary
   owner: Diego + Private team
```

Naomi Feldman: This matches what we’ve been telling customers (esp export + retention/residency). One nuance: for #9 policy.routing.update, can we guarantee `diff_summary` is bounded + not free-form? We should call out it must be “field allowlist diff” not a raw JSON diff.

Jordan Lee: +1. We’re generating diff from a structured allowlist (e.g. `fallback_enabled`, `allowed_models_added_count`, etc.). Will not dump full policy. I’ll add “diff_summary MUST be generated from allowlisted keys only” to the policy engine implementation notes.

Allison Grant: For the “extra required fields” list, let’s be careful about adding new top-level fields vs packing into `change_summary`. Our standard is: stable schema fields stay stable; any service-specific fields should be prefixed (e.g. `rbac_role_id` instead of `role_id`) OR go into `extensions.<namespace>.*`.

Ethan Park: Yep. The block above is conceptual — actual emission should follow the flattened standard:
- use `resource_type/resource_id` for the primary object
- put additional IDs into `extensions.*` with namespacing
We’ll keep `change_summary` required and keep extra IDs in `extensions.<service>_*`.

Benji Okafor: RBAC list looks good. I’ll align naming to:
- `resource_type=rbac_role`, `resource_id=<role_id>`
- `extensions.rbac.principal_id`, `extensions.rbac.principal_type`, `extensions.rbac.role_permissions_delta`
Also confirming we’ll populate `impersonated_by_actor_id` for support/admin impersonation.
(reactions: :thumbsup: x3)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Cred lifecycle (#6-8) ok. We’ll set:
- `resource_type=api_key`, `resource_id=<key_id>`
- `extensions.credential.key_type`, `extensions.credential.expires_at`
No last4. We can include `key_name` if it’s user-provided but cap length + character set.

Amara Diallo: Quota override is correct. One question: do we also need an event when a quota override *expires* (system action)?

Naomi Feldman: Good catch. Yes, that’s a “sensitive effect” even if it’s system-initiated. Can we add:
- `admin.quota.override.expire` (actor_type=system)
Same required fields + before/after.

Ethan Park: Agree. Adding #21:
```
21) Quota override expiration
    event_type: admin.quota.override.expire
    actor_type: system
    extra required: quota_metric, before_value, after_value, override_id
    owner: Amara / Ethan
```

Kevin Osei: Export destination workflows look right. For URL handling we’re doing `extensions.audit_export.destination_host` (hostname only) + optional `destination_host_hash`. No full URL, no headers, no tokens.
Also: for `audit.export_destination.test` we should emit the test event itself *as audited* (i.e. audit log about the test), but the payload sent to SIEM should be a separate “synthetic” event that is clearly labeled and does NOT include real tenant activity.

Chloe Martin: Console backend: I’m good w/ #12-14 + #15. One dependency: actor attribution when Console calls control plane on behalf of a user. We’ll ensure we pass end-user identity and not “console-service” as actor.

Diego Santos: Private items (#17-20) are in parity checklist. We can’t always get operator identity (depends on installation method), but we’ll populate `actor_type=system` and put operator into `extensions.private.operator` when it exists. Also we should add `extensions.private.command`? (like `upgrade`/`rollback`)

Allison Grant: Please no raw command strings. If you need it, make it enum: `operation` = install|upgrade|rollback|config_apply. Cardinality budget will thank you.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard, this list is perfect as the “expected event_types” source of truth. Please keep it stable + versioned. If we add #21 we need to update the expected list in telemetry.

Sahana Iyer: CI linting: once we finalize the enum list (incl #21), I’ll add it to the registry and make unknown `event_type` fail builds. Also adding a check that updates (PATCH/PUT) must have `change_summary` non-empty.

Ethan Park: Cool. I’ll post the final list in the taxonomy JSON and tag owners in Linear. Any objections to the naming `admin.quota.override.expire`? (vs `admin.quota_override.expired`)

Naomi Feldman: Prefer consistent verb at end (create/update/delete/expire). `admin.quota.override.expire` is fine.

Amara Diallo: Works for me.

Ethan Park: Finalizing then. Thanks all — this is the launch gating list. If you discover a missing workflow later, we’ll add via the exception process (time-boxed waiver) but default is “must emit”.
(reactions: :white_check_mark: x6)

source.content:
Ethan Park: Need quick gut check / decision in-thread on config diff endpoint perms for incident-bot v2. We’re about to cut the API contract and I don’t want to ship something security will block later.

Ethan Park: Questions:
1) Who/what can call `GET /config/diff` (bot, humans, both?)
2) Do we support raw diffs or only redacted diffs?
3) Audit events: what fields do we need to log?

Benji Okafor: Current strawman from our notes:
- `GET /v1/config/diff?tenant_id=&service=&region=&from=&to=`
- Returns “changes” summary + optional expanded diff
- Redaction hook in config-service before response

Benji Okafor: But the sticking point is who can see what. If incident-bot needs this in #incidents, we can’t require oncaller to have elevated perms just to *render* the card.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. We should treat bot output as “public-to-oncall channel” content, so assume it can be seen by anyone in #incidents / #eng-oncall.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: So: bot should only ever get a *tenant-safe* diff. No secrets, no tokens, no customer identifiers, no raw values for certain fields.

Mina Haddad: For routing incidents we mostly care about: model pins, fallback policy, routing weights, feature flags (enable/disable), quota/rate-limit knobs. But those can include tenant-specific overrides.

Mina Haddad: I don’t need exact customer values in Slack. I need to know “routing_policy changed (hash X->Y)” and “fallback enabled/disabled” and “model_pin changed (redacted)”.

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side, the common pain is “what changed?” We do a bunch of manual console clicks + grep in config repo. If Slack can say *what category changed* and *where to look* w/ links, that’s already a win.

Marcus Lin: Security POV:
- Slack is not a privileged UI
- We must assume logs/screenshots/exports
- Diff endpoint must be least-privilege and auditable

Marcus Lin: I’d be comfortable with: incident-bot has a service identity that can only fetch REDACTED diffs, with tight query bounds (time window + limited fields).

Marcus Lin: Any “raw diff” should require an authenticated human w/ appropriate role AND should not be callable by incident-bot.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also we should encode this in policy engine scopes vs “special casing bot”.

Jordan Lee: Eg scopes:
- `config.diff.read_redacted`
- `config.diff.read_full`

Jordan Lee: And then roles map to that:
- incident-bot svc acct => redacted only
- oncall humans => maybe redacted by default, full via break-glass

Benji Okafor: Makes sense. Implementation-wise, config-service can expose one endpoint but gate by scope and apply redaction depending on scope.

Benji Okafor: Something like:
`if scope == full -> response_mode=full`
`else -> response_mode=redacted`

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Careful: even “full” should still remove secrets (passwords, API keys). Full != unredacted.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’d propose 3 tiers:
1) secret-stripped (always)
2) tenant-safe (Slack-safe)
3) internal full (still secret-stripped)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Tenant-safe should also avoid showing tenant name if it’s customer-identifying. Use tenant_id or internal slug only.

Kevin Osei: On audit logs: we should emit an audit event for every call regardless of response_mode. Minimum fields:
- actor_type (service|user)
- actor_id (service principal or user)
- scopes used
- tenant_id (or “none” if global)
- service/region
- from/to revision
- response_mode (tenant_safe/internal_full)
- redaction_summary (counts only, not the values)
- request_id / trace_id

Kevin Osei: Retention requirement from compliance is 1y for privileged config reads. We can route to SIEM.

Ethan Park: What about the “who can request diff for tenant X” part? Is tenant isolation enforced at config-service or at gateway?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Enforce at config-service. Gateway authz alone isn’t enough; internal callers can bypass.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For humans: authorize based on tenant access + role.
For bot: authorize based on “incident responder” scope + tenant-safe mode only.

Ravi Menon: Re break-glass: we should be explicit.
- Only allowed for Sev0/Sev1 incident context
- Time-bound grant (e.g., 1 hour)
- Requires ticket/incident link
- Forces audit event with “break_glass=true” and reason

Ravi Menon: Otherwise we’ll end up with “full diff for everyone” permanently because it’s convenient.

Rafael Mendes: +1 on break-glass. We can wire it to incident channel thread link. (Even if manual at first.)

Mina Haddad: Also consider partial propagation: config diff between two revisions might show changes that aren’t rolled out everywhere. Can the API return rollout status or at least the revision IDs + timestamps so we can correlate?

Benji Okafor: We can include metadata:
- from_rev / to_rev
- from_ts / to_ts
- writer (service/user)
- rollout_phase if available (if config change went through rollout controller)

Benji Okafor: But that’s separate from authz; just noting.

Marcus Lin: Back to redaction: allowlist is better than blocklist. Blocklists miss new keys.

Marcus Lin: We can do:
- structured configs: allowlist fields per domain (routing, quotas, flags)
- everything else => show “changed (redacted)” with path only
- always strip any value matching secret patterns

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Agree. Provide “path + change type”:
- added/removed/modified
- if modified, show old/new only if field is allowlisted and type is safe

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For safe fields like booleans/enums we can show values.
For numeric quotas maybe show bucketed values (e.g., 100-200) or exact? Unsure.

Rafael Mendes: Exact quotas in Slack feels ok if they’re not tenant identifying, but might leak customer contract terms. I’d rather show “quota changed” + deep link to Console for authorized folks.

Kevin Osei: Audit: deep links are fine; still log the diff read. Also note: Slack message itself isn’t an audit log. We need the server-side event.

Jordan Lee: Policy engine detail: The diff endpoint should require BOTH:
- `config.diff.read_*` scope
- `tenant.access.read` (for that tenant)

Jordan Lee: Bot will have `tenant.access.read` only for “all tenants”?? That’s scary.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yeah we should NOT give bot blanket tenant read if we can avoid it.

Benji Okafor: How will bot know tenant? Today incidents are usually per service/region, not always per tenant. If we do tenant-scoped diffs only when incident has a tenant explicitly tagged, we can avoid broad access.

Rafael Mendes: In #incidents we usually have:
- service
- region
- sometimes route/model
- tenant only for Dedicated or a specific customer ticket

Rafael Mendes: So bot can default to global/service/region config diff (no tenant). Then if someone invokes “Include tenant overrides” action, require extra auth.

Ravi Menon: +1 to interactive action for tenant-specific diffs. That gives us an escalation path + can require a permission check / break-glass.

Mina Haddad: That works for routing too. Most routing policy is global per region/pool; tenant overrides are less common.

Ethan Park: Proposed decision (please react/confirm):
A) Bot only fetches tenant-safe diffs by default and only for global/service/region scope.
B) Tenant-specific diffs require explicit action + permission (and possibly break-glass) and do NOT render in-channel unless tenant-safe.
C) “Full/internal” diffs only in Console or a secure UI, never Slack.
D) Every diff read emits audit event w/ actor/scopes/tenant/service/region + redaction counts.

Ethan Park: If no strong objections, I’ll write this into ADR + implementation ticket.

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: on A-D.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: :thumbsup: + add: tenant-safe mode must not include tenant name, only tenant_id.

Kevin Osei: :thumbsup: and please ensure audit includes request origin (incident-bot vs console) so we can alert on unusual access patterns.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: I’ll help with scope names in policy engine so we don’t end up with ad-hoc checks.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: I’ll update endpoint design to include `response_mode` and enforce caps (max diff size, max time window).

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: and please include routing fields in allowlist review (I’ll send list).

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: From SRE this meets needs. If card is empty due to perms we should have a clear “why” + link + command to request access.

Ethan Park: Cool. Next steps:
- Benji: update spec w/ response modes + query bounds
- Kaitlyn/Marcus: review redaction allowlist approach
- Kevin: draft audit event schema + retention notes
- Jordan: scope naming + role mapping

Ethan Park: Will paste ADR draft in #architecture later today.

Jada Williams: Need final eyes on Console backend contract for gate evidence before we lock the endpoint + FE starts wiring. This is for the stage timeline “Gate evidence” drawer.

Jada Williams: Context: Console calls `GET /console/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollouts/{rollout_id}/stages/{stage_id}/gates/{gate_id}/evidence` (naming tbd) and we return *what signals we evaluated*, over *what window*, *what we observed*, *what the threshold was*, and *why pass/fail/unknown*.

Chloe Martin: +1, we need to stabilize today. FE needs consistent fields for table + “View query” + “Open dashboard” links.

Jordan Lee: Biggest requirement from policy engine side: determinism + auditability. Evidence response should be reconstructable: what eval run produced it (`evaluation_id`), what window, and the exact comparisons.

Logan Wright: Also: don’t ship raw PromQL to customer-facing UI by default. We can include a query “ref” and a redacted/pretty string. Full query should be admin-only.

Allison Grant: Standardization ask: align with Observability pack conventions. Use `observed_value`, `threshold_value`, `unit`, `comparator`, `aggregation`, `window_duration_s`, `sample_size` (if available). Avoid “p95_ms” as a field name.

Kenji Nakamura: From UX, evidence drawer needs:
- Status chip: PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN
- For each signal: name, observed, threshold, window, plus “why unknown” if telemetry stale
- Optional “Link to dashboard panel”

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC: customer admins should see evidence results, but not internal query text if it exposes cluster identifiers. Also if we include actor notes/reasons those are sensitive; keep those in audit trail endpoint, not here.

Hannah Rivera: One more UX nuance: if we’re in HOLD state, the drawer still shows last evaluation, but should say “Evaluation paused” and give `evaluation_stale=true`.

Jada Williams: Ok here’s proposed response shape v3. Please nitpick.

Jada Williams: ```json
{
  "pool_id": "dp_7Yk3…",
  "rollout_id": "dpr_01J…",
  "stage_id": "stage_10pct",
  "gate_id": "gate_latency_p95",
  "gate_name": "Latency p95",
  "status": "PASS|FAIL|UNKNOWN",
  "status_reason": "string", 
  "evaluated_at": "2025-02-10T19:07:32Z",
  "evaluation_id": "g_eval_01J…",
  "window_start": "2025-02-10T18:52:32Z",
  "window_end": "2025-02-10T19:07:32Z",
  "window_duration_s": 900,
  "missing_data": false,
  "staleness_s": 22,
  "signals": [
    {
      "signal_key": "latency_p95",
      "signal_name": "Latency p95",
      "unit": "ms",
      "aggregation": "p95",
      "query_ref": "dedicated.rollout.latency.p95",
      "query_display": "p95(request_latency_ms) for stage over 15m",
      "query_admin": null,
      "observed_value": 182.4,
      "threshold_comparator": "LTE",
      "threshold_value": 220,
      "pass": true,
      "sample_size": 24891,
      "notes": ""
    }
  ],
  "links": {
    "dashboard_url": "https://console.redwoodinference.com/…",
    "trace_search_url": "https://console.redwoodinference.com/…"
  }
}
```

Chloe Martin: This is good but `links` is nested (fine for API) — for the contract discussion, ok. For our internal doc we can keep it; backend already returns nested JSON.

Jordan Lee: I want `status_reason` to be machine-ish, not freeform. We’re going to have FE logic + tests. Suggest:
- `status_reason_code`: `THRESHOLD_PASSED|THRESHOLD_FAILED|INSUFFICIENT_SAMPLES|TELEMETRY_STALE|QUERY_ERROR|EVAL_DISABLED`
- `status_reason_message`: optional human string.

Logan Wright: +1. Also add `query_error` if query failed (timeout / parse / backend down). We need to distinguish “0 samples” from “query engine 500”.

Allison Grant: If we add both code + message, please keep message short. Also `staleness_s` is great.

Ravi Menon: Routing side: can we include the stage selector used? We’ve had confusion when stage tags mismatched.

Logan Wright: Selector is derived from `stage_id` + pool labels. But yes, we can show a sanitized `scope` string, like `pool=dp_… stage=stage_10pct region=us-west-2`.

Kenji Nakamura: Scope display would be helpful for “why is this empty” debugging.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Careful: pool_id is already customer-visible. Region is fine. Don’t include internal cluster names. “scope_display” should be safe.

Hannah Rivera: Another edge: if stage has no traffic yet (0 req), gate should likely be UNKNOWN with `INSUFFICIENT_SAMPLES`. FE needs sample size + min sample size threshold to explain.

Jordan Lee: We can include `min_sample_size` in response. That is part of gate definition.

Jada Williams: So per-signal:
- `sample_size`
- `min_sample_size`
- if missing, treat as N/A

Allison Grant: Naming: `min_sample_size` ok. Also add `observed_value` null when unknown (not 0). Same for threshold sometimes (for “manual gate”).

Chloe Martin: Manual gates MVP: we do have a gate type that’s “operator verified” with no metrics. We need to support `signals=[]` and a top-level `manual_gate` boolean?

Jordan Lee: Prefer `gate_type`: `METRIC|MANUAL|QUALITY` (future). If MANUAL, evidence returns:
- who last set it (but that’s audit-ish)
- status + reason code/message.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: “who last set it” should come from audit endpoint, not evidence. For evidence, just the evaluation timestamp is fine.

Logan Wright: For METRIC gates, I’d like `query_admin` to be omitted unless caller is admin. Don’t send null; just exclude field.

Chloe Martin: Our current Console backend is already doing “shape trimming” based on `X-Redwood-Actor-Tier`. We can do conditional fields.

Jada Williams: Ok revised contract proposal:

Jada Williams: - Top-level:
  - `gate_type`
  - `status`
  - `status_reason_code`
  - `status_reason_message` (optional)
  - `evaluated_at`, `evaluation_id`
  - `window_start`, `window_end`, `window_duration_s`
  - `missing_data` (true if any required series missing)
  - `staleness_s`
  - `scope_display`
- `signals[]`:
  - `signal_key`, `signal_name`
  - `unit`, `aggregation`
  - `query_ref`
  - `query_display`
  - `observed_value` (nullable)
  - `threshold_comparator`, `threshold_value` (nullable)
  - `pass` (nullable when UNKNOWN)
  - `sample_size`, `min_sample_size` (nullable)
  - `query_status`: `OK|ERROR|STALE|NO_DATA`
  - `query_status_message` (optional)
- links: we’ll keep `dashboard_url` and `trace_search_url` as top-level strings to avoid extra nesting.

Kenji Nakamura: Love top-level links as plain fields. FE can show as buttons.

Allison Grant: +1 on `query_status`. Please make sure `NO_DATA` vs `STALE` is consistent. Stale = we have data but beyond staleness threshold? Or no new points.

Logan Wright: Our telemetry evaluator defines:
- `NO_DATA`: query returns empty vector for entire window
- `STALE`: last datapoint timestamp < `window_end - staleness_threshold`
- `ERROR`: query engine error/timeout
- `OK`: got data and fresh enough

Jordan Lee: For determinism: we should add `policy_version` or `gate_definition_hash` so we can prove what comparator/threshold were used at eval time.

Chloe Martin: Is that too heavy for Console UI?

Jordan Lee: FE can ignore it, but it matters for debugging “why did it change” when defaults update.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Hash is fine. Just don’t include the entire policy document.

Jada Williams: Adding `gate_definition_hash` (string). Also `policy_engine_build` maybe? (like git sha)

Hannah Rivera: Can we also include `next_evaluation_at`? FE wants to show “re-evaluates in ~30s” for auto gates.

Jordan Lee: That’s scheduler-dependent and not guaranteed. I’d rather not.

Chloe Martin: Agree, don’t promise schedules.

Ravi Menon: One more: when rollout is in multi-region, do we return per-region evidence? Or aggregated?

Logan Wright: v1 is aggregated across regions but we keep region breakdown in dashboards. Returning per-region in API may explode payload / complexity.

Allison Grant: Agree. But at least include `aggregation_scope`: `GLOBAL|PER_REGION_AGGREGATED` or just a note in `scope_display`.

Jada Williams: Let’s add `aggregation_scope`: `POOL_GLOBAL` (default). Future can be `POOL_BY_REGION`.

Kenji Nakamura: Works for UX. If customer asks “which region failed” we can send them to dashboard.

Chloe Martin: Ok, action items:
- Jada update doc + backend response schema (ENG-5980)
- Logan confirm `query_status` mapping + staleness behavior
- Jordan provide list of reason codes + ensure policy engine emits them

Jada Williams: +1. I’ll post final contract text here once updated.

Jada Williams: Final (what I’m implementing unless someone objects in next ~2h):
- `status_reason_code` enum as above
- `query_status` enum `OK|ERROR|STALE|NO_DATA`
- `gate_definition_hash` included
- `dashboard_url`, `trace_search_url` are top-level strings
- `query_admin` omitted for non-admin

Allison Grant: :thumbsup:

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll DM you the current `query_ref` allowlist + the pretty display templates.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: sending reason code list in a sec.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: :thumbsup: please ensure authz check is “customer_admin can view, but only internal_admin can view query_admin”.

Chloe Martin: :white_check_mark: locking this in. FE will assume fields exist (nullable) vs missing; please be consistent.

Jada Williams: ack. Nullable for values, present fields for stability. `query_admin` is the only conditional omit.

source.messages:
Kaitlyn Nguyen: Need a decision on RBAC for route experiments before we lock the GA roles. Specifically: who can Start/Stop an experiment? And do we need a break-glass path for oncall?

Marissa Cole: +1. Beta customers asked “who in my org can stop an experiment if it’s blowing up prod.” We need a simple story in Console.

Sean Gallagher: For oncall: we *must* be able to stop quickly even if the customer team misconfigured RBAC / no one’s online. But also we can’t turn “stop” into “edit routing policy.”

Marcus Lin: Security perspective: “stop experiment” is a production-impacting action. Should be least-privileged but not blocked by needing full route admin.

Dylan Brooks: Current proposal in Console backend is `experiments:write` for create/update/start/stop. That’s too coarse.

Chloe Martin: Yeah right now it’s a single gate on mutation endpoints. We can split actions:
- create/update = `experiments:configure`
- start/stop = `experiments:operate`
- delete = `experiments:admin`

Jada Williams: Also note stop semantics: we have "stop (immediate)" and "stop (drain)" coming (state machine thread). If we expose drain later, that’s still an “operate” permission.

Rafael Mendes: If guardrail auto-stop triggers, that’s not a human. We should ensure audit logs show `actor=system` w/ reason `guardrail_violation`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Agree. The question is: do we tie operate permissions to *route ownership*? e.g. if you can operate a route, you can start/stop experiments on that route.

Marissa Cole: UX wise: I’d rather not force customers to manage a second permission set if they already have route-level roles.

Marcus Lin: But experiments can shift traffic between models/variants. That’s basically routing. If someone can stop/start experiments on a route they don’t own, they can still change prod behavior.

Sean Gallagher: During incident, we want the opposite: allow stop even if you don’t have route-level perms. But only for a tiny set of responders.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: So maybe normal path: operate requires BOTH:
1) experiments:operate
2) route:operate for that route
…and then break-glass overrides #2.

Chloe Martin: Implementation-wise we can check:
- request has workspace role permitting experiments:operate
- AND the actor has access to route_id via existing policy ACL

Dylan Brooks: What about “stop only” as a separate permission? People keep asking for “let my support team stop but not start.”

Marcus Lin: +1. Split start/stop.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Something like:
```
experiments:start
experiments:stop
experiments:configure
experiments:delete
```
But we should map these to bundles in Console (Admin/Operator/Editor/Viewer).

Connor O'Brien: From release POV: fewer roles for GA. But we can keep internal fine-grain and expose 3-4 bundles.

Marissa Cole: Bundles I’d suggest:
- Viewer (read-only)
- Editor (create/update, not start/stop?)
- Operator (start/stop)
- Admin (delete + everything)

Rafael Mendes: Editor without operate is weird; you can stage config but can’t run it. Some customers will want that separation though.

Sean Gallagher: Staging config w/o operate actually matches “change management.” Platform team configures, SRE team operates.

Chloe Martin: We can make it optional in enterprise but default bundles for hosted. Still—backend permissions should support it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Ok, break-glass: what’s the actual flow? We can’t just say “SRE can do it” without process.

Sean Gallagher: Proposal: Redwood SRE “BreakGlass” role in our internal tenant only. Requires:
- MFA
- reason string (ticket/incident link)
- time-limited session (e.g., 1h)
Then can perform *stop* and *hard-disable* at feature-flag layer.

Marcus Lin: Make sure stop action is scoped. Break-glass should be able to stop an experiment by `experiment_id` without needing access to customer configs beyond what’s required. Also ensure redaction in debug endpoint.

Kevin Osei: For audit export: enterprise customers will want to see that Redwood used break-glass + reason. We should include `actor_type=redwood_operator`, `impersonation=false`, and a `support_case_id` field if provided.

Marissa Cole: Do we want customers to have their own “break-glass admin” too? Like an account-level owner who can always stop, regardless of route ownership.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s basically “Org Admin” for their workspace. We already have that in RBAC model. Question is: should Org Admin bypass route ACLs.

Marcus Lin: Careful. If Org Admin bypasses route ACLs, it can undermine route isolation across business units. Some enterprises explicitly want BU isolation even from central admins.

Dylan Brooks: But in practice, someone needs to recover the system. If we don’t allow bypass, they’ll file a support ticket anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Another angle: allow Org Admin to stop but not to start/configure across all routes. “Emergency stop” is safer than “emergency start.”

Sean Gallagher: +1, the safest universal action is "stop" (reduces blast radius). Start/configure should remain scoped.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: So policy:
- `experiments:stop` can be granted at workspace scope (Org Admin / Emergency Operator)
- `experiments:start` requires route-level operate
- create/update requires route-level admin (or route editor)

Chloe Martin: Implementation detail: stop endpoint currently does `PATCH /experiments/{id}:stop`. We can enforce:
- if actor lacks route access, allow only if actor has workspace emergency_stop flag
- and only if experiment is in RUNNING/DEGRADED (not DRAFT) to prevent messing with configs

Marcus Lin: Also log the diff: `status: RUNNING -> STOPPING` and include the reason. Stop reason must be captured from UI/API.

Marissa Cole: UX: we can add a modal for stop requiring a reason (optional for normal operators, required for emergency stop?).

Sean Gallagher: Required for emergency stop. Otherwise, people will click-stop without context during incidents.

Connor O'Brien: Can we get this locked today? Release train wants RBAC checklists.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Proposed decision (unless objections):
1) Split permissions: `experiments:configure`, `experiments:start`, `experiments:stop`, `experiments:delete`.
2) Default bundles exposed in Console: Viewer / Editor / Operator / Admin.
3) Start/configure scoped by route ACL (must have route access).
4) Stop allowed via route ACL OR via workspace-level “Emergency Stop” permission (no start/configure), with required reason + extra audit fields.
5) Redwood internal BreakGlass can exercise Emergency Stop; always audited with support/incident link.

Marcus Lin: Works for security if (4) is truly stop-only and audited. Add: break-glass sessions time-bound + MFA.

Sean Gallagher: Works for SRE. Also add a kill switch to disable hard-stop automation if we get false positives (separate thread but related).

Marissa Cole: Works for product. We’ll document “Emergency Stop” in enterprise RBAC docs.

Chloe Martin: I’ll update ENG-9029/9030 implementation notes and add tests for start vs stop perms. :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: I’ll update runbooks: who can stop, and where to find audit logs. Also add a note on stop reason.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Cool. I’ll write up a short RBAC section for the PRD + ping Kevin for audit export fields.

Kevin Osei: Please include: `emergency=true/false`, `reason`, `actor_role`, and `support_case_id` if present. I’ll sync with audit pipeline.

Connor O'Brien: Marking as decision for GA checklist. Thanks all.

preview:
Kaitlyn Nguyen: Need a decision on RBAC for route experiments before we lock the GA roles. Specifically: who can Start/Stop an experiment? And do we need a break-glass path for oncall?

Marissa Cole: +1. Beta customers asked “who in my org can stop an experiment if it’s blowing up prod.” We need a simple story in Console.

Sean Gallagher: For oncall: we *must* be able to stop quickly even if the customer team misconfigured RBAC / no one’s online. But also we can’t turn “stop” into “edit routing policy.”

Marcus Lin: Security perspective: “stop experiment” is a production-impacting action. Should be least-privileged but not blocked by needing full route admin.

Dylan Brooks: Current proposal in Console backend is `experiments:write` for create/update/start/stop. That’s too coarse.

Chloe Martin: Yeah right now it’s a single gate on mutation endpoints. We can split actions:
- create/update = `experiments:configure`
- start/stop = `experiments:operate`
- delete = `experiments:admin`

Jada Williams: Also note stop semantics: we have "stop (immediate)" and "stop (drain)" coming (state machine thread). If we expose drain later, that’s still an “operate” permission.

Rafael Mendes: If guardrail auto-stop triggers, that’s not a human. We should ensure audit logs show `actor=system` w/ reason `guardrail_violation`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Agree. The question is: do we tie operate permissions to *route ownership*? e.g. if you can operate a route, you can start/stop experiments on that route.

Marissa Cole: UX wise: I’d rather not force customers to manage a second permission set if they already have route-level roles.

Marcus Lin: But experiments can shift traffic between models/variants. That’s basically routing. If someone can stop/start experiments on a route they don’t own, they can still change prod behavior.

Sean Gallagher: During incident, we want the opposite: allow stop even if you don’t have route-level perms. But only for a tiny set of responders.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: So maybe normal path: operate requires BOTH:
1) experiments:operate
2) route:operate for that route
…and then break-glass overrides #2.

Chloe Martin: Implementation-wise we can check:
- request has workspace role permitting experiments:operate
- AND the actor has access to route_id via existing policy ACL

Dylan Brooks: What about “stop only” as a separate permission? People keep asking for “let my support team stop but not start.”

Marcus Lin: +1. Split start/stop.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Something like:
```
experiments:start
experiments:stop
experiments:configure
experiments:delete
```
But we should map these to bundles in Console (Admin/Operator/Editor/Viewer).

Connor O'Brien: From release POV: fewer roles for GA. But we can keep internal fine-grain and expose 3-4 bundles.

Marissa Cole: Bundles I’d suggest:
- Viewer (read-only)
- Editor (create/update, not start/stop?)
- Operator (start/stop)
- Admin (delete + everything)

Rafael Mendes: Editor without operate is weird; you can stage config but can’t run it. Some customers will want that separation though.

Sean Gallagher: Staging config w/o operate actually matches “change management.” Platform team configures, SRE team operates.

Chloe Martin: We can make it optional in enterprise but default bundles for hosted. Still—backend permissions should support it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Ok, break-glass: what’s the actual flow? We can’t just say “SRE can do it” without process.

Sean Gallagher: Proposal: Redwood SRE “BreakGlass” role in our internal tenant only. Requires:
- MFA
- reason string (ticket/incident link)
- time-limited session (e.g., 1h)
Then can perform *stop* and *hard-disable* at feature-flag layer.

Marcus Lin: Make sure stop action is scoped. Break-glass should be able to stop an experiment by `experiment_id` without needing access to customer configs beyond what’s required. Also ensure redaction in debug endpoint.

Kevin Osei: For audit export: enterprise customers will want to see that Redwood used break-glass + reason. We should include `actor_type=redwood_operator`, `impersonation=false`, and a `support_case_id` field if provided.

Marissa Cole: Do we want customers to have their own “break-glass admin” too? Like an account-level owner who can always stop, regardless of route ownership.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s basically “Org Admin” for their workspace. We already have that in RBAC model. Question is: should Org Admin bypass route ACLs.

Marcus Lin: Careful. If Org Admin bypasses route ACLs, it can undermine route isolation across business units. Some enterprises explicitly want BU isolation even from central admins.

Dylan Brooks: But in practice, someone needs to recover the system. If we don’t allow bypass, they’ll file a support ticket anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Another angle: allow Org Admin to stop but not to start/configure across all routes. “Emergency stop” is safer than “emergency start.”

Sean Gallagher: +1, the safest universal action is "stop" (reduces blast radius). Start/configure should remain scoped.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: So policy:
- `experiments:stop` can be granted at workspace scope (Org Admin / Emergency Operator)
- `experiments:start` requires route-level operate
- create/update requires route-level admin (or route editor)

Chloe Martin: Implementation detail: stop endpoint currently does `PATCH /experiments/{id}:stop`. We can enforce:
- if actor lacks route access, allow only if actor has workspace emergency_stop flag
- and only if experiment is in RUNNING/DEGRADED (not DRAFT) to prevent messing with configs

Marcus Lin: Also log the diff: `status: RUNNING -> STOPPING` and include the reason. Stop reason must be captured from UI/API.

Marissa Cole: UX: we can add a modal for stop requiring a reason (optional for normal operators, required for emergency stop?).

Sean Gallagher: Required for emergency stop. Otherwise, people will click-stop without context during incidents.

Connor O'Brien: Can we get this locked today? Release train wants RBAC checklists.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Proposed decision (unless objections):
1) Split permissions: `experiments:configure`, `experiments:start`, `experiments:stop`, `experiments:delete`.
2) Default bundles exposed in Console: Viewer / Editor / Operator / Admin.
3) Start/configure scoped by route ACL (must have route access).
4) Stop allowed via route ACL OR via workspace-level “Emergency Stop” permission (no start/configure), with required reason + extra audit fields.
5) Redwood internal BreakGlass can exercise Emergency Stop; always audited with support/incident link.

Marcus Lin: Works for security if (4) is truly stop-only and audited. Add: break-glass sessions time-bound + MFA.

Sean Gallagher: Works for SRE. Also add a kill switch to disable hard-stop automation if we get false positives (separate thread but related).

Marissa Cole: Works for product. We’ll document “Emergency Stop” in enterprise RBAC docs.

Chloe Martin: I’ll update ENG-9029/9030 implementation notes and add tests for start vs stop perms. :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: I’ll update runbooks: who can stop, and where to find audit logs. Also add a note on stop reason.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Cool. I’ll write up a short RBAC section for the PRD + ping Kevin for audit export fields.

Kevin Osei: Please include: `emergency=true/false`, `reason`, `actor_role`, and `support_case_id` if present. I’ll sync with audit pipeline.

Connor O'Brien: Marking as decision for GA checklist. Thanks all.
