source.content:
Allison Grant: hey folks, need to settle model grouping for unit econ before we lock warehouse dims. right now we’ve got a mix of “marketing name” and internal model ids showing up in metering, and top-N tables look… chaotic.

Quentin Harris: +1. today model shows as any of:
- `meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct`
- `llama-3.1-70b`
- `Llama 3.1 70B Instruct`
- `L3.1-70B-Instruct`
- `rw/llama3.1-70b-instruct` (gateway alias)
We need a canonical key for reporting.

Logan Wright: agreed. the metric dictionary wants drilldown by model + version + quant profile. if model is “whatever string the caller sent”, unit cost becomes unusable.

Felix Schneider: for the dashboard UX, Finance/Product will want the friendly names (e.g. “Llama 3.1 70B Instruct”) but grouping can’t be on display names or we’ll get splits.

Mei Lin: on the warehouse side, we can materialize a dimension table and join: `canonical_model_id`, `model_release`, `model_version`, `serving_variant`, `display_name`, `alias[]`. but we need an authoritative source + rules for when an alias maps.

Nadia Rahman: from metering POV: we should not treat alias as the primary dim. dims should be stable + bounded cardinality.
Proposal:
- metering emits `canonical_model_id` (required) and optionally `requested_model` (raw string) for debugging
- aggregation uses `canonical_model_id` for grouping
- UI shows `display_name` looked up from metadata table

Marissa Cole: product perspective: yes to “friendly name in UI, canonical id under the hood”. also we’ll need a way to group across “marketing bundles” later (e.g. “Redwood Verified: Llama 3.1 70B”) but that’s a separate label.

Rishi Malhotra: finance angle: grouping must be consistent quarter-over-quarter. if marketing renames a model or we change the console label, the historical series must not fork.
Also we need a paper trail for renames (so we can explain why labels changed but numbers didn’t).

Chloe Martin: implementation detail: query API currently accepts `model` filter as string. we should standardize it to `canonical_model_id` and allow `display_name` search in the UI, but API stays canonical.

Quentin Harris: ok so key question: what is “canonical_model_id”? is it the upstream provider id (meta/…) or our internal “serving model id”? because we sometimes serve different weights under same “marketing model” (hotfix, safety finetune, etc.)

Logan Wright: for unit econ the canonical key should map to the actual served weights/config, otherwise cost deltas will get blurred.
So I’d define:
- `canonical_model_id`: stable internal identifier for the base weights lineage (e.g. `rw.model.llama31_70b_instruct`)
- `model_version`: semver-ish / git-ish immutable build of the weights + runtime config (e.g. `2025-02-01.1`)
- `quantization_profile`: separate dim
and then `display_name` can be “Llama 3.1 70B Instruct”.

Nadia Rahman: +1. and per the token metering dimension standard we should avoid unbounded strings. `requested_model` should be sampled/hashed or only in logs, but not in primary metrics pipeline.

Mei Lin: we can do this with two tables:
1) `dim_model_versions`
- canonical_model_id
- model_version
- display_name
- provider
- upstream_model_id (optional)
- active_from / active_to
2) `map_model_aliases`
- alias (string)
- canonical_model_id
- model_version (nullable; if alias pins to a specific version)
- source (gateway/runtime/marketing)
- created_by
- created_at
Then aggregation fact tables store only canonical + version + quant profile.

Felix Schneider: from a UI standpoint: filter control can show display_name, but when user selects we store canonical id in URL state. also top-N tables should show display_name with a tooltip “canonical: …”.

Allison Grant: what about “marketing name” that spans multiple versions? e.g. “Llama 3.1 70B Instruct” should include all versions unless user drills into version.

Quentin Harris: that’s fine. alias mapping can be to canonical only (no version) for most entries. if a customer pins `llama-3.1-70b-instruct@2025-02-01` then mapping can include version.

Rishi Malhotra: do we ever change canonical_model_id? please no.

Quentin Harris: canonical_model_id should be immutable once created. if we deprecate, we set active_to and stop routing new traffic, but keep it for history.

Logan Wright: edge case: “RW-Chat-70B” is a marketing bundle that could route to different backends over time (capacity/routing). we should NOT use that as canonical. treat it as an alias label only.

Marissa Cole: yes. for v1 we’ll avoid “bundle names” as the primary grouping. can add a separate dimension later like `product_model_family` or `catalog_slug`.

Nadia Rahman: also need an explicit “unknown” behavior. if canonical_model_id is missing/null, do we drop rows? (please no). we should map to `rw.model.unknown` and flag coverage.

Mei Lin: +1. warehouse aggregation can coalesce null -> `rw.model.unknown` and we alert on %unknown above threshold. also helps with the confidence indicator idea.

Felix Schneider: can we keep a small “Model alias splits” panel for debugging? like top requested_model strings that map into unknown.

Allison Grant: sounds good.

Chloe Martin: action item: update API contract to accept `canonical_model_id` and optionally `model_version` filter. UI will do lookup/search by display_name.

Quentin Harris: I’ll publish a first cut of `dim_model_versions` and `map_model_aliases` in the model metadata repo. We can seed it with the current catalog + known gateway aliases.

Rishi Malhotra: please add a “rename log” / change history. we need to be able to explain why display_name changed in a given week.

Quentin Harris: will do. we can include `display_name` in dim with effective dates + a `notes` field (and keep old names as aliases).

Logan Wright: decision summary (for the record):
- Grouping key in unit economics = (`canonical_model_id`, `model_version`, `quantization_profile`)
- Dashboard displays `display_name` from metadata, not the raw id
- Marketing names / catalog slugs are aliases/labels, not grouping keys
- `requested_model` is not a primary metric dimension; used only for debug/coverage panels
- Null/unknown canonical ids roll up to `rw.model.unknown` and trigger coverage alerts

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: works for me. Quentin can you drop a link once the seed table is ready? we’ll wire it into the aggregation job join.

Quentin Harris: yep, will post in this thread tomorrow. also, please stop adding “new names” directly in gateway config without adding to alias map :)

Marissa Cole: +1. we’ll add “model naming + alias mapping” to the launch checklist so we don’t regress.

Felix Schneider: thanks all. I’ll update the top-N table behavior to show display name but stable grouping. will also add tooltip for canonical/version to reduce confusion.

Nadia Rahman: and I’ll make sure metering enforces canonical_model_id as required on the streaming path too, not just non-streaming. (we’ve been burned here).

Allison Grant: closing the loop: we’re good to proceed with this approach for v1. if anyone objects, speak now, otherwise I’ll mark the thread as resolved in PM notes.

Mei Lin: seeing a weird mismatch in daily aggregates between the hourly unit econ rollups and Finance’s QTD view. looks like “day” is not the same definition. anyone else looking at this?

Rishi Malhotra: yes — Finance is pulling daily numbers by “Pacific day” (midnight PT -> 11:59pm PT) for internal reporting. dashboard tables are grouping by `date_trunc('day', hour_ts)` which is UTC. so we’re effectively shifting a chunk of traffic into the wrong day.

Laura Bennett: +1, this is showing up in the reconciliation report as ~1.2–1.8% day-over-day volatility that’s not real spend. totals over a full month are close, but daily deltas don’t tie to our close process.

Logan Wright: ok that’s likely it. our hourly pipeline emits `hour_bucket` in UTC and the “daily” view is a simple rollup. we never set an explicit reporting timezone.

Caleb Johnson: confirming w/ a quick check in warehouse. if I rebuild daily totals using America/Los_Angeles conversion, the Finance export matches within rounding.

Caleb Johnson: example:
```
-- current (UTC)
select date_trunc('day', hour_ts) as day_utc, sum(output_tokens) from econ_hourly group by 1;

-- finance (PT)
select date_trunc('day', convert_timezone('UTC','America/Los_Angeles', hour_ts)) as day_pt,
       sum(output_tokens)
from econ_hourly
group by 1;
```
On high-volume days, the delta is basically the 00:00–07:59 UTC window (which is prior day PT).

Allison Grant: this also explains why we had that “token drop” alert last week around day boundary. alert compares today-vs-yesterday “daily totals” in UTC but Slack humans were looking at PT.

Sean Gallagher: yup. if we keep UTC for storage (good), we need to be explicit for any “daily” SLO/alert and the UI.

Chloe Martin: what’s the expected behavior for the Console? I assumed filters are “viewer local timezone” but that gets messy for exec reporting.

Rishi Malhotra: for Finance KPI reporting, we need a stable definition. proposal: all “Finance view” exports are in PT for now (since corp reporting is PT). for general eng usage we can keep UTC default but the UI has to label it clearly.

Laura Bennett: +1. also note: fiscal close uses PT day boundaries; if we have to explain “it’s UTC” every week, we’ll lose trust.

Felix Schneider: we can add a timezone selector in the filter bar but v1 scope is tight. alternative: ship two tabs/views: “Live (UTC)” and “Finance (PT)”.

Mei Lin: from data model side, easiest is to materialize a `econ_daily_finance_pt` view/table that groups by PT day. keep hourly as-is.

Cole Summers: FYI cost inputs from rate card are daily-ish but applied hourly (we pro-rate hourly rate). timezone doesn’t affect cost rate application, but it does affect which day the cost lands on for daily rollups.

Logan Wright: right. we should also check DST boundaries (spring forward / fall back). PT day length changes. using `America/Los_Angeles` conversion in warehouse should handle it, but we need to ensure any “day has 23/25 hours” doesn’t break completeness checks.

Caleb Johnson: completeness check currently expects 24 hourly buckets per day. that will page us on DST days.

Allison Grant: good catch. we should update the data quality job to treat daily completeness as “all hours present for the window” rather than hardcoding 24. or compute completeness on hourly only.

Sean Gallagher: for SLO we should stick to hourly freshness/completeness anyway (e.g., last complete hour < 90m) and avoid a daily completeness alert that depends on timezone.

Chloe Martin: for the API endpoints: today we accept `start_ts`/`end_ts` and `granularity=hour|day`. if we keep “day” meaning UTC, Finance will keep getting bitten.

Felix Schneider: could extend API with `timezone` param for day/week bucketing, default UTC. but needs caching key updates (timezone affects results) + docs.

Logan Wright: +1 timezone param is the clean fix long term. but for next week’s Finance signoff, let’s do a short-term warehouse view in PT and point the Finance export to it.

Laura Bennett: timeline-wise: I need QTD daily numbers stable by Monday for the KPI pack draft. I’m ok with “Finance view (PT)” being the canonical for Finance.

Mei Lin: action items I can take:
1) add `econ_daily_finance_pt_v1` materialized view (or table) grouped by PT day
2) update reconciliation report to use PT day for “daily” comparison
3) add a note in metric dictionary about storage in UTC vs reporting timezones

Rishi Malhotra: please also document explicitly: “Finance day = America/Los_Angeles”, including DST behavior. we should link it from the blended cost methodology note.

Caleb Johnson: I’ll patch the data quality checks: remove the “24 buckets/day” assumption and instead validate coverage across the query window. will also add a DST unit test.

Allison Grant: I’ll update alert definitions in Grafana pack — anything “daily” should either be hourly-based or explicitly tagged as UTC/PT.

Sean Gallagher: can we also add UI copy? even just “Daily buckets are UTC” vs “Daily buckets are Pacific Time” to avoid confusion.

Chloe Martin: I’ll file a follow-up for API `timezone` param + UI label. likely v1.1 unless we can sneak it in.

Felix Schneider: quick UI workaround: on Finance tab we can show a pill “Timezone: PT” next to the granularity selector. minimal effort.

Logan Wright: sounds good. Mei/Caleb please drop PR links here when up.

Mei Lin: will do. aiming EOD today for the view + reconciliation report change.

Rishi Malhotra: thanks all. once that’s live I’ll rerun QTD and confirm it ties to our FP&A sheet.

Laura Bennett: appreciate it. this is exactly the kind of thing that causes executive “why did cost spike on Tuesday” questions.

Caleb Johnson: lol yep. also: we should consider week boundaries too (Finance weeks start Monday PT). but one fire at a time.

Allison Grant: noted. adding to backlog: week bucketing + fiscal calendar support.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: ping me if any alert noise changes need review.

Mei Lin: PR is up for the PT daily view + recon update, will share link in eng-platform once merged.

source.text:
Felix Schneider: Heads up: seeing a pretty gnarly DB load spike tied to Console route experiment *results* view. :warning: Grafana panel for `console-api postgres cpu` jumped from ~35% -> 92% when a couple folks opened Results dashboards.

Felix Schneider: Looks like the new “variant deltas” endpoint is doing a heavy query when you toggle `group_by=model` / `group_by=region`. Anyone around to help triage? I can drop query sample.

Chloe Martin: yep, looking. Which env/cluster? prod or staging?

Felix Schneider: prod. `console-api` hitting `cp-prod-aurora-01`. Started ~10 min ago.

Deploy Bot: [alert] aurora-cp-prod-aurora-01: CPUUtilization > 85% for 10m (sev2)

Sean Gallagher: on it. Can we confirm it’s *read* saturation vs locks?

Felix Schneider: from RDS insights: mostly read + temp file usage. no obvious lock waits.

Chloe Martin: paste query / explain if you have it.

Felix Schneider: from slow query log (redacted ids):
```
SELECT
  date_trunc('minute', bucket_ts) as t,
  variant_id,
  region,
  model_id,
  percentile_cont(0.95) within group (order by latency_ms) as p95,
  sum(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens) as tokens,
  sum(cost_usd) as cost
FROM experiment_request_facts
WHERE experiment_id = $1
  AND route_id = $2
  AND bucket_ts BETWEEN $3 AND $4
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
ORDER BY 1 ASC;
```
This is for `group_by=region,model` (the toggle in UI).

Allison Grant: that’s going straight to request_facts?? that table is huge, right? we intended results UI to hit the rollups.

Logan Wright: yeah, request_facts is the “raw-ish” attribution table. Console should be reading `experiment_variant_metrics_hourly` (or 5m) rollup dataset.

Mei Lin: +1. GROUP BY 4 dims over a large time range = temp files + pain. Also `bucket_ts BETWEEN` on a non-partitioned table will scan more than you think.

Chloe Martin: I think the new Results endpoint is using a fallback code path when rollups aren’t available (it tries to be “real time” if aggregation lags). Might be kicking in due to rollup freshness check.

Nadia Rahman: also cost_usd in request_facts is derived from token accounting join, right? if that join is happening inline anywhere, it’s worse.

Felix Schneider: the endpoint is `/v1/experiments/:id/results` (from PR-28644/28658). When you open Results it requests last 24h by default.

Jordan Lee: can we just cap it to 1h until we fix? 24h *by minute* *and* grouping by region/model is asking for it.

Dylan Brooks: jumping in. We need an immediate mitigation (today) + proper fix (rollups/caching). What’s quickest? feature-flag disable the group_by toggles?

Chloe Martin: quick mitigations I can ship:
1) hard cap default window to last 1h (still allow 24h via explicit “Load more”)
2) disallow `group_by=region,model` combined for now (pick one)
3) add response cache (in-memory) keyed by `{experiment_id, route_id, window, group_by}` with 60s TTL

Sean Gallagher: do (1) + (2) now. Cache is good but doesn’t fix first-hit thundering herd if a bunch of ppl click the same dashboard after a deploy.

Allison Grant: what about server-side “top-N regions/models + other”? we don’t need all combos.

Logan Wright: longer-term we should not be grouping raw request_facts at all. We already emit rollups by {route, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id} at 5m granularity in analytics. Console should query that.

Mei Lin: if we *must* query request_facts in emergency, add index on `(experiment_id, route_id, bucket_ts)` at minimum. But that’s still heavy for percentile calc.

Caleb Johnson: percentile_cont over millions of rows is expensive. Our rollup tables store precomputed p50/p95/p99 already.

Felix Schneider: why isn’t it using rollup? :thinking:

Chloe Martin: I found it. The endpoint checks “rollup staleness” and if lag > 10m it flips to raw to “avoid stale charts”. Our rollup lag monitor is currently reading ~18m in prod (aggregation job behind).

Rafael Mendes: ^ that matches what I saw earlier: the experiment rollup job is running slower after we added dimensions. Might be backlog from yesterday.

Nadia Rahman: yep cost attribution step got heavier after adding experiment tags. We can reduce by moving cost calc into the rollup job once per bucket (not per request query).

Dylan Brooks: so two parallel tracks:
- Console: stop falling back to raw (or make fallback *coarser* / smaller window)
- Analytics: fix rollup lag

Sean Gallagher: I’m strongly against “fallback to raw” in prod if it can melt the DB. Prefer stale data + banner over taking out control plane.

Allison Grant: agree. We can show “Data is delayed (~18m)” and keep dashboard responsive.

Chloe Martin: proposed change: remove raw fallback entirely for prod, behind `route_experiments_results_raw_fallback` flag default OFF. Only allow for internal staging.

Felix Schneider: +1. UX wise we can add a yellow banner “Results delayed” if rollup staleness > threshold.

Logan Wright: for rollup lag: I can check the aggregator workers. Might need to bump concurrency or reduce cardinality expansions (we had that thread last week).

Mei Lin: also query side: ensure results endpoint is hitting the read replica / analytics store if possible, not primary CP DB.

Caleb Johnson: the rollup dataset lives in analytics DB already. Console results API is still wired to CP DB because it was easiest. We should switch to analytics connection for results.

Dylan Brooks: ok, immediate plan:
1) Chloe: hotfix to disable raw fallback + cap default window to 1h + disable combined group_by (ship behind flag, roll out asap)
2) Logan/Caleb/Mei: unstick rollup lag (today) + confirm where rollup lives and best query path
3) Sean/Rafael: keep an eye on DB CPU, set temporary alert threshold + paging until mitigations are out

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: also add a rate limit / circuit breaker on that endpoint. If `db_cpu>80%`, return 429 with “try again” vs continuing to pile on.

Chloe Martin: I can add a simple guard: if request asks for > 6h window with minute granularity, reject. Also clamp bucket size to 5m/1h depending on window.

Felix Schneider: I’ll open a quick UI patch to make 24h not the default + hide region/model combo toggle until backend fix. (I’ll coordinate with Kenji later)

Rafael Mendes: meanwhile I’m muting non-critical alerts to reduce noise, but keeping the sev2 on CPU.

Deploy Bot: deploy started: console-api hotfix branch `disable-results-raw-fallback` -> prod canary

Logan Wright: update: rollup workers are behind due to a bad query plan on `experiment_variant_metrics_5m` upsert. Mei, I’m DMing you the job logs. Might need an index on conflict target.

Mei Lin: got it. The `ON CONFLICT (experiment_id, route_id, variant_id, bucket_ts, region, model_id)` needs a matching unique index; I don’t think it exists in prod.

Caleb Johnson: wow ok that would absolutely explain backlog.

Nadia Rahman: if we’re adding that unique index, please double-check cardinality constraints (region/model normalized) so we don’t explode row count.

Chloe Martin: canary is live. Raw fallback disabled, default window set to 1h. Combined group_by returns 400 with message “Select a single breakdown (region OR model)”.

Sean Gallagher: DB CPU already trending down: 92% -> 63% in last 5m.

Felix Schneider: dashboard is usable again on my end. Results are slightly delayed but at least page loads.

Allison Grant: please add a postmortem-ish note somewhere: “Never query raw request_facts for percentiles from Console.” This will come up again.

Dylan Brooks: +1. After incident: we should (a) move results to analytics DB (b) add caching (c) ensure rollup index exists via migration (d) keep stale-banner behavior.

Deploy Bot: deploy finished: console-api hotfix -> prod 100%

Rafael Mendes: marking incident mitigated. Keeping watch until rollup backlog is cleared.

Logan Wright: rollup index migration queued for tonight’s maintenance window. In the meantime we increased worker concurrency + reduced per-bucket cost calc.

Sean Gallagher: thanks all. If CPU spikes again, we’ll flip the results feature flag off for affected tenants.

title: #eng-platform: 1738798031
preview:
Felix Schneider: Heads up: seeing a pretty gnarly DB load spike tied to Console route experiment *results* view. :warning: Grafana panel for `console-api postgres cpu` jumped from ~35% -> 92% when a couple folks opened Results dashboards.

Felix Schneider: Looks like the new “variant deltas” endpoint is doing a heavy query when you toggle `group_by=model` / `group_by=region`. Anyone around to help triage? I can drop query sample.

Chloe Martin: yep, looking. Which env/cluster? prod or staging?

Felix Schneider: prod. `console-api` hitting `cp-prod-aurora-01`. Started ~10 min ago.

Deploy Bot: [alert] aurora-cp-prod-aurora-01: CPUUtilization > 85% for 10m (sev2)

Sean Gallagher: on it. Can we confirm it’s *read* saturation vs locks?

Felix Schneider: from RDS insights: mostly read + temp file usage. no obvious lock waits.

Chloe Martin: paste query / explain if you have it.

Felix Schneider: from slow query log (redacted ids):
```
SELECT
  date_trunc('minute', bucket_ts) as t,
  variant_id,
  region,
  model_id,
  percentile_cont(0.95) within group (order by latency_ms) as p95,
  sum(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens) as tokens,
  sum(cost_usd) as cost
FROM experiment_request_facts
WHERE experiment_id = $1
  AND route_id = $2
  AND bucket_ts BETWEEN $3 AND $4
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
ORDER BY 1 ASC;
```
This is for `group_by=region,model` (the toggle in UI).

Allison Grant: that’s going straight to request_facts?? that table is huge, right? we intended results UI to hit the rollups.

Logan Wright: yeah, request_facts is the “raw-ish” attribution table. Console should be reading `experiment_variant_metrics_hourly` (or 5m) rollup dataset.

Mei Lin: +1. GROUP BY 4 dims over a large time range = temp files + pain. Also `bucket_ts BETWEEN` on a non-partitioned table will scan more than you think.

Chloe Martin: I think the new Results endpoint is using a fallback code path when rollups aren’t available (it tries to be “real time” if aggregation lags). Might be kicking in due to rollup freshness check.

Nadia Rahman: also cost_usd in request_facts is derived from token accounting join, right? if that join is happening inline anywhere, it’s worse.

Felix Schneider: the endpoint is `/v1/experiments/:id/results` (from PR-28644/28658). When you open Results it requests last 24h by default.

Jordan Lee: can we just cap it to 1h until we fix? 24h *by minute* *and* grouping by region/model is asking for it.

Dylan Brooks: jumping in. We need an immediate mitigation (today) + proper fix (rollups/caching). What’s quickest? feature-flag disable the group_by toggles?

Chloe Martin: quick mitigations I can ship:
1) hard cap default window to last 1h (still allow 24h via explicit “Load more”)
2) disallow `group_by=region,model` combined for now (pick one)
3) add response cache (in-memory) keyed by `{experiment_id, route_id, window, group_by}` with 60s TTL

Sean Gallagher: do (1) + (2) now. Cache is good but doesn’t fix first-hit thundering herd if a bunch of ppl click the same dashboard after a deploy.

Allison Grant: what about server-side “top-N regions/models + other”? we don’t need all combos.

Logan Wright: longer-term we should not be grouping raw request_facts at all. We already emit rollups by {route, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id} at 5m granularity in analytics. Console should query that.

Mei Lin: if we *must* query request_facts in emergency, add index on `(experiment_id, route_id, bucket_ts)` at minimum. But that’s still heavy for percentile calc.

Caleb Johnson: percentile_cont over millions of rows is expensive. Our rollup tables store precomputed p50/p95/p99 already.

Felix Schneider: why isn’t it using rollup? :thinking:

Chloe Martin: I found it. The endpoint checks “rollup staleness” and if lag > 10m it flips to raw to “avoid stale charts”. Our rollup lag monitor is currently reading ~18m in prod (aggregation job behind).

Rafael Mendes: ^ that matches what I saw earlier: the experiment rollup job is running slower after we added dimensions. Might be backlog from yesterday.

Nadia Rahman: yep cost attribution step got heavier after adding experiment tags. We can reduce by moving cost calc into the rollup job once per bucket (not per request query).

Dylan Brooks: so two parallel tracks:
- Console: stop falling back to raw (or make fallback *coarser* / smaller window)
- Analytics: fix rollup lag

Sean Gallagher: I’m strongly against “fallback to raw” in prod if it can melt the DB. Prefer stale data + banner over taking out control plane.

Allison Grant: agree. We can show “Data is delayed (~18m)” and keep dashboard responsive.

Chloe Martin: proposed change: remove raw fallback entirely for prod, behind `route_experiments_results_raw_fallback` flag default OFF. Only allow for internal staging.

Felix Schneider: +1. UX wise we can add a yellow banner “Results delayed” if rollup staleness > threshold.

Logan Wright: for rollup lag: I can check the aggregator workers. Might need to bump concurrency or reduce cardinality expansions (we had that thread last week).

Mei Lin: also query side: ensure results endpoint is hitting the read replica / analytics store if possible, not primary CP DB.

Caleb Johnson: the rollup dataset lives in analytics DB already. Console results API is still wired to CP DB because it was easiest. We should switch to analytics connection for results.

Dylan Brooks: ok, immediate plan:
1) Chloe: hotfix to disable raw fallback + cap default window to 1h + disable combined group_by (ship behind flag, roll out asap)
2) Logan/Caleb/Mei: unstick rollup lag (today) + confirm where rollup lives and best query path
3) Sean/Rafael: keep an eye on DB CPU, set temporary alert threshold + paging until mitigations are out

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: also add a rate limit / circuit breaker on that endpoint. If `db_cpu>80%`, return 429 with “try again” vs continuing to pile on.

Chloe Martin: I can add a simple guard: if request asks for > 6h window with minute granularity, reject. Also clamp bucket size to 5m/1h depending on window.

Felix Schneider: I’ll open a quick UI patch to make 24h not the default + hide region/model combo toggle until backend fix. (I’ll coordinate with Kenji later)

Rafael Mendes: meanwhile I’m muting non-critical alerts to reduce noise, but keeping the sev2 on CPU.

Deploy Bot: deploy started: console-api hotfix branch `disable-results-raw-fallback` -> prod canary

Logan Wright: update: rollup workers are behind due to a bad query plan on `experiment_variant_metrics_5m` upsert. Mei, I’m DMing you the job logs. Might need an index on conflict target.

Mei Lin: got it. The `ON CONFLICT (experiment_id, route_id, variant_id, bucket_ts, region, model_id)` needs a matching unique index; I don’t think it exists in prod.

Caleb Johnson: wow ok that would absolutely explain backlog.

Nadia Rahman: if we’re adding that unique index, please double-check cardinality constraints (region/model normalized) so we don’t explode row count.

Chloe Martin: canary is live. Raw fallback disabled, default window set to 1h. Combined group_by returns 400 with message “Select a single breakdown (region OR model)”.

Sean Gallagher: DB CPU already trending down: 92% -> 63% in last 5m.

Felix Schneider: dashboard is usable again on my end. Results are slightly delayed but at least page loads.

Allison Grant: please add a postmortem-ish note somewhere: “Never query raw request_facts for percentiles from Console.” This will come up again.

Dylan Brooks: +1. After incident: we should (a) move results to analytics DB (b) add caching (c) ensure rollup index exists via migration (d) keep stale-banner behavior.

Deploy Bot: deploy finished: console-api hotfix -> prod 100%

Rafael Mendes: marking incident mitigated. Keeping watch until rollup backlog is cleared.

Logan Wright: rollup index migration queued for tonight’s maintenance window. In the meantime we increased worker concurrency + reduced per-bucket cost calc.

Sean Gallagher: thanks all. If CPU spikes again, we’ll flip the results feature flag off for affected tenants.

source.messages:
Jordan Lee: We need to lock audit event schema for promote/abort (and overrides). Trying to get ahead of Console + SIEM export.

Jordan Lee: Fields we *think* we need: actor, reason, old/new stage, override flags, and where event emitted (control plane vs policy engine).

Benji Okafor: +1. I’m implementing promote/abort endpoints this week. Need to know exact payload so I don’t have to churn migrations.

Ethan Park: For Dedicated rollouts, we should have a single source of truth: control plane state machine. Emit audit events from there, not from routing/policy engine.

Hannah Rivera: Console wants to show the audit trail row for: promote, abort, hold, force-promote. Also “gate failed -> auto-abort” should show as system action.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Security note: actor must capture both the authenticated principal and the “effective actor” if impersonation / support tools used.

Kevin Osei: + retention/export: we need stable event_type strings + a consistent “resource” tuple so the export pipeline can route it.

Jordan Lee: Ok, proposal (v0). Event types:
- dedicated_pool_rollout.plan_created
- dedicated_pool_rollout.plan_updated
- dedicated_pool_rollout.stage_promoted
- dedicated_pool_rollout.aborted
- dedicated_pool_rollout.held
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_force_promote
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_force_abort (do we need? or just aborted w/ override)
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_release_hold

Benji Okafor: For the schema, can we keep it mostly consistent with existing audit envelope?
Like:
- event_id
- event_type
- ts
- actor (id, type)
- actor_display
- request_id / trace_id
- resource_type + resource_id

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Please don’t rely on actor_display for anything security-related. Nice-to-have only.
Need:
- actor_principal_id
- actor_principal_type (user/service)
- actor_org_id (if internal)
- on_behalf_of_principal_id (optional)
- auth_method (api_key, session, service_token)

Ethan Park: +1. Also include rbac_role / permission evaluated? Maybe too much.
At least include: authorization_decision_id? That’s overkill.

Jordan Lee: For promote/abort specific fields:
- rollout_plan_id
- dedicated_pool_id
- change_id (the thing we’re rolling out, e.g. model_version/runtime_build/policy)
- from_stage_id / to_stage_id
- from_stage_name / to_stage_name
- from_percent / to_percent (if percentage stages)
- from_partition / to_partition (if replica partition stages)
- rollout_state_before / rollout_state_after
- reason (freeform, required for overrides)
- override_type (none|force_promote|force_abort|hold|release_hold)

Hannah Rivera: Console also asked for “gate evidence” pointer. Not the full metrics payload, but a reference.
Maybe:
- gate_evaluation_id
- gate_summary (pass/fail/unknown + which gate tripped)

Rafael Mendes: Oncall POV: for auto-aborts we need to know which gate and the window. Even a short summary is fine.
Don’t make us click 3 different systems during an incident.

Kevin Osei: Careful on putting raw metric values into audit logs (PII risk low, but volume/cardinality). A compact summary is okay.
Also: the export job expects event payload size under ~16KB.

Benji Okafor: Where does gate_evaluation_id live? In policy engine or telemetry?

Jordan Lee: In policy engine (gate evaluator) we generate an evaluation record id. Control plane queries it (or gets it pushed) and makes the state transition.
So audit event should be emitted at the moment control plane persists new state, with gate_evaluation_id attached.

Ethan Park: Strong preference: policy engine should not emit audit events. Otherwise we get duplicates when promote triggers eval and then CP applies transition.
We can log internal events in PE for debugging, but “audit” should be CP only.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Agree. “Audit” == authoritative, deterministic, minimal. Emit on state transition.
But please include:
- idempotency_key (if present)
- optimistic_concurrency_version (or etag) used
So we can reconcile double-clicks / retries.

Benji Okafor: I can include:
- idempotency_key
- request_id
- plan_version_before / plan_version_after (int)

Jordan Lee: What about “who/what triggered the change” for auto events?
I propose:
- trigger: manual|automatic
- trigger_source: console|api|policy_engine
If automatic, actor_principal_type=service and actor_principal_id="policy-engine"?

Rafael Mendes: + include “runbook_url” optional? lol
(jk but a link would be nice)

Hannah Rivera: +1 on trigger_source. Console wants to show “Auto-aborted by gates” vs “Aborted by user”.

Kevin Osei: For SIEM, resource keys matter. Suggest we standardize:
- resource_type: "dedicated_pool_rollout_plan"
- resource_id: rollout_plan_id
And include related:
- related_resource_types: dedicated_pool, account
- related_resource_ids

Kaitlyn Nguyen: + account_id is mandatory. We need tenant boundary in every event.

Benji Okafor: ok. Adding:
- account_id
- dedicated_pool_id
- region (optional?)

Ethan Park: Region is tricky since plan spans pool which may be multi-region. Maybe:
- regions_affected: comma-separated list
Or leave out of audit, keep in rollout plan record.

Jordan Lee: I’d keep:
- regions_affected
as a string like "iad, fra".

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Works as long as we’re not putting anything customer-identifying beyond their account_id (which is already the tenant id).

Jordan Lee: Another question: include old/new config diff? e.g. model version old->new.

Kevin Osei: I’d avoid a full diff. But include identifiers:
- change_kind: model_version|runtime_build|routing_policy|config
- change_ref_old
- change_ref_new
No big blobs.

Benji Okafor: That maps well to our plan model anyway.

Hannah Rivera: Also for hold/release hold, stage doesn’t change but state does. Should audit include stage_id still? so UI can show “held at 10%”.

Jordan Lee: yes:
- current_stage_id
- current_stage_name

Rafael Mendes: For abort, do we capture “rollback target stage”? We’ve talked about reverting to previous stable.

Ethan Park: For v1 abort semantics: abort returns routing to baseline (stage 0 / stable partition), and marks rollout aborted.
So include:
- abort_target_stage_id (probably "stable")

Jordan Lee: ok.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One more thing: “reason” is user-provided text. Sanitize/limit length.
And make it required for override_type != none.

Benji Okafor: ✅ I’ll enforce max 280 chars and strip newlines.

Jordan Lee: Drafting final field list. Pasting as pseudo:

Jordan Lee: ```
required:
- event_id
- event_type
- ts
- account_id
- actor_principal_id
- actor_principal_type (user|service)
- auth_method
- request_id
- resource_type (dedicated_pool_rollout_plan)
- resource_id (rollout_plan_id)
- dedicated_pool_id
- rollout_state_before
- rollout_state_after

optional (but strongly recommended):
- on_behalf_of_principal_id
- on_behalf_of_principal_type
- idempotency_key
- plan_version_before
- plan_version_after
- trigger (manual|automatic)
- trigger_source (console|api|policy_engine)
- reason
- override_type
- from_stage_id
- to_stage_id
- current_stage_id
- stage_representation (percent|replica_partition)
- from_allocation
- to_allocation
- abort_target_stage_id
- change_kind
- change_ref_old
- change_ref_new
- gate_evaluation_id
- gate_summary
- regions_affected
```

Kevin Osei: Looks good. For gate_summary keep it a string, e.g. "FAIL latency_p99 i.e. 920ms > 750ms (15m window)".

Rafael Mendes: + include "UNKNOWN" when missing metrics. That matters.

Jordan Lee: Right, and if missing metrics caused auto-hold (if we implement) then event should say it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Where will events be emitted precisely?
Need to confirm: CP emits after DB write succeeds. Not before.

Benji Okafor: Plan is:
- promote endpoint validates, evaluates gate preconditions, then writes state transition (within tx)
- after commit, emit audit event
For auto-abort, policy engine calls CP "Abort" API (service identity), CP writes + emits.

Ethan Park: Great. That gives exactly-once *ish* semantics; in practice may need dedupe in exporter.
We should include event_id uuid generated at persistence time.

Kevin Osei: exporter dedupes on event_id today, so yes please.

Hannah Rivera: Any concern about users seeing internal actor_principal_id like "policy-engine"? Console audit view will need a friendly label.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s fine as long as it’s consistent and not secret. Also: don’t leak internal emails in customer-facing UI.

Jordan Lee: ok, we’ll map service ids to friendly names in Console.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update PR with this schema + add TODO for any fields we can’t populate in v1 (regions_affected maybe).

Priya Natarajan: jumping in late. This looks reasonable. Please make sure we don’t end up with 3 similar event_types (aborted vs override_force_abort). Prefer one event_type + override_type if possible.

Jordan Lee: +1, simplify:
- dedicated_pool_rollout.aborted
and include override_type=force_abort when applicable.
Same for promote.

Kevin Osei: Works. Event types should be stable + low cardinality.

Rafael Mendes: Also: add a note in runbook: if you force-promote, include incident link in reason.

Benji Okafor: will do.

Jordan Lee: Closing this thread: emitting from control plane on state transitions, schema as above, one event_type per action + override_type for overrides. I’ll post final in Confluence + link from the PR.

title: #eng-platform: 1738785501
preview:
Jordan Lee: We need to lock audit event schema for promote/abort (and overrides). Trying to get ahead of Console + SIEM export.

Jordan Lee: Fields we *think* we need: actor, reason, old/new stage, override flags, and where event emitted (control plane vs policy engine).

Benji Okafor: +1. I’m implementing promote/abort endpoints this week. Need to know exact payload so I don’t have to churn migrations.

Ethan Park: For Dedicated rollouts, we should have a single source of truth: control plane state machine. Emit audit events from there, not from routing/policy engine.

Hannah Rivera: Console wants to show the audit trail row for: promote, abort, hold, force-promote. Also “gate failed -> auto-abort” should show as system action.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Security note: actor must capture both the authenticated principal and the “effective actor” if impersonation / support tools used.

Kevin Osei: + retention/export: we need stable event_type strings + a consistent “resource” tuple so the export pipeline can route it.

Jordan Lee: Ok, proposal (v0). Event types:
- dedicated_pool_rollout.plan_created
- dedicated_pool_rollout.plan_updated
- dedicated_pool_rollout.stage_promoted
- dedicated_pool_rollout.aborted
- dedicated_pool_rollout.held
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_force_promote
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_force_abort (do we need? or just aborted w/ override)
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_release_hold

Benji Okafor: For the schema, can we keep it mostly consistent with existing audit envelope?
Like:
- event_id
- event_type
- ts
- actor (id, type)
- actor_display
- request_id / trace_id
- resource_type + resource_id

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Please don’t rely on actor_display for anything security-related. Nice-to-have only.
Need:
- actor_principal_id
- actor_principal_type (user/service)
- actor_org_id (if internal)
- on_behalf_of_principal_id (optional)
- auth_method (api_key, session, service_token)

Ethan Park: +1. Also include rbac_role / permission evaluated? Maybe too much.
At least include: authorization_decision_id? That’s overkill.

Jordan Lee: For promote/abort specific fields:
- rollout_plan_id
- dedicated_pool_id
- change_id (the thing we’re rolling out, e.g. model_version/runtime_build/policy)
- from_stage_id / to_stage_id
- from_stage_name / to_stage_name
- from_percent / to_percent (if percentage stages)
- from_partition / to_partition (if replica partition stages)
- rollout_state_before / rollout_state_after
- reason (freeform, required for overrides)
- override_type (none|force_promote|force_abort|hold|release_hold)

Hannah Rivera: Console also asked for “gate evidence” pointer. Not the full metrics payload, but a reference.
Maybe:
- gate_evaluation_id
- gate_summary (pass/fail/unknown + which gate tripped)

Rafael Mendes: Oncall POV: for auto-aborts we need to know which gate and the window. Even a short summary is fine.
Don’t make us click 3 different systems during an incident.

Kevin Osei: Careful on putting raw metric values into audit logs (PII risk low, but volume/cardinality). A compact summary is okay.
Also: the export job expects event payload size under ~16KB.

Benji Okafor: Where does gate_evaluation_id live? In policy engine or telemetry?

Jordan Lee: In policy engine (gate evaluator) we generate an evaluation record id. Control plane queries it (or gets it pushed) and makes the state transition.
So audit event should be emitted at the moment control plane persists new state, with gate_evaluation_id attached.

Ethan Park: Strong preference: policy engine should not emit audit events. Otherwise we get duplicates when promote triggers eval and then CP applies transition.
We can log internal events in PE for debugging, but “audit” should be CP only.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Agree. “Audit” == authoritative, deterministic, minimal. Emit on state transition.
But please include:
- idempotency_key (if present)
- optimistic_concurrency_version (or etag) used
So we can reconcile double-clicks / retries.

Benji Okafor: I can include:
- idempotency_key
- request_id
- plan_version_before / plan_version_after (int)

Jordan Lee: What about “who/what triggered the change” for auto events?
I propose:
- trigger: manual|automatic
- trigger_source: console|api|policy_engine
If automatic, actor_principal_type=service and actor_principal_id="policy-engine"?

Rafael Mendes: + include “runbook_url” optional? lol
(jk but a link would be nice)

Hannah Rivera: +1 on trigger_source. Console wants to show “Auto-aborted by gates” vs “Aborted by user”.

Kevin Osei: For SIEM, resource keys matter. Suggest we standardize:
- resource_type: "dedicated_pool_rollout_plan"
- resource_id: rollout_plan_id
And include related:
- related_resource_types: dedicated_pool, account
- related_resource_ids

Kaitlyn Nguyen: + account_id is mandatory. We need tenant boundary in every event.

Benji Okafor: ok. Adding:
- account_id
- dedicated_pool_id
- region (optional?)

Ethan Park: Region is tricky since plan spans pool which may be multi-region. Maybe:
- regions_affected: comma-separated list
Or leave out of audit, keep in rollout plan record.

Jordan Lee: I’d keep:
- regions_affected
as a string like "iad, fra".

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Works as long as we’re not putting anything customer-identifying beyond their account_id (which is already the tenant id).

Jordan Lee: Another question: include old/new config diff? e.g. model version old->new.

Kevin Osei: I’d avoid a full diff. But include identifiers:
- change_kind: model_version|runtime_build|routing_policy|config
- change_ref_old
- change_ref_new
No big blobs.

Benji Okafor: That maps well to our plan model anyway.

Hannah Rivera: Also for hold/release hold, stage doesn’t change but state does. Should audit include stage_id still? so UI can show “held at 10%”.

Jordan Lee: yes:
- current_stage_id
- current_stage_name

Rafael Mendes: For abort, do we capture “rollback target stage”? We’ve talked about reverting to previous stable.

Ethan Park: For v1 abort semantics: abort returns routing to baseline (stage 0 / stable partition), and marks rollout aborted.
So include:
- abort_target_stage_id (probably "stable")

Jordan Lee: ok.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One more thing: “reason” is user-provided text. Sanitize/limit length.
And make it required for override_type != none.

Benji Okafor: ✅ I’ll enforce max 280 chars and strip newlines.

Jordan Lee: Drafting final field list. Pasting as pseudo:

Jordan Lee: ```
required:
- event_id
- event_type
- ts
- account_id
- actor_principal_id
- actor_principal_type (user|service)
- auth_method
- request_id
- resource_type (dedicated_pool_rollout_plan)
- resource_id (rollout_plan_id)
- dedicated_pool_id
- rollout_state_before
- rollout_state_after

optional (but strongly recommended):
- on_behalf_of_principal_id
- on_behalf_of_principal_type
- idempotency_key
- plan_version_before
- plan_version_after
- trigger (manual|automatic)
- trigger_source (console|api|policy_engine)
- reason
- override_type
- from_stage_id
- to_stage_id
- current_stage_id
- stage_representation (percent|replica_partition)
- from_allocation
- to_allocation
- abort_target_stage_id
- change_kind
- change_ref_old
- change_ref_new
- gate_evaluation_id
- gate_summary
- regions_affected
```

Kevin Osei: Looks good. For gate_summary keep it a string, e.g. "FAIL latency_p99 i.e. 920ms > 750ms (15m window)".

Rafael Mendes: + include "UNKNOWN" when missing metrics. That matters.

Jordan Lee: Right, and if missing metrics caused auto-hold (if we implement) then event should say it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Where will events be emitted precisely?
Need to confirm: CP emits after DB write succeeds. Not before.

Benji Okafor: Plan is:
- promote endpoint validates, evaluates gate preconditions, then writes state transition (within tx)
- after commit, emit audit event
For auto-abort, policy engine calls CP "Abort" API (service identity), CP writes + emits.

Ethan Park: Great. That gives exactly-once *ish* semantics; in practice may need dedupe in exporter.
We should include event_id uuid generated at persistence time.

Kevin Osei: exporter dedupes on event_id today, so yes please.

Hannah Rivera: Any concern about users seeing internal actor_principal_id like "policy-engine"? Console audit view will need a friendly label.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s fine as long as it’s consistent and not secret. Also: don’t leak internal emails in customer-facing UI.

Jordan Lee: ok, we’ll map service ids to friendly names in Console.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update PR with this schema + add TODO for any fields we can’t populate in v1 (regions_affected maybe).

Priya Natarajan: jumping in late. This looks reasonable. Please make sure we don’t end up with 3 similar event_types (aborted vs override_force_abort). Prefer one event_type + override_type if possible.

Jordan Lee: +1, simplify:
- dedicated_pool_rollout.aborted
and include override_type=force_abort when applicable.
Same for promote.

Kevin Osei: Works. Event types should be stable + low cardinality.

Rafael Mendes: Also: add a note in runbook: if you force-promote, include incident link in reason.

Benji Okafor: will do.

Jordan Lee: Closing this thread: emitting from control plane on state transitions, schema as above, one event_type per action + override_type for overrides. I’ll post final in Confluence + link from the PR.

source.content:
Ethan Park: Question for SRE + telemetry folks: for Dedicated staged rollouts, how should gate eval behave if metrics are missing / delayed (telemetry lag, ingest outage, etc.)? We need to pick fail-open vs fail-closed before we lock the state machine.

Peter Holtz: +1. we saw “Unknown” gates during last week’s dogfood when prometheus remote_write was backpressured. Promotion logic needs a deterministic rule.

Logan Wright: Missing can mean a few things:
- no traffic (0 reqs) => some metrics legitimately absent
- traffic exists but tag mismatch / cardinality drop => stage labels not present
- ingest delay => data is there but late
- hard outage => no data at all
We should distinguish “no samples” vs “telemetry stale”.

Sean Gallagher: SRE preference: default should be fail-closed for *promotion*. If you can’t prove it’s healthy, don’t advance. But we need an escape hatch (force-promote) with audit + RBAC.

Jordan Lee: Agree on deterministic. If gate evaluation returns UNKNOWN due to missing data, treat as FAIL for the policy engine decision (block auto-promotion). But we should also surface “why unknown” so oncall isn’t guessing.

Allison Grant: +1 on surfacing reason codes. Can we standardize:
- PASS
- FAIL
- UNKNOWN_NO_DATA (no samples in window)
- UNKNOWN_STALE (data older than staleness threshold)
- UNKNOWN_ERROR (query execution error)
Then promotion logic uses a mapping.

Ethan Park: That works. We also have the “manual gates” phase (operator checked) in Phase 1. In that case, missing metrics shouldn’t hard-block manual promote, but should warn.

Rafael Mendes: careful: even for “manual”, the UI should still show the gate status as UNKNOWN and require a reason string if you override. We need paper trail for change mgmt.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: On RBAC: force-promote should be a separate permission (admin-only) vs normal “promote” (operator). And force-promote should *require* `reason` + ticket link if possible.

Jordan Lee: Mechanically in policy engine, I propose:
1) Gate evaluation returns (status, observed_value?, threshold?, window, samples, reason_code)
2) Promotion eligibility:
- if any gate FAIL => block
- if any gate UNKNOWN_* => block (same as FAIL) unless `override=true`
3) Override path is explicit action: `POST /promote?override=true` with audit event capturing reason_code(s) + freeform justification.

Peter Holtz: what about abort/rollback triggers? If metrics go missing *after* promotion to a stage, do we auto-abort? That’s different than blocking promotion.

Sean Gallagher: yeah, we shouldn’t auto-abort just because telemetry blips for 2 mins. But we also can’t keep progressing. I’d say:
- auto-promotion stops if telemetry is unknown
- auto-abort only on explicit fail signals (error rate, saturation, OOM)
- if telemetry is stale beyond X minutes while in an early stage, raise an alert + optionally auto-hold

Logan Wright: +1. We can implement staleness detection using the max timestamp in the timeseries. e.g. `now() - last_sample_ts > 3m` => STALE.
Also, we should treat “0 traffic” specially: for error-rate / latency gates, if req_count < min_samples, status should be UNKNOWN_NO_DATA (not PASS).

Allison Grant: we already have min sample size standards for SLO alerting. Can reuse: e.g. min 100 reqs over window, or min 5 req/min sustained for 10m. But Dedicated can be low volume.

Ethan Park: Dedicated low volume is real esp for some enterprise batch jobs. For those, do we allow promotion w/ low samples?

Rafael Mendes: I’d keep the rule consistent: if you don’t have enough samples, you don’t have enough confidence. But then force-promote exists. Also we can allow stage duration to be longer (wait for samples) rather than relaxing min_samples.

Jordan Lee: +1. We can add “max_wait” / “min_duration” per stage. Promotion requires:
- stage >= min_duration
- all gates PASS
If min_samples not met, gates are UNKNOWN => stage just waits.

Peter Holtz: Make sure waiting doesn’t deadlock the rollout forever. Need a “held” state or “timed out” event.

Priya Natarajan: Popping in — directionally I want fail-closed for promotion. We got dinged in the last incident retro for “blind” changes. But product needs an operational escape hatch.
Concretely:
- default behavior: UNKNOWN blocks promotion
- manual override allowed for admins with reason + audit
- UI should loudly indicate telemetry outage / staleness
- do NOT auto-rollback on UNKNOWN alone; do auto-hold and alert after some threshold

Ethan Park: Thanks @Priya Natarajan. What’s the staleness threshold? 3m? 5m?

Logan Wright: For our pipeline, p95 end-to-end latency is ~45s, p99 around 2m during load. I’d set staleness at 5m to avoid false STALE during normal backpressure.

Sean Gallagher: 5m is fine. But the eval window might be 10–15m; if you’re stale for 5m, you’re already missing half the window. The status should be UNKNOWN_STALE and block.

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a distinct alert: “Gate eval stale” should page telemetry oncall if it’s global, vs ticket if it’s pool-specific tag mismatch.

Jordan Lee: We can detect global vs pool-specific by checking a control query (no stage tag) and comparing. If base metrics exist but stage-tagged ones don’t => likely tagging / cardinality issue.

Peter Holtz: that would’ve caught SUP-28605 earlier (gate evidence empty due to tag mismatch). +100.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Re audit fields, please include:
- actor
- pool_id
- rollout_plan_id
- from_stage -> to_stage
- override=true/false
- override_reason
- gate_snapshot (list of gate statuses)
Even if we don’t store full payload, we need enough for compliance.

Ethan Park: ok summarizing proposal:
- Gate eval returns PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN_* with reason codes + samples + window.
- Promotion: fail-closed on UNKNOWN.
- Auto-abort: only on FAIL (objective fail), not on UNKNOWN.
- If UNKNOWN persists > 10m (config), transition to HELD + alert.
- Override requires admin RBAC + reason, emits audit.
I’ll thread this into the design spec + policy engine PR.

Priya Natarajan: Sounds good. Please make sure Product understands “unknown blocks promotion” so we don’t surprise anyone in beta.

Logan Wright: I’ll add to telemetry backlog: expose `gate_eval_data_freshness_seconds` and tag-mismatch detector. Also document “no traffic” vs “missing telemetry”.

Jordan Lee: I’ll update the policy engine contract to include `reason_code` and `staleness_ms`. We should keep the response stable for Console.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: once you have the exact mappings, post in #eng-sre too so oncall playbook matches.

Ethan Park: will do. closing loop after I write it up.

Leila Farouk: ok need to lock integration test plan for “sensitive actions emits audit event” (ENG-4834 / PR-22058). main question: how do we run in CI for hosted vs private fixtures without it becoming a 45min flaky suite?

Ethan Park: +1. also want to be clear on what the test asserts. event presence? or full schema validation + required fields + event_type?

Sahana Iyer: from CI side: we can support 2 lanes:
1) fast lane on every PR (unit + a couple smoke E2E)
2) full integration lane nightly / on merge queue
But need deterministic env + timeouts.

Diego Santos: parity concern: hosted/dedicated/private should emit equivalent events. I’d like the test matrix to cover at least 1 action per surface (control plane, console backend, api gateway, policy engine, private installer) in *both* hosted and private.

Leila Farouk: proposal:
- define a canonical list of test cases keyed by event_type (from taxonomy)
- each test performs the action, then polls audit sink and asserts: event_type + schema_version + actor + target + request_id/trace_id present
- schema validation: yes, but not strict on optional fields

Kaitlyn Nguyen: for credential lifecycle (API keys etc) careful: we cannot create real keys and then dump raw material in logs. tests should verify that `credential_fingerprint` exists and `credential_value` does NOT.

Amara Diallo: for gateway admin endpoints we can do a synthetic action via internal admin client. but hosted CI doesn’t have “real” admin perms unless we mint a test tenant w/ elevated role.

Chloe Martin: console backend actions: easiest is to call backend APIs directly (skip UI). do we have a stable admin token flow in test env?

Ethan Park: we can create a test tenant + bootstrap an org owner in fixtures. in hosted mode, use local stack (kind) w/ control plane + gateway + audit ingestion running. basically “hosted-like” but local.

Sean Gallagher: +1 keep it local. No tests calling real prod services in CI.
Also: audit pipeline delivery is at-least-once-ish with retries. tests need to be resilient to duplicates. assert “>= 1 matching event_id” and use correlation ids to match.

Kevin Osei: exporter side: for CI, we should bypass SIEM targets and read from the internal audit topic / HTTP collector.
Do we have a canonical “test sink” already? If not, we can add an in-memory sink or local HTTP receiver and store events in sqlite for polling.

Logan Wright: if you want coverage instrumentation later, please include `admin_action_id` in the tests. makes it easier to link expected actions to observed events.

Leila Farouk: ok, so minimal assertions:
- `schema_version` == "1.0"
- `event_type` == expected
- `event_time` present (RFC3339)
- `event_id` present
- `actor_type`, `actor_id` present
- `actor_ip` present when action happens over HTTP (not for installer)
- `tenant_id` present
- `request_id` or `trace_id` present depending on path
- redaction: verify absence of `*_secret`, `token`, raw API key

Jordan Lee: for policy engine changes, we should verify `change_summary` is capped (cardinality). not sure integration test should assert length though. maybe just presence + non-empty.

Sahana Iyer: CI mechanics question: private fixture. are we spinning the private installer in CI? that sounds heavy.

Diego Santos: private parity can be validated via the “private mode” docker compose we already use for installer PRs. It’s not full on-prem, but should exercise the same installer code path.
Maybe split:
- hosted fixture (kind) runs on PRs
- private fixture runs nightly + on installer/exporter changes (path filter)

Connor O'Brien: release gating ask from PM was “100% sensitive actions coverage” but I think for CI we can do representative coverage. for gate, we can run full suite in merge queue if needed.

Ethan Park: +1 representative on PRs. full matrix might be too much until stable.

Amara Diallo: on gateway admin actions: can we include just one: quota override update? It touches actor attribution + source IP. event_type should be `gateway.quota_override.updated` (or whatever registry final name is).

Leila Farouk: yep. action list draft:
1) RBAC: create role / update role permissions (control plane)
2) Credential: create API key + revoke API key (control plane)
3) Gateway: set quota override (gateway admin)
4) Policy: update routing policy fallback rule (policy engine)
5) Data: update retention setting (control plane)
6) Export: update audit export destination (console backend)
7) Private: installer upgrade or config apply (private installer)

Chloe Martin: for console backend “export destination” we need a stable API endpoint + sample config. also we must ensure we don’t include real customer URLs.
We can use `https://example.invalid/hec` and assert it’s hashed/redacted in event payload.

Kevin Osei: exporter config should treat destination URLs as sensitive-ish. Some customers consider endpoints sensitive. In schema we planned `export_destination_host` + `export_destination_type` and no full URL. integration test can assert full URL not present.

Sahana Iyer: polling strategy: tests should have bounded retries w/ jitter. Don’t do `sleep(10)` fixed.
Also please emit logs on failure: dump last N audit events to help debugging.

Sean Gallagher: +1 and add a strict upper bound so CI doesn’t hang. e.g. 60s total per test, 10s for env startup? Also: if audit ingestion is backlogged, tests should fail loudly with actionable message (link runbook later).

Leila Farouk: concrete approach:
- test harness provides `perform_action()` then `wait_for_audit_event(filter)`
- filter uses `admin_action_id` (preferred) else `request_id`
- if duplicates, pick the first matching event and validate fields
- also validate against JSON schema using the shared validator lib

Ethan Park: validator lib is already in PR-21984. just call `audit.Validate(event)` in tests.

CI Bot: (autogenerated) Reminder: new jobs must be added to `ci/pipelines/monorepo.yml` and include an owner + expected runtime.

Sahana Iyer: hosted fixture in kind: please limit resources. our GH runners choke if we schedule 6 services w/ huge CPU limits. Use minimal replicas.

Diego Santos: for private fixture: can we run it as docker compose and not kind? easiest for installer. Then just run a small “private control plane + audit sink” container set.

Leila Farouk: ok, so:
PR checks (required): hosted fixture, subset of tests (1-6)
Nightly / merge-queue: + private fixture (test 7) and maybe rerun full list in private mode when stable

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also for impersonation: do we need a test where actor is “admin acting on behalf of user”? that affects `actor_impersonated_*` fields.

Ethan Park: not for v1 coverage gate but would be good. maybe add a test case later. we don’t have stable impersonation in fixtures yet.

Jordan Lee: can we add a test to ensure unknown event_type is rejected? that’s more unit/lint though.

Leila Farouk: I’ll keep that out of integration suite. That belongs to registry/lint CI (ENG-4822 / PR-21991).

Sean Gallagher: last point: make sure we don’t assert exact timestamps or ordering. The audit pipeline is async.

Leila Farouk: ack. I’ll write up in ENG-4834 and link in PR description.
Action items:
- Leila: update test plan doc in ENG-4834 + implement harness (admin_action_id correlation)
- Ethan: confirm event_type strings for the 6 hosted cases (post final registry names)
- Kevin: advise on test sink endpoint + fields to redact for export config
- Sahana: add CI jobs (hosted required, private optional/nightly) + time budget

Connor O'Brien: pls also add a note for release gating: “full private fixture runs in merge queue before tagging enterprise release”.

Sahana Iyer: sounds good. once you have an estimate of runtime, ping me. if >20 mins we’ll need to chunk.

Leila Farouk: will do. I’m aiming <8 mins for hosted suite.

Diego Santos: thanks. please keep parity notes in the test cases so we can point customers to “we test this in hosted+private”.

Leila Farouk: yep, will add a comment per test: expected parity + any known delta.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: ping me when you have the final list so I can sanity check required fields per event_type.

Kevin Osei: :thumbsup: I’ll drop a snippet for the test sink config.

Sahana Iyer: :thumbsup:

Talia Benson: quick q we need to land today: feature flag scope for beta enablement (Dedicated staged rollouts). do we gate by account? pool? region? worried about multi-region Dedicated pools + partial exposure.

Ethan Park: control plane side easiest is account_id allowlist (new `dedicated_rollouts_beta_accounts`), but that means *all* pools under account get rollout plan API / state machine behavior.

Benji Okafor: +1. pool-level is a bit more work but doable (table keyed by pool_id). region-level would be… a mess unless we plumb it everywhere.

Mina Haddad: routing/data plane: region-level scares me. if only us-east has stage partitions and eu-west doesn’t, you can end up with cross-region failover shifting traffic into a non-staged region. then stage metrics look “fine” but you’re not actually testing the new runtime/model.

Ravi Menon: yeah, and our routing policy for Dedicated already does “prefer local region, fallback to other region if impaired”. if flags differ per region, we get non-deterministic behavior during impairment.

Jordan Lee: policy engine / gates also assumes a single truth per pool for stage state. if region A thinks stage=10% and region B thinks stage=0%, audits + idempotency get weird fast.

Talia Benson: ok so region-level = no. between account vs pool, what are the tradeoffs?

Vanessa Ortiz: infra angle: pool-level is safer for fleet ops. we’ll have internal pools for dogfood and we don’t want to accidentally enable staged rollouts on a customer pool just because the account got allowlisted for one pilot.

Ethan Park: agreed. also, some beta customers have multiple reservations (prod + sandbox). we might only want sandbox.

Benji Okafor: pool-level allowlist can live alongside account-level. like evaluation order: if pool_id explicitly enabled -> on; else if account enabled -> on; else off. but we should pick one for beta to reduce confusion.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: security/RBAC: whichever flag we pick, it must not allow bypassing authz checks. but separate from that, pool-level allowlist has less blast radius. also makes audit story cleaner (“feature enabled for pool X by admin Y”).

Priya Natarajan: +1 on minimizing blast radius. we can always expand later. we just need something that ops can flip quickly for specific design partners.

Mina Haddad: +1. plus partial region exposure concern goes away if flag is pool-scoped AND we require “all regions for the pool” to be enabled together.

Ravi Menon: how do we enforce “all regions for the pool”? some pools are single-region, some multi-region. in routing config we already know pool -> region set.

Ethan Park: control plane can treat enablement as pool_id only, not (pool_id, region). when enabled, it applies to the pool’s config pushed to all regions. so it’s inherently all-or-nothing.

Jordan Lee: policy engine reads pool config snapshot (includes rollout plan id + current stage). if the config is replicated to regions via the normal mechanism, it should converge.

Benji Okafor: caveat: config propagation delays. if we flip the flag, region1 might see it before region2 for a few mins.

Mina Haddad: that’s the partial exposure we were worried about. during that window you can route differently per region.

Ravi Menon: we can mitigate by designing routing behavior to be “safe” during mixed states. like: if rollout plan present but stage metadata missing/unknown, treat as stage=0% (baseline) and do not allocate to canary partitions.

Jordan Lee: policy engine side we can do similar: if stage state unknown or config_version mismatch, gates return “unknown” and promotion is blocked. (fail-closed for promotion, fail-open for serving baseline)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: careful with terminology: “fail-open for serving baseline” is ok; but promotion actions must be auditable and deterministic.

Ethan Park: also for beta we can add a guard: don’t allow “running” rollouts unless all regions have acknowledged config_version >= X. we already have an ack mechanism from regional controllers.

Benji Okafor: yup, we can require `min_region_ack` before allowing create/promote endpoints. might be too heavy for MVP though.

Talia Benson: for beta, can we accept “config converge in a few mins” but ensure no traffic goes to canary until converge?

Mina Haddad: yes if routing defaults to baseline until it sees both: (1) rollout plan enabled AND (2) stage partitions exist in that region’s pool. otherwise baseline.

Ravi Menon: we should log a metric when rollout enabled but partitions not present so we can see propagation lag.

Vanessa Ortiz: and we should ensure partitions are created *before* routing starts splitting. otherwise we repeat the earlier “canary caused 429s” incident when effective capacity drops.

Ethan Park: makes sense. control plane can do it in sequence: enable flag -> provisioning creates canary slice -> once slice ready -> set stage=1%.

Jordan Lee: that’s basically: “enabled” state != “running rollout”. first step is just allow the APIs, but actual plan execution should be explicit.

Talia Benson: ok, so decision proposal:
- Feature flag scope: pool-level allowlist for beta
- Applies to pool across all regions (no region overrides)
- Serving behavior during mixed config: baseline only, promotion blocked until stage data present
- Ops workflow: internal support flips allowlist per pool_id

Priya Natarajan: +1. can we name the flag something stable? and document how it’s evaluated.

Benji Okafor: implementation idea:
- `dedicated_staged_rollouts_enabled_pools` (set of pool_ids) in config service
- optional `dedicated_staged_rollouts_enabled_accounts` for later
- API middleware checks pool_id in allowlist; otherwise 404 or “feature not enabled”

Kaitlyn Nguyen: prefer explicit 403 with a clear error code for internal callers; for customer-facing APIs, we can do 404 to avoid feature enumeration. but Console will need a consistent error.

Ethan Park: Console backend can translate. for now internal admin tooling uses 403.

DeployBot: PR build #84219 succeeded for branch benji/rollout-allowlist

Mina Haddad: one more: what about a pool that spans us-east + us-west. if we enable it, but us-west region is mid-maintenance, do we block the rollout entirely?

Jordan Lee: gate eval should incorporate region health signal. but for enablement flag, I’d still allow it; rollout creation can proceed, but stage promotion should fail gates if a region is impaired / missing metrics.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. don’t tie enablement to fleet health; tie promotion eligibility to gates.

Ravi Menon: routing fallback: if a region is impaired mid-rollout, traffic might shift. we should note that stage split should be evaluated at the pool level (aggregate across regions) otherwise you can get 1% in one region but 30% overall after failover.

Mina Haddad: agreed. for beta we can say: stage percentage is applied per-pool across active regions based on routing weights, not per-region hard allocation. (unless the stage type is replica-based partitioning, then it’s more explicit.)

Talia Benson: i’ll capture this in the beta enablement doc. action items:
- Benji/Ethan: implement pool allowlist + API behavior (403/404) and note no region-level overrides
- Mina/Ravi: define baseline-only behavior during mixed config + metric for “enabled but partitions missing”
- Kaitlyn: confirm error code guidance for Console

Priya Natarajan: sounds good. please post a short decision in #eng-platform once merged so support + SRE know the rule: enablement is per-pool, all-regions.

Ethan Park: will do.

Benji Okafor: 👍 i’ll update ENG-6011 comment as well.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: as long as we keep region overrides out of v1.

Talia Benson: thanks all.

source.messages:
Benji Okafor: heads up: seeing weird behavior in canary rollout controller in staging. it progressed from 10%->50% even though gate eval returned FAIL. digging now but flagging bc this is scary for safe apply

Benji Okafor: symptoms: controller posts "advanced to next step" while `gate_status=FAIL` for error-rate rule. looks like we’re treating timeout as pass? :thinking_face:

SentryBot: Alert in staging: `optimization-canary-controller` error rate elevated
- service: control-plane
- env: staging
- fingerprint: CanaryControllerAdvance
- message: "advanced rollout despite failing gate"
- link: https://sentry.redwood.ai/organizations/redwood/issues/1287711

Ethan Park: can you paste the rollout record + controller logs? want to confirm this isn’t just UI lag

Benji Okafor: yep. controller logs from `oc-canary-controller-7b9d7c4f9d-w8kz2` (staging)
```
2025-02-03T18:21:39.114Z INFO  reconcile rollout_id=rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0 stage=CANARY step=1 pct=10 gate=PASS action=advance
2025-02-03T18:23:41.004Z INFO  reconcile rollout_id=rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0 stage=CANARY step=2 pct=50 gate=FAIL gate_reason="5xx_rate 0.92% > 0.50%" action=advance
2025-02-03T18:23:41.005Z WARN  reconcile rollout_id=rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0 note="advancing due to canary window elapsed" elapsed=300000ms window=300000ms
```

Benji Okafor: rollout object snapshot (from `GET /internal/optimization/rollouts/rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0`)
```
status:
  stage: CANARY
  step: 2
  percent: 50
  gate:
    status: FAIL
    evaluated_at: 1738621421
    rules:
      - name: error_rate_5xx
        status: FAIL
        value: 0.0092
        threshold: 0.005
  last_transition: "advanced"
```

Jordan Lee: lol that WARN line is… concerning. we shouldn’t advance on elapsed if gate is FAIL. should be pause + require manual override

Sean Gallagher: +1. does this exist in prod behind FF? if yes, we should immediately disable auto-advance until fixed

Ethan Park: agree. canary controller should only advance on (gate PASS) OR (gate UNAVAILABLE + allowlist) but never FAIL. elapsed should trigger evaluation / re-evaluation, not advancement

Michael Nguyen: could be the time math bug from last week? i remember we had `window_ms` vs `window_s` confusion in an earlier branch

Benji Okafor: i think you’re onto it. code path is `maybeAdvance()`
- it calculates `elapsed := now - stepStartedAt`
- compares against `canaryWindowMs`
- if elapsed >= window -> advance

Benji Okafor: that logic was meant for the *PASS* case to avoid getting stuck when metrics stop updating (gate UNKNOWN), not for FAIL. looks like i accidentally put it outside the switch

Chloe Martin: does UI show anything? bc customers will freak if they see FAIL but it still progresses

Allison Grant: from metrics side: gate publisher is definitely emitting FAIL correctly. i checked `gate_result{rollout_id="...",rule="error_rate_5xx"}` flips FAIL at 18:23 and stays FAIL until rollback

Benji Okafor: repro steps (staging):
1) create rollout with canary window = 5m, step percents [10, 50, 100]
2) inject 5xx via `perf-canary` toggles for ~3m around step 2
3) wait 5m from step start
=> controller advances even w/ FAIL

Ethan Park: ok so not a race w/ stale gate. it’s plain controller logic

Connor O'Brien: what’s the blast radius if this ships? any chance this is already in the release train artifact?

Benji Okafor: this is in `main` but feature-flagged for staging + internal beta. not enabled in prod tenant paths (yet). but yes it’s in the branch we were planning to cut this week

Jordan Lee: we need hotfix + add regression test. also would like policy: FAIL => pause, regardless of elapsed, unless an explicit `override_token` is present

Sean Gallagher: for now can we also flip staging FF off? i don’t want the controller to surprise-advance and confuse gating dashboards

Ethan Park: +1. Benji can you coordinate w/ oncall to disable `opt_rollout_auto_advance` in staging? i’ll ping Sean separately but do it if you have perms

Benji Okafor: i can do it. will update once flipped

Benji Okafor: proposed fix:
- move timeout/elapsed logic into `GateStatusUnknown` branch only
- add explicit guard: if gate FAIL => action=PAUSE
- log action reason = "gate_failed" vs "window_elapsed_no_data"

Michael Nguyen: + tests for the state machine: FAIL should never transition to next step. also check that `evaluated_at` updates don’t get ignored

Benji Okafor: yep. i’ll add unit test around `ComputeNextAction()` + integration test harness in controller package

Chloe Martin: pls also ensure API response doesn’t say "advanced" if we only *scheduled* an eval. our UI uses that field

Benji Okafor: good call. currently we set `last_transition` anytime the controller writes status, even on no-op. that’s another bug. i’ll split `last_write_at` vs `last_transition`

Jordan Lee: careful w/ schema migration timing though. hotfix should be minimal if we want it in quickly

Ethan Park: +1. keep schema out of hotfix. we can do the last_transition cleanup as follow-up ticket

Benji Okafor: ok, hotfix will be only progression logic + test + better log message. UI metadata later

Benji Okafor: opened PR: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/20016
- "Fix canary controller advancing on failing gates"
- adds regression test `TestFailingGateNeverAdvances`
- restricts elapsed advancement to UNKNOWN only

CI Bot: Build started for PR #20016 (redwood)
- unit-tests
- integration-tests
- lint

Sean Gallagher: staging FF turned off yet?

Benji Okafor: yep, toggled `opt_rollout_auto_advance=false` in staging at 18:44Z. controller now pauses at step 2 w/ FAIL (confirmed)

Allison Grant: confirmed on dashboard: rollout annotation shows "paused" and gate FAIL stays pinned. no further percent changes

Ethan Park: reviewing PR now

Jordan Lee: left a comment: can we also ensure we *don’t* advance when gate is FAIL even if gate payload is missing rules (empty)? might happen if gate service partial outage

Benji Okafor: good point. i changed default from "empty rules => pass" to "empty rules => unknown" unless `gate_status=PASS` explicitly. pushed commit

CI Bot: Build passed for PR #20016
- unit-tests: PASS
- integration-tests: PASS

Connor O'Brien: if we merge in next hour, i can include in tomorrow’s control-plane patch cut. pls tag me when merged

Ethan Park: approved. merging now

Benji Okafor: merged. thanks all. next steps i’ll file:
- follow-up ticket to fix `last_transition` semantics so UI isn’t misleading
- add canary controller invariant metric: `rollout_advanced_with_gate_fail` should be 0 (alert in staging/internal)

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: pls add that invariant metric. would’ve caught this sooner

Chloe Martin: +1. ping me when you have the `last_transition` change; UI team can adjust once API is ready

Benji Okafor: will do
