```
2025-02-03T18:21:39.114Z INFO  reconcile rollout_id=rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0 stage=CANARY step=1 pct=10 gate=PASS action=advance
2025-02-03T18:23:41.004Z INFO  reconcile rollout_id=rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0 stage=CANARY step=2 pct=50 gate=FAIL gate_reason="5xx_rate 0.92% > 0.50%" action=advance
2025-02-03T18:23:41.005Z WARN  reconcile rollout_id=rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0 note="advancing due to canary window elapsed" elapsed=300000ms window=300000ms
```

Benji Okafor: rollout object snapshot (from `GET /internal/optimization/rollouts/rollout_01JKTJ9Z2F1G4R1J7QG0`)
```
status:
  stage: CANARY
  step: 2
  percent: 50
  gate:
    status: FAIL
    evaluated_at: 1738621421
    rules:
      - name: error_rate_5xx
        status: FAIL
        value: 0.0092
        threshold: 0.005
  last_transition: "advanced"
```

Jordan Lee: lol that WARN line is… concerning. we shouldn’t advance on elapsed if gate is FAIL. should be pause + require manual override

Sean Gallagher: +1. does this exist in prod behind FF? if yes, we should immediately disable auto-advance until fixed

Ethan Park: agree. canary controller should only advance on (gate PASS) OR (gate UNAVAILABLE + allowlist) but never FAIL. elapsed should trigger evaluation / re-evaluation, not advancement

Michael Nguyen: could be the time math bug from last week? i remember we had `window_ms` vs `window_s` confusion in an earlier branch

Benji Okafor: i think you’re onto it. code path is `maybeAdvance()`
- it calculates `elapsed := now - stepStartedAt`
- compares against `canaryWindowMs`
- if elapsed >= window -> advance

Benji Okafor: that logic was meant for the *PASS* case to avoid getting stuck when metrics stop updating (gate UNKNOWN), not for FAIL. looks like i accidentally put it outside the switch

Chloe Martin: does UI show anything? bc customers will freak if they see FAIL but it still progresses

Allison Grant: from metrics side: gate publisher is definitely emitting FAIL correctly. i checked `gate_result{rollout_id="...",rule="error_rate_5xx"}` flips FAIL at 18:23 and stays FAIL until rollback

Benji Okafor: repro steps (staging):
1) create rollout with canary window = 5m, step percents [10, 50, 100]
2) inject 5xx via `perf-canary` toggles for ~3m around step 2
3) wait 5m from step start
=> controller advances even w/ FAIL

Ethan Park: ok so not a race w/ stale gate. it’s plain controller logic

Connor O'Brien: what’s the blast radius if this ships? any chance this is already in the release train artifact?

Benji Okafor: this is in `main` but feature-flagged for staging + internal beta. not enabled in prod tenant paths (yet). but yes it’s in the branch we were planning to cut this week

Jordan Lee: we need hotfix + add regression test. also would like policy: FAIL => pause, regardless of elapsed, unless an explicit `override_token` is present

Sean Gallagher: for now can we also flip staging FF off? i don’t want the controller to surprise-advance and confuse gating dashboards

Ethan Park: +1. Benji can you coordinate w/ oncall to disable `opt_rollout_auto_advance` in staging? i’ll ping Sean separately but do it if you have perms

Benji Okafor: i can do it. will update once flipped

Benji Okafor: proposed fix:
- move timeout/elapsed logic into `GateStatusUnknown` branch only
- add explicit guard: if gate FAIL => action=PAUSE
- log action reason = "gate_failed" vs "window_elapsed_no_data"

Michael Nguyen: + tests for the state machine: FAIL should never transition to next step. also check that `evaluated_at` updates don’t get ignored

Benji Okafor: yep. i’ll add unit test around `ComputeNextAction()` + integration test harness in controller package

Chloe Martin: pls also ensure API response doesn’t say "advanced" if we only *scheduled* an eval. our UI uses that field

Benji Okafor: good call. currently we set `last_transition` anytime the controller writes status, even on no-op. that’s another bug. i’ll split `last_write_at` vs `last_transition`

Jordan Lee: careful w/ schema migration timing though. hotfix should be minimal if we want it in quickly

Ethan Park: +1. keep schema out of hotfix. we can do the last_transition cleanup as follow-up ticket

Benji Okafor: ok, hotfix will be only progression logic + test + better log message. UI metadata later

Benji Okafor: opened PR: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/20016
- "Fix canary controller advancing on failing gates"
- adds regression test `TestFailingGateNeverAdvances`
- restricts elapsed advancement to UNKNOWN only

CI Bot: Build started for PR #20016 (redwood)
- unit-tests
- integration-tests
- lint

Sean Gallagher: staging FF turned off yet?

Benji Okafor: yep, toggled `opt_rollout_auto_advance=false` in staging at 18:44Z. controller now pauses at step 2 w/ FAIL (confirmed)

Allison Grant: confirmed on dashboard: rollout annotation shows "paused" and gate FAIL stays pinned. no further percent changes

Ethan Park: reviewing PR now

Jordan Lee: left a comment: can we also ensure we *don’t* advance when gate is FAIL even if gate payload is missing rules (empty)? might happen if gate service partial outage

Benji Okafor: good point. i changed default from "empty rules => pass" to "empty rules => unknown" unless `gate_status=PASS` explicitly. pushed commit

CI Bot: Build passed for PR #20016
- unit-tests: PASS
- integration-tests: PASS

Connor O'Brien: if we merge in next hour, i can include in tomorrow’s control-plane patch cut. pls tag me when merged

Ethan Park: approved. merging now

Benji Okafor: merged. thanks all. next steps i’ll file:
- follow-up ticket to fix `last_transition` semantics so UI isn’t misleading
- add canary controller invariant metric: `rollout_advanced_with_gate_fail` should be 0 (alert in staging/internal)

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: pls add that invariant metric. would’ve caught this sooner

Chloe Martin: +1. ping me when you have the `last_transition` change; UI team can adjust once API is ready

Benji Okafor: will do

source.content:
Jordan Lee: Heads up — seeing a bug w/ overlapping route experiment + fallback policy. Experiment assignment is happening (variant=B) but final routed model is getting overwritten by fallback policy. Net effect: traffic split looks skewed and results are basically measuring the fallback path.

Jordan Lee: Example from staging:
```
route=/v1/chat/completions (route_id=chat-prod)
experiment_id=exp_1GmZ... variant_id=B
variant target model = llama-3.1-70b-instruct
served model = llama-3.1-70b-instruct-fallback
reason=policy_fallback_applied
```

Jordan Lee: Anyone else seeing this? We got a beta account pinging that “variant headers say B but behavior looks like control.”

Mina Haddad: yep, I can repro in router logs. The request context has `experiment_id/variant_id` set correctly, but the downstream `selected_model_id` changes after fallback evaluation.

Mina Haddad: grabbed a trace:
- span `policy.evaluate_experiment` -> variant B
- span `policy.evaluate_fallback` -> selects fallback target based on latency SLO
- final `route.target_model` = fallback model

Ravi Menon: That sounds like ordering bug in routing pipeline. We’re treating experiment selection as just another “candidate” but then we run fallback as a second pass and it replaces candidate unconditionally.

Ravi Menon: What’s the expected precedence? I thought we wanted experiments to pin the variant unless the variant’s own model is unhealthy/unavailable.

Jada Williams: expected precedence (from earlier rollouts alignment):
1) eligibility -> experiment bucketing
2) pick variant target
3) apply fallback *only if* variant target fails health/capacity checks, not as a generic policy override

Jada Williams: right now fallback is acting like “always evaluate and pick best” which defeats the point of A/B.

Ethan Park: impact-wise: is this only when the route has an explicit fallback policy configured? (vs default region fallback)

Jordan Lee: only when route has explicit fallback policy in policy engine config. Default region failover doesn’t seem to override.

Vikram Singh: +1, we also see it on routes with “fallback on p99 regression” settings. It’s picking the fallback target because fallback policy evaluates as “true” even when primary is healthy.

Elena Popov: I dug into code path. We do:
- `selectTarget()` (includes experiment)
- then `applyFallbackPolicy()` which can swap `targetModel`

Elena Popov: For experiments, we should treat selected variant target as “pinned” and only allow fallback on hard failure (5xx / no capacity / model not compatible). Right now fallback uses soft signals and overrides.

Elena Popov: Mitigation right now (for beta): disable fallback policy on routes where they run experiments OR set fallback mode to `only_on_error=true` (if they’re using SLO-based fallback it’ll keep overriding).

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: please don’t ship something that can silently route away from experiment targets without a metric. We should add a counter like `experiment_variant_overridden_total{reason}` or at least log clearly.

Mina Haddad: +1. Also explains why attribution looks “correct” but the actual served model differs.

Benji Okafor: can Console block starting experiments on routes with non-error-based fallbacks until fix? even a warning banner would help stop folks self-footgunning.

Jordan Lee: yes please. Even a “Known issue: fallback may override variants” warning until we roll patch.

Elena Popov: I have a fix already mostly aligned with the earlier router change: apply experiment selection before fallback and also make fallback conditional on `variant_pinned=true`.

Elena Popov: Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood/pr-28457 (router apply route experiment before fallback) — I’m adding an extra guard for the pinned case + test.

Ravi Menon: Can you add a unit/integration test that asserts:
- assigned variant != control
- fallback policy evaluates “true” on soft condition
- final served model remains variant target

Ravi Menon: and separate test for hard failure -> fallback should trigger.

Elena Popov: yep. Will add both. Also going to update debug endpoint output to show `pre_fallback_model` vs `final_model` so Support can see what happened.

Ethan Park: what’s rollout plan? we should gate behind the route experiments feature flag and then flip for beta allowlist first.

Jada Williams: careful: even outside experiments, changing fallback ordering can change behavior. Keep it scoped: only alter behavior when experiment context is present.

Elena Popov: agreed. Implementation plan:
- if no experiment assignment: keep current fallback evaluation order
- if experiment assigned: treat variant selection as pinned; fallback only for hard failure reasons

Sean Gallagher: define “hard failure reasons” explicitly and log them. Otherwise we’ll get pager noise when fallback doesn’t kick in.

Mina Haddad: Hard failure list we use elsewhere:
- model_unavailable
- no_capacity_after_timeout
- incompatible_request (e.g., context length)
- upstream_5xx

Mina Haddad: We should *not* use p99-based or “cost saver” fallbacks for experiment pinned selection.

Jordan Lee: +1, that matches what PM told beta customers.

DeployBot: buildkite: PR #28457 — tests running (pipeline redwood/router) <https://buildkite.com/redwood/router/builds/129884>

Benji Okafor: I’ll add a Console-side validation note today:
- if route has fallback policy with mode != `on_error`, show warning + require explicit confirmation to start experiment

Benji Okafor: also adding an internal doc snippet for CS: “If variant headers show B but behavior matches control, check route fallback policy.”

Elena Popov: PR updated w/ tests + metric:
- `experiment_variant_override_total{reason}` increments when we *do* fallback from a pinned variant
- and a log line when fallback *would have* applied but was suppressed due to pin

Ravi Menon: can you also tag the metric with `route_id` only (no experiment_id) to avoid cardinality blowups? experiment id belongs in traces/logs.

Elena Popov: done. metric tags: `route_id`, `reason`, `region`

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: once it’s merged, I want it in staging for 24h and then prod for beta accounts. No full GA change yet.

Jordan Lee: Sounds good. For immediate mitigation I’m telling beta: pause experiments on routes w/ SLO-based fallback or switch fallback to on-error only.

Ethan Park: please drop that in the beta channel too (and link PR) so Marissa/Dylan aren’t surprised by skewed dashboards.

Jordan Lee: will do.

DeployBot: buildkite: PR #28457 — passed (main) and ready to merge <https://buildkite.com/redwood/router/builds/129884>

Elena Popov: merged. Will cut a router deploy behind feature flag in ~30m.

Sean Gallagher: thanks. Add a note to the oncall handoff that this was the suspected root cause of “experiment skew + fallback route” and point to metric + logs.

Jordan Lee: ack, will add and monitor dashboards after deploy.

source.messages:
Ethan Park: trying to lock down rollout plan state machine today so we can finish API shape + migrations. current proposal: `DRAFT -> RUNNING -> COMPLETED` and `RUNNING <-> HELD`, `RUNNING|HELD -> ABORTED`. terminal = COMPLETED/ABORTED. anything missing?

Benji Okafor: +1. question: do we need an intermediate `ABORTING` or `PROMOTING` state for UI? or just model actions as events and keep state simple.

Jordan Lee: strongly prefer simple state, and represent “in progress” via action_id / audit event status. promote/abort must be idempotent anyway so having `PROMOTING` can create weird stuck states when clients retry.

Chloe Martin: from console perspective we do need to show “promotion in progress” sometimes (user clicks promote, spinner, etc). but that can be client-side / action endpoint returns immediately. if we need server-side progress, we can expose `last_action` fields without new top-level state.

Priya Natarajan: agree with Jordan—avoid extra steady states. we should keep state machine as the “source of truth” and treat actions as events that mutate state atomically.

Rafael Mendes: SRE angle: having `ABORTING` makes oncall think something is still changing when it might already have reverted traffic. for v1 we want deterministic semantics: abort = stage routing reverts to baseline (previous stable) and plan becomes terminal.

Ethan Park: ok, no ABORTING/PROMOTING state.

Ethan Park: next: transitions + constraints. DRAFT should be editable. once RUNNING, edits restricted? (e.g., can’t change stages, only description/notes?)

Benji Okafor: PR-28411 assumed: DRAFT fully editable. RUNNING: only allow `hold`, `abort`, `promote`. stage definitions immutable once running to keep auditability + prevent “move the goalposts” during gate evaluation.

Jordan Lee: +1 immutable once RUNNING. if someone needs different stages, they create a new plan revision (new rollout_plan_id) and start over. policy engine caching will be simpler too.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC needs to align with those actions. if RUNNING can’t be edited, we reduce the surface for “silent changes”. also easier to reason about what needs audit events (create/update vs promote/abort/hold).

Ethan Park: good.

Ethan Park: about HELD: should HELD be a state or just a flag? earlier thread said we need explicit “freeze/hold” state.

Jordan Lee: HELD as explicit state is fine. semantics: routing stays at current_stage, but “auto” evaluation/promotion is disabled, and manual promote is disallowed unless we explicitly allow it.

Chloe Martin: console wants: HELD shows “resume” CTA and explanation. also we want “hold” to stop auto-abort if gate signals are flapping? (at least stops promotion). but abort should still be available.

Rafael Mendes: hold shouldn’t mask gate failures. if a gate is failing hard, we still want the page/ticket. hold is more for “pause while investigating” not “ignore signals”.

Priya Natarajan: propose: gate evaluation continues and evidence keeps updating, but promotion is blocked. abort always available.

Jordan Lee: yes. HELD blocks state transitions except `resume` (HELD->RUNNING) and `abort`.

Ethan Park: ok: HELD blocks `promote`.

Benji Okafor: minor: should we allow promote from HELD if it’s a force-promote admin action? (since force actions exist)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: if force-promote exists, it should be explicit as a different permission and should work from HELD (otherwise oncall will have to “resume” first and that creates extra audit noise). but we need guardrails.

Jordan Lee: agreed. normal promote disallowed in HELD, but `force_promote` allowed if role permits.

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Ethan Park: idempotency semantics for promote/abort: suggestion is require `Idempotency-Key` header + optimistic concurrency via `If-Match: <etag>` or `expected_version` in body.

Jordan Lee: please do both. idempotency key prevents duplicate transitions on retry. expected_version prevents concurrent promotes from two clients.

Benji Okafor: implementation sketch:
- rollout_plan has `version` int incremented on every mutation
- `POST /rollout-plans/{id}:promote` requires `idempotency_key` and `expected_version`
- if (idempotency_key already seen for this plan + action=promote) return same response (200) without changing version
- if expected_version != current, return 409
- if state not RUNNING, return 409
- if at final stage already, return 409 (or 400?)

Chloe Martin: from console, 409 is fine, we can refresh and show “already promoted” etc. but we need stable error codes.

Priya Natarajan: also ensure retries with same idempotency key don’t fail if original request succeeded but response got dropped.

Jordan Lee: careful: if two different idempotency keys hit same expected_version at the same time, only one should win. the loser gets 409 due to version mismatch.

Ethan Park: agree. and abort is similar:
- allowed from RUNNING or HELD
- terminalizes plan
- idempotency should return same terminal state if repeated

Rafael Mendes: abort should be idempotent even without idempotency key? i.e., if plan already ABORTED, calling abort again returns 200 (no-op) rather than 409. makes oncall tooling simpler.

Jordan Lee: I’m ok with abort being “safe” and returning current state (200) if already ABORTED. promote is trickier because repeated promote without key could skip multiple stages.

Benji Okafor: +1: abort can be idempotent by definition (if already aborted -> no-op). promote must be protected.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: audit logging: even if abort is no-op, don’t emit a new audit event. otherwise compliance export will show multiple aborts. return response with last_abort_action_id.

Ethan Park: good call.

Ethan Park: promote endpoint response should include: `old_stage`, `new_stage`, `state`, `version`, `action_id`. and for no-op (idempotent replay) it should return same action_id.

Jordan Lee: yep. also include `effective_at` timestamp maybe, but not required.

Chloe Martin: we’ll need `action_id` to drive the console timeline / “promotion annotation”.

Priya Natarajan: +1.

Ethan Park: final question: do we need `COMPLETED` vs “RUNNING with current_stage=last”? I prefer explicit COMPLETED.

Jordan Lee: explicit COMPLETED. state machine is clearer and avoids corner cases where “running” but no transitions left.

Rafael Mendes: explicit COMPLETED helps with oncall automation too (“ignore alerts after completion” etc) and cleanup policies.

Benji Okafor: ok. COMPLETED when stage index reaches final and gates passed + promote executed.

Ethan Park: decision summary:
- States: DRAFT, RUNNING, HELD, ABORTED, COMPLETED
- Terminal: ABORTED/COMPLETED
- DRAFT editable; stage definitions immutable after RUNNING
- HELD blocks normal promote; allow abort; allow force_promote if permissioned
- promote requires idempotency key + expected_version; abort safe idempotent if already aborted (no new audit event)
I’ll update the ticket + PR notes.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please tag me when you add RBAC checks for force_promote/hold, want to review the permission names.

Chloe Martin: and when response schema is final, can you drop it here? we’ll align console backend.

Ethan Park: will do. pushing notes now.

Ethan Park: good.

Ethan Park: about HELD: should HELD be a state or just a flag? earlier thread said we need explicit “freeze/hold” state.

Jordan Lee: HELD as explicit state is fine. semantics: routing stays at current_stage, but “auto” evaluation/promotion is disabled, and manual promote is disallowed unless we explicitly allow it.

Chloe Martin: console wants: HELD shows “resume” CTA and explanation. also we want “hold” to stop auto-abort if gate signals are flapping? (at least stops promotion). but abort should still be available.

Rafael Mendes: hold shouldn’t mask gate failures. if a gate is failing hard, we still want the page/ticket. hold is more for “pause while investigating” not “ignore signals”.

Priya Natarajan: propose: gate evaluation continues and evidence keeps updating, but promotion is blocked. abort always available.

Jordan Lee: yes. HELD blocks state transitions except `resume` (HELD->RUNNING) and `abort`.

Ethan Park: ok: HELD blocks `promote`.

Benji Okafor: minor: should we allow promote from HELD if it’s a force-promote admin action? (since force actions exist)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: if force-promote exists, it should be explicit as a different permission and should work from HELD (otherwise oncall will have to “resume” first and that creates extra audit noise). but we need guardrails.

Jordan Lee: agreed. normal promote disallowed in HELD, but `force_promote` allowed if role permits.

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Ethan Park: idempotency semantics for promote/abort: suggestion is require `Idempotency-Key` header + optimistic concurrency via `If-Match: <etag>` or `expected_version` in body.

Jordan Lee: please do both. idempotency key prevents duplicate transitions on retry. expected_version prevents concurrent promotes from two clients.

Benji Okafor: implementation sketch:
- rollout_plan has `version` int incremented on every mutation
- `POST /rollout-plans/{id}:promote` requires `idempotency_key` and `expected_version`
- if (idempotency_key already seen for this plan + action=promote) return same response (200) without changing version
- if expected_version != current, return 409
- if state not RUNNING, return 409
- if at final stage already, return 409 (or 400?)

Chloe Martin: from console, 409 is fine, we can refresh and show “already promoted” etc. but we need stable error codes.

Priya Natarajan: also ensure retries with same idempotency key don’t fail if original request succeeded but response got dropped.

Jordan Lee: careful: if two different idempotency keys hit same expected_version at the same time, only one should win. the loser gets 409 due to version mismatch.

Ethan Park: agree. and abort is similar:
- allowed from RUNNING or HELD
- terminalizes plan
- idempotency should return same terminal state if repeated

Rafael Mendes: abort should be idempotent even without idempotency key? i.e., if plan already ABORTED, calling abort again returns 200 (no-op) rather than 409. makes oncall tooling simpler.

Jordan Lee: I’m ok with abort being “safe” and returning current state (200) if already ABORTED. promote is trickier because repeated promote without key could skip multiple stages.

Benji Okafor: +1: abort can be idempotent by definition (if already aborted -> no-op). promote must be protected.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: audit logging: even if abort is no-op, don’t emit a new audit event. otherwise compliance export will show multiple aborts. return response with last_abort_action_id.

Ethan Park: good call.

Ethan Park: promote endpoint response should include: `old_stage`, `new_stage`, `state`, `version`, `action_id`. and for no-op (idempotent replay) it should return same action_id.

Jordan Lee: yep. also include `effective_at` timestamp maybe, but not required.

Chloe Martin: we’ll need `action_id` to drive the console timeline / “promotion annotation”.

Priya Natarajan: +1.

Ethan Park: final question: do we need `COMPLETED` vs “RUNNING with current_stage=last”? I prefer explicit COMPLETED.

Jordan Lee: explicit COMPLETED. state machine is clearer and avoids corner cases where “running” but no transitions left.

Rafael Mendes: explicit COMPLETED helps with oncall automation too (“ignore alerts after completion” etc) and cleanup policies.

Benji Okafor: ok. COMPLETED when stage index reaches final and gates passed + promote executed.

Ethan Park: decision summary:
- States: DRAFT, RUNNING, HELD, ABORTED, COMPLETED
- Terminal: ABORTED/COMPLETED
- DRAFT editable; stage definitions immutable after RUNNING
- HELD blocks normal promote; allow abort; allow force_promote if permissioned
- promote requires idempotency key + expected_version; abort safe idempotent if already aborted (no new audit event)
I’ll update the ticket + PR notes.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please tag me when you add RBAC checks for force_promote/hold, want to review the permission names.

Chloe Martin: and when response schema is final, can you drop it here? we’ll align console backend.

Ethan Park: will do. pushing notes now.

source.messages:
Ethan Park: we need to settle API resource shape for Dedicated staged rollouts. question: is rollout plan its own resource, or embedded under dedicated pool config?

Benji Okafor: +1. current draft in my branch is a top-level `DedicatedPoolRolloutPlan` with `pool_id` FK. endpoints like `/v1/dedicated-pool-rollout-plans/{plan_id}`. but I keep getting the "why not just stick it in pool config" question.

Marissa Cole: from Console POV, I’d *really* prefer it as its own resource. we need audit history + stage timeline + promotion actions without making pool update a giant god-object.

Jordan Lee: also determinism/auditability: promotion/abort are state machine transitions. treating as "update pool config" feels wrong (and makes idempotency messy).

Mina Haddad: routing layer cares about a stable pointer: pool -> active rollout plan -> current stage. if it’s embedded in pool config, every pool config write becomes a potential routing-affecting mutation. higher blast radius.

Ethan Park: the argument for embedding is "single place to look" + backwards compatibility with existing pool update flows. but I agree, pool config is already overloaded.

Benji Okafor: here are the two options as I see them:

Option A (separate resource):
- GET `/v1/dedicated-pools/{pool_id}/rollout-plans` (list)
- POST `/v1/dedicated-pools/{pool_id}/rollout-plans` (create)
- POST `/v1/dedicated-pool-rollout-plans/{plan_id}:promote`
- POST `/v1/dedicated-pool-rollout-plans/{plan_id}:abort`

Option B (embedded):
- PATCH `/v1/dedicated-pools/{pool_id}` with `rollout_plan` subobject
- POST `/v1/dedicated-pools/{pool_id}:promoteRolloutStage` etc

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC is simpler with separate resource IMO. we can say: `dedicated.pool.read` vs `dedicated.rollout.manage`. embedding tends to leak manage permission into "pool admin" personas.

Ravi Menon: +1 to separate. we also need to handle multi-region pools where plan exists but region availability differs. would rather not couple that to base pool config semantics.

Jordan Lee: re: backward compat: if we embed it, we either:
1) add new fields on existing pool API responses (ok) but also
2) overload existing optimistic locking / etag behavior for pool updates

promotion is *not* a patch; it’s an action with preconditions + audit event. the plan being a resource is the canonical model.

Talia Benson: timeline note: we need an answer by EOW to unblock ENG-5932 + Console backend contract. can we converge on "plan as resource" and just provide convenience fields on pool?

Ethan Park: what about discoverability? operators typically start from pool page. if plan is separate, we need an easy link in API + Console.

Marissa Cole: we can do both:
- pool GET includes `active_rollout_plan_id` and `rollout_status_summary`
- Console uses those to deep-link to plan view.

Benji Okafor: yes. pool response could include:
```json
{
  "id": "pool_123",
  "name": "lattice-prod-usw2",
  "active_rollout_plan_id": "rplan_456",
  "active_rollout_stage": "stage_10_percent",
  "rollout_state": "RUNNING"
}
```
(no embedding of full stage list)

Mina Haddad: routing wants exactly that. it can fetch plan details from control plane cache keyed by `active_rollout_plan_id`. avoids pulling giant pool payloads.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also security: full plan may include internal-only gate evidence links / override reasons. don’t want that accidentally exposed via pool read endpoints for customer admins.

Ethan Park: ok but what about existing automation scripts that do pool updates for model version changes? they’ll ask "where do I set model version". if plan is separate, do they create a plan to change model version? or do we still support direct pool config changes?

Jordan Lee: we probably need both. direct pool updates remain (for non-staged changes), but staged rollout is the *recommended* mechanism and may be required behind flag for certain change types.

Benji Okafor: +1. control plane can enforce: if pool has `rollout_enforced=true` and change touches model/runtime/routing policy, reject PATCH pool and require a rollout plan.

Marissa Cole: that’s consistent with UX too. pool config page can show banner: "Changes to model/runtime require staged rollout".

Ravi Menon: if we embed, we risk accidental "partial updates" where some clients PATCH pool and unintentionally clobber rollout fields (or vice versa). separate resource prevents config drift.

Ethan Park: agreed. last question: path shape: do we want nested under pool, or purely top-level?

Benji Okafor: I’d like both patterns for ergonomics:
- canonical: `/v1/dedicated-pool-rollout-plans/{plan_id}`
- convenience: `/v1/dedicated-pools/{pool_id}/rollout-plans` (server just filters by pool_id)

Jordan Lee: +1. actions should be on the plan resource, not pool. `:promote` and `:abort` hang off plan id.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please make sure promote/abort accept idempotency key header (or request field) so retries don’t double-promote. also include `actor_reason` string for audit.

Marissa Cole: Console backend needs a stable contract:
- list stages
- show current stage
- show available actions (promote/hold/abort)

separate plan resource makes that sane.

Ethan Park: consensus seems clear: plan as first-class resource; pool gets pointers/summary fields for discoverability; direct pool patch remains but can be restricted by policy. Benji can you update the API doc + thread in ENG-5932?

Benji Okafor: yup. I’ll update draft endpoints to:
- `POST /v1/dedicated-pools/{pool_id}/rollout-plans` create
- `GET /v1/dedicated-pools/{pool_id}/rollout-plans?state=...`
- `POST /v1/dedicated-pool-rollout-plans/{plan_id}:promote` (+ idempotency)
- `POST /v1/dedicated-pool-rollout-plans/{plan_id}:abort`

and add pool fields `active_rollout_plan_id`, `rollout_state`, `rollout_stage_id`.

Talia Benson: :thumbsup: I’ll note decision in the program tracker + ping Console/backend folks.

Ethan Park: thanks all. next up is how we handle plan versioning + concurrent edits, but separate thread.

source.content:
Logan Wright: syncing here on onboarding checklist eventing. main open Q: emit events from API gateway/control plane vs rely on telemetry pipeline inference. want to lock this before schema v1 goes out.

Amara Diallo: from gateway side we can emit pretty cleanly for request lifecycle stuff: first 2xx, first streaming established, first 429, auth failures (401/403), etc. question is if we want to do that in-line (synchronous) or async fire-and-forget.

Nadia Rahman: telemetry pipeline can *infer* some of this from existing request logs, but it’s messy (sampling, field drift, different sources per region). also hard to guarantee “first” semantics without idempotency.

Ethan Park: for control plane events (API key created, quota/budget configured, model pinned, fallback policy saved) it’s not in gateway anyway. we should emit at the write path in control plane.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. API key created is in auth service / control plane. we can instrument there with stable `api_key_id` (hashed) + `tenant_id`.

Brian Murphy: concern: if we emit in multiple places, we’ll double count. ex: first successful request could be inferred from usage tables later *and* emitted from gateway.

Caleb Johnson: pipeline inference is fine for backfills / historical, but for checklist UX we need near-real-time and consistent semantics. gateway emission gives us low latency + consistent fields.

Logan Wright: proposal i’m leaning toward: “source of truth” events are server-side emissions at the point of truth (gateway for request outcomes, control plane for config writes). pipeline can enrich/route, but shouldn’t be the thing deciding what happened.

Amara Diallo: if we do gateway emit, we need to keep it lightweight. no blocking calls to telemetry. we can enqueue to our internal event bus (same pattern as metering) and drop on backpressure w/ metrics.

Nadia Rahman: yeah, ingestion should be write-only + tolerant. we can accept eventual consistency for the checklist (seconds-minutes), but not “hours later when the batch job runs”.

Chloe Martin: from Console side, we just need a stable progress service API. but we do need ordering guarantees—dogfood already showed out-of-order is common across regions.

Caleb Johnson: ordering: include `event_ts` (server time), `request_id`, and an idempotency key per event type (like `tenant_id + step + unique_id`). progress service should apply monotonic state transitions (can’t go from complete -> incomplete).

Mei Lin: also for TTFS/TTFP metrics we’ll join across datasets. if gateway emits “first successful request”, we should still validate against billing/usage to catch missing events.

Allison Grant: SRE lens: emitting from gateway is ok if we cap cardinality and have clear failure modes. if telemetry pipeline is down, gateway shouldn’t cascade. please add a counter + alert on dropped onboarding events per region.

Brian Murphy: what about client-side SDK signals? folks wanted “user completed quickstart” or “used streaming helper”. can we rely on user-agent tags instead?

Logan Wright: i’m ok with *limited client hints*, but not trusting SDK to declare completion. user-agent tags for attribution are fine (ex: `sdk=python`, `onboarding=quickstart`) but completion should be based on server-observed behavior.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: privacy note: no prompt text, no raw emails. tenant_id ok. api_key_id must be hashed + scoped. avoid IP address capture.

Amara Diallo: gateway can emit these event names (draft):
- `onboarding.api_request.first_success_2xx`
- `onboarding.api_request.first_stream_established`
- `onboarding.api_request.first_429_rate_limited`
- `onboarding.api_request.first_401_auth`
we can also emit `onboarding.api_request.first_timeout`? but that’s client-side usually.

Nadia Rahman: timeout is tricky. we can capture gateway-side timeouts (upstream timeout) but not client disconnect. i’d keep MVP to the ones we can observe reliably.

Ethan Park: control plane list (draft):
- `onboarding.api_key.created`
- `onboarding.quota.updated` (budget/quota)
- `onboarding.model_pinning.updated`
- `onboarding.fallback_policy.updated`
these map directly to checklist steps.

Chloe Martin: + admin override events too? like support marking a step complete should be auditable.

Logan Wright: yes but separate namespace: `onboarding.checklist.override_applied` w/ `actor_type=support|admin`, reason enum.

RedwoodBot: Reminder: Please add decisions to ADR-2025-02-onboarding-checklist-eventing once finalized. Reply with "ADR READY" when aligned.

Allison Grant: one more: please document retention + which dataset these land in (prod analytics vs operational). if these are used in-app, we need the operational pipeline to meet freshness SLO.

Logan Wright: ok sounds like alignment:
1) emit server-side at source of truth (gateway + control plane)
2) pipeline may enrich/route/backfill but not infer completion for checklist
3) client hints limited to attribution (user-agent tags) only
4) idempotency + monotonic progress in progress service
Amara/Ethan can you confirm owners for implementation + field list? i’ll update ADR.

Amara Diallo: confirmed for gateway emission. i’ll draft the exact payload fields + rate limiting / drop behavior.

Ethan Park: confirmed for control plane. will coordinate w/ Kaitlyn for api key event.

Nadia Rahman: confirmed for ingestion side. we’ll define the topic + schema versioning. also can we keep event_name set tight to avoid drift.

Logan Wright: ADR READY (after i paste notes).

Ethan Park: Kicking off schema discussion for Dedicated burst config in control plane. Need to lock: fields, validation rules, and migration/back-compat. Goal is to get something Benji can wire into `tenant-config` API this week.

Benji Okafor: Current thought is add `burst` object under `dedicated_capacity` in Tenant model (JSONB + API schema). Something like:
```
dedicated_capacity:
  baseline_gpus: 32
  burst:
    enabled: true
    max_burst_ratio: 1.5
    max_burst_gpus: 16
    cooldown_seconds: 300
    priority_tier: "silver"
```
We need to decide precedence if both ratio + absolute cap set.

Jordan Lee: +1 on nesting under dedicated. Please don’t put it at root; policy engine wants “burst is a property of dedicated reservations”.

Jordan Lee: Re: precedence: IMO compute effective cap as min(ratio_cap, abs_cap) when both set, but require at least one is set if enabled.

Ethan Park: That’s what I was leaning to. Also validation: ratio >= 0, abs cap >= 0, cooldown >= 0. But PM wants guardrails so customers can’t configure something insane.

Talia Benson: Reminder from PRD review: default GA guardrail is “max burst ratio <= 2.0 unless approved”. We can enforce in control plane validation and allow override via internal admin flag.

Benji Okafor: How do we want to express “approved override”? Separate field like `allow_over_max_ratio: true` feels gross.

Naomi Feldman: Please avoid “magic override booleans” in customer-visible config. If there is an override, make it internal-only (admin endpoint / internal config scope) and ensure it’s audited.

Ethan Park: Agree. Proposal: API accepts `max_burst_ratio` up to 2.0 for customer role. Admin role can set up to e.g. 3.0 (still bounded). RBAC + audit event covers it.

Cole Summers: Jumping in from billing side: we also need to know *what tier* they’re on for pricing/metering (and to block burst if contract doesn’t include it). Is `priority_tier` the same thing as packaging tier?

Ethan Park: Not necessarily. Priority tier is runtime scheduling/fairness. Packaging is commercial.

Cole Summers: Then we need a commercial flag too (even if internal). Otherwise metering will export usage but billing won’t know if it’s billable vs beta/free.

Benji Okafor: Could be `burst.billing_plan`? But we said avoid stuffing too much into config.

Jordan Lee: Strong preference: keep runtime policy inputs (caps, cooldown, priority) separate from billing metadata.

Cole Summers: Ok. But we do need *some* linkage. Even “burst.enabled” should only be set when contract allows it.

Ethan Park: How about this split:
- Customer-configurable (or at least customer-visible) under `dedicated_capacity.burst`: caps/cooldown/priority
- Commercial entitlement lives in separate `entitlements` object controlled by internal systems (SalesOps/Billing). Control plane admission checks both.

Chloe Martin: +1 split. Console can show “Burst: Enabled” but the source of truth should be entitlement + config. Otherwise we get weird states (enabled but not billable / not allowed).

Benji Okafor: Works. In tenant schema we already have `entitlements` (used for log retention etc). We can add `entitlements.dedicated_burst=true` and keep the `burst` config purely operational.

Naomi Feldman: From compliance POV, config changes need audit events:
- burst enabled/disabled
- cap changes (ratio/abs)
- cooldown changes
- priority changes
- entitlement changes (separate event)
Also: record “actor”, “source” (Console/API/admin tool), and before/after.

Ethan Park: Yup, audit events are tracked in the separate doc but we’ll ensure schema supports it.

Vikram Singh: Data plane integration question: do we want `cooldown_seconds` per tenant in config or will admission controller hardcode? We need determinism + avoid flapping if control plane changes mid-window.

Jordan Lee: Put it in config. Admission controller should read config snapshot and apply deterministically for request_ts. If config updates, it should take effect on next evaluation tick, not retroactively.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: config is cached ~30s in admission controller. We should document that updates may take up to 30s to propagate.

Chloe Martin: Console will need to show “last updated” + “may take up to N seconds” messaging; but that’s separate. Just confirming we aren’t promising instant.

Ethan Park: Back to validation rules. Draft:
- If `burst.enabled=false` => allow other fields to be null/omitted (don’t reject)
- If `burst.enabled=true` => require at least one of `max_burst_ratio` or `max_burst_gpus`
- If both provided => effective cap = min
- `max_burst_ratio` (customer) in [0, 2.0]; (admin) in [0, 3.0]
- `max_burst_gpus` in [0, baseline_gpus]?? or allow > baseline?

Jordan Lee: Allow > baseline. Some customers may reserve small baseline but want bigger bursts (within pool constraints). But we should still cap it via max ratio / fleet safety.

Talia Benson: Product expectation was burst is “temporarily exceed baseline” but not necessarily limited to baseline. So allow > baseline, but put a hard upper bound, e.g. <= 4x baseline or <= 256 GPUs, whichever smaller.

Benji Okafor: Careful w/ baseline=0 edge cases (some old tenants in staging?). Validation should guard division by zero if ratio is used.

Ethan Park: Good call. If baseline_gpus missing/0 and ratio is set, reject enablement. If only absolute cap is set, baseline can still be 0? But Dedicated should always have baseline.

Benji Okafor: There’s a couple legacy “dedicated-like” tenants with baseline in another field. I can migrate them but not in this PR.

Ethan Park: Migration strategy:
1) Add new optional `dedicated_capacity.burst` object in schema (no backfill)
2) Default behavior when missing: `enabled=false`
3) One-time migration script for known legacy tenants to populate baseline in dedicated_capacity if needed (separate ticket)
4) Gate enablement behind feature flag + entitlement

Jordan Lee: + Make sure PATCH semantics won’t accidentally wipe nested objects. We’ve had “partial update nukes JSONB” incidents.

Benji Okafor: Yeah. I’ll implement merge-patch for `dedicated_capacity` or add explicit endpoints:
- `POST /tenants/{id}/dedicated/burst:configure`
- `POST /tenants/{id}/dedicated/burst:disable`
This also makes audit events cleaner.

Naomi Feldman: Please prefer explicit endpoints for anything that impacts capacity/policy. Generic PATCH with JSON is hard to reason about and audit.

Cole Summers: + explicit endpoints gives billing a stable integration point too (we can hook entitlement checks + record reason codes).

Chloe Martin: Console team prefers explicit endpoints as well; easier to model UI states and avoid partial update bugs.

Ethan Park: Ok decision: explicit endpoints, burst config stored under `dedicated_capacity.burst`, entitlements separate. Benji can you post a proposed proto/schema in-thread for final review?

Benji Okafor: Will do. Drafting now. Also question: `priority_tier` enum values? I’ve seen “gold/silver/standard” floated.

Jordan Lee: Keep it simple: `standard`, `priority` (or `silver`), `enterprise` (or `gold`). Naming bikeshed but we need stable wire values.

Talia Benson: Product called them gold/silver in pricing draft. Might as well align.

Ethan Park: Ok, enum: `standard`, `silver`, `gold`. Default `standard`.

Benji Okafor: Posting proposed schema (wire values only):
```
message DedicatedBurstConfig {
  bool enabled = 1;
  optional double max_burst_ratio = 2; // 0..2 customer, 0..3 admin
  optional int32 max_burst_gpus = 3;   // 0..256 hard cap
  int32 cooldown_seconds = 4;         // default 300
  enum PriorityTier { STANDARD=0; SILVER=1; GOLD=2; }
  PriorityTier priority_tier = 5;
}
```
Validation rules as Ethan listed + hard cap 256.

Vikram Singh: Works for data plane. Please ensure `cooldown_seconds` min is like 60 to prevent thrash.

Jordan Lee: +1 min 60. Also if cooldown changes, don’t reset token bucket immediately (admission controller should apply next epoch). Might be in service logic not schema.

Ethan Park: Great. Benji please capture the min cooldown in validation (>=60). I’ll write up final validation spec + migration notes in the control plane doc.

Naomi Feldman: Last ask: make sure audit event includes denial of invalid configs too (who attempted, what was rejected). Not as critical, but useful.

Benji Okafor: Ack. I’ll log failed configure attempts (sanitized) at least in admin audit stream.

Ethan Park: Thanks all. I’ll summarize decisions and link from PM-528 + ENG-4816. :thumbsup:

source.text:
Talia Benson: ok team, per go/no-go prep: we need owners assigned for the v1 launch checklist items for unit econ dashboard. dropping the list here — please claim + confirm ETA.

Talia Benson: Checklist items:
1) SLOs + alerts finalized (freshness/completeness + routing)
2) Runbooks linked from alerts (investigation + backfill steps)
3) Backfill plan for 2025Q1 + throttling window
4) Access controls (feature flag + RBAC allowlist + audit)
5) Comms plan (dogfood -> Finance/Product/Eng rollout) + caveats
6) Finance signoff checklist (methodology + reconciliation report)
7) Model/region/tier dimension “known issues” doc for v1

Dylan Brooks: +1. let’s lock owners today. we’re too close to keep this floating.

Sean Gallagher: I can take (1) SLOs + alert routing, but I’ll need Allison/Aisha for the actual alert rules + dashboard panels.

Allison Grant: yep, I’ll own implementing the alert rules + the oncall routing config. Sean can own the targets + escalation path.

Aisha Bello: I can do the Grafana bits + alert tuning thresholds (reduce noise) once we agree SLOs. Also can add links in alert payloads.

Talia Benson: great. marking (1) as Sean (primary), Allison + Aisha (implementation).

Logan Wright: I’ll take (2) runbooks as primary, but want Caleb to be the “hands-on” owner for the queries + validation steps. Nadia for the token metering parity section.

Caleb Johnson: sure. I can own the “what to check first” decision tree + the warehouse validation queries. I’ll also add the common failure modes we’ve seen (missing region mapping, streaming undercount, ingestion lag).

Nadia Rahman: I’ll add the token metering sanity checks + parity notes (streaming vs non-streaming paths, tool calls). Can also add the “when to page runtime” part.

Talia Benson: perfect. (2) Logan primary, Caleb/Nadia contributors.

Mei Lin: For (3) backfill: I can own running + monitoring backfill jobs, and I’ll coordinate with SRE on throttling so we don’t melt the warehouse again.

Cole Summers: I’ll own the cost attribution inputs side + rate-card version pinning for the backfill window. Also can update the reconciliation view to ensure the backfilled hours line up w/ FP&A cutoffs.

Sean Gallagher: please include a “maintenance window” note + max concurrency settings. last time we had warehouse timeouts.

Mei Lin: yep, I’ll propose a run schedule + throttles and get SRE signoff.

Talia Benson: (3) Mei primary, Cole for cost inputs/versioning, SRE consulted.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I can take (4) access controls. I’ll verify feature flag (internal_unit_econ_dashboard) + RBAC policy. Need Marcus for security review + audit log requirements.

Marcus Lin: ok. I’ll review the RBAC rules + ensure we’re not leaking customer-identifiable info in drilldowns (esp top-N tables). Also want to confirm audit logging is on for page access.

Chloe Martin: note: API endpoints already have authz checks, but UI gating needs to match. Kaitlyn, I’ll point you to the middleware used for Console internal tools.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: thx. I’ll also define the initial allowlist groups (Finance, Product, Eng leads) and who can grant access.

Talia Benson: (4) Kaitlyn primary, Marcus security reviewer, Chloe consulted.

Marissa Cole: I’ll take (5) comms plan + caveats (what’s reliable in v1, what’s not). Talia, can you help with sequencing + stakeholder lists?

Talia Benson: yes, I’ll co-own comms sequencing. I’ll draft the rollout steps + deadlines (Eng->Product->Finance) and get Dylan signoff.

Felix Schneider: pls include a short “how to interpret blended vs marginal cost” blurb. that confusion came up in dogfood.

Marissa Cole: yup, will add a “read this first” section + link to metric dictionary.

Talia Benson: (5) Marissa primary, Talia for rollout coordination.

Rishi Malhotra: For (6) finance signoff checklist: I’ll own the methodology signoff items + reconciliation expectations. Need Laura to confirm final caveats + cadence for rate card updates.

Laura Bennett: agreed. I’ll do final approve on the methodology note once reconciliation report matches within the agreed tolerance band. Also want the dashboard to show which rate card version is active.

Cole Summers: rate card version display is already in the aggregation metadata table; we can surface it in the UI footer or info tooltip.

Chloe Martin: UI can show “Rate card: 2025Q4 v3” in the header. Nora was looking at that pattern already.

Talia Benson: great. (6) Rishi primary, Laura approver, Cole/Chloe for surfacing rate card version.

Jada Williams: On (7) “known issues” doc: I can draft a short list of current limitations + mitigations (e.g., unknown region bucket, tier mapping edge cases, quant profile cardinality constraints). Quentin, can you confirm model aliasing caveats?

Quentin Harris: yep. I’ll add the current model alias mapping behavior + guidance on grouping by model vs model_version. Also note any pending alias changes that could shift trend lines.

Nadia Rahman: add a note re: “confidence indicator” when dimension coverage drops (missing tier/region). even if we don’t show it in v1 UI, we should document how to interpret gaps.

Jada Williams: good call. I’ll include “how to spot incomplete coverage” + link to the internal pipeline health dashboard.

Talia Benson: (7) Jada primary, Quentin + Nadia contributors.

Dylan Brooks: this looks good. please post links to the docs/tickets as you create them. also: hard deadline for checklist complete is EOW so we can do go/no-go Monday.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll post SLO doc draft in #eng-sre for review today, then link here.

Allison Grant: I’ll open the PR to add/adjust alert rules once SLO targets are finalized. Will include runbook links in payload.

Caleb Johnson: I’ll start the runbook doc from the previous incident notes and share for review.

Mei Lin: I’ll propose backfill window + throttling settings and get signoff from SRE + warehouse owners.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll verify RBAC + feature flag in staging today, then schedule prod enablement with audit logging checked.

Marissa Cole: I’ll draft comms + caveats and circulate for Dylan/Laura review.

Talia Benson: thanks all. I’ll turn this into a checklist in the epic + track status async. please reply in-thread with links as they’re ready.

source.messages:
Mei Lin: Heads up: drilldowns on the internal Unit Economics dashboard are still spiky (p95 8–15s) when Finance runs 30d + group_by=model,region,tier. We need a partitioning/materialization plan before we scale dogfood. Can folks weigh in on the warehouse side?

Felix Schneider: +1. The API endpoint `GET /internal/unit-econ/timeseries?group_by=model,region,tier&window=30d` is the main offender. Console is basically doing top-N + timeseries in the same page load. If the underlying view is scanning too much, we’re toast.

Allison Grant: What’s the current layout? Are we still querying the "raw" metering rollup table (the one that has request_id-level rows) or are we on the hourly agg already?

Mei Lin: We’re on hourly agg, but it’s a wide table and not partitioned in a useful way. Current objects:
- `ANALYTICS.UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_FACT` (hourly aggregated, still ~1.2B rows because of extra dims)
- views: `ANALYTICS.VW_UNIT_ECON_DASHBOARD_*`
The fact table is keyed by `hour_ts`, `model_key`, `region_norm`, `customer_tier`, `quant_profile`, `streaming`, `route_id` (route_id is killing cardinality even with guardrails).

Logan Wright: To be clear: route_id should NOT be in the dashboard-facing cube. We added it for debugging but it should be separate (or sampled). Otherwise you can’t cluster effectively.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also route_id coverage isn’t stable. It’ll tank our completeness checks because it flips when we deploy gateway routes.

Chloe Martin: Agree. From API side we do not expose route_id. We only filter on model/region/tier/quant/streaming. Route was just “nice-to-have” for eng.

Mei Lin: Ok then proposal: split the fact table into:
1) `UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_CORE_FACT` (dims: hour_ts, model_key, region_norm, customer_tier, quant_profile, streaming)
2) `UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_DEBUG_FACT` (includes route_id + extra tags) with shorter retention
And we materialize dashboard views exclusively from CORE.

Sean Gallagher: Before we create more tables, can we sanity-check expected query patterns? Is it basically always bounded by time + group_by among those dims?

Felix Schneider: Yep. Typical patterns:
- time series over N days (hourly buckets) for blended $/1M output tokens
- top-N tables (e.g. top 10 models by $/1M output tokens, optionally filtered by region/tier)
- compare two ranges (last 7d vs prev 7d) which becomes two scans
No one is doing per-customer or per-route.

Allison Grant: What warehouse are we assuming? If this is Snowflake, “partitioning” isn’t a thing but CLUSTER BY + pruning is.

Mei Lin: Snowflake.

Allison Grant: Then the most impactful levers are:
- ensure queries always have a predicate on `hour_ts`
- add a derived `day` column (DATE(hour_ts)) and cluster on (day, model_key, region_norm, customer_tier) or similar
- avoid high-cardinality columns in cluster keys
- materialize the expensive join(s) (rate card / allocation inputs)

Cole Summers: Re: expensive joins: right now `VW_UNIT_ECON_DASHBOARD_HOURLY` joins `UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_FACT` to `GPU_RATE_CARD_HOURLY` on (region_norm, capacity_type, hour_ts) and then applies allocation multipliers. That join is *not* cheap.

Mei Lin: Yep. My thought is to compute cost_usd in the agg job and store it (already partially done) so dashboard queries don’t join rate card at runtime.

Logan Wright: That’s consistent with the ADR direction. Rate card versioning should be baked into the hourly aggregates (with a `rate_card_version` column) so Finance can reproduce QTD.

Aisha Bello: Any changes here need to be reflected in SLO checks. If we add new tables, we need completeness checks (hour coverage + key dimensions) on the new CORE fact.

Mei Lin: Plan draft (please shoot holes):
A) Schema change: create `UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_CORE_FACT` with:
- `hour_ts` (TIMESTAMP_NTZ)
- `day` (DATE) computed
- dims: `model_key`, `model_version`, `region_norm`, `customer_tier`, `quant_profile`, `streaming`
- measures: `output_tokens`, `input_tokens`, `request_count`, `gpu_cost_usd`, `overhead_cost_usd`, `total_cost_usd`
- derived: `blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens` as view calc (not stored)
B) Clustering:
- CLUSTER BY (day, model_key, region_norm, customer_tier)
C) Materializations:
- Create a materialized view for the most common query shape:
  `MV_UNIT_ECON_HOURLY_BY_MRTPQS` (hour_ts + the 6 dims, sums of measures)
  (Yes it’s basically the same as the table but Snowflake MV can help with pruning + query rewrite)
- Create an additional daily rollup table `UNIT_ECONOMICS_DAILY_CORE_FACT` for Finance views and “30d default” (reduces points)
D) Retention:
- CORE_FACT: 400 days
- DEBUG_FACT (route_id): 30 days, and not wired to console

Felix Schneider: Question: do we need hourly resolution for 30d default? The chart can downsample after 7d. If daily rollup exists, we can fetch daily for >14d windows.

Chloe Martin: We can do that in the API contract pretty easily: if window > 14d and group_by includes multiple dims, switch to daily endpoint. But Product wanted “hourly freshness” for incident-ish debugging.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE side: hourly is useful when we’re chasing a metering drop or ingestion lag. But Finance doesn’t need hourly past like 14d.

Allison Grant: You can satisfy both by having both tables and deciding in the API:
- <= 7d: hourly
- > 7d: daily (unless user explicitly selects “hourly” w/ warning)
That also reduces warehouse load.

Logan Wright: +1, but please keep the *definition* consistent between hourly and daily (daily = sum hourly, not separately recomputed).

Mei Lin: Yep daily is derived strictly from CORE hourly.

Caleb Johnson: Data quality concern: if daily is derived, we need a check that daily totals match summed hourly within epsilon and flag drift.

Mei Lin: Adding to DQ checklist.

Cole Summers: Rate card versioning: if we bake cost_usd into CORE_FACT, we must store `rate_card_version` and `allocation_model_version` so Finance can reconcile when those change.

Mei Lin: Agree. I’ll add both columns.

Felix Schneider: Another thing: current queries do `ORDER BY blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens DESC LIMIT 10` for top-N. If computed as `sum(cost)/sum(tokens)` across a big scan, MV/daily rollup will help, but we also should have a precomputed "top models" helper table?

Allison Grant: Precomputing top-N is tricky because it depends on filters. Better is: reduce scan size and ensure pruning works. Clustering by day + model_key will at least avoid full table scans.

Sean Gallagher: Also: watch out for MV refresh costs. If the hourly job writes continuously, MVs can churn.

Mei Lin: For Snowflake MVs, we can keep them narrow and only on CORE dims. Alternatively we can skip MV and instead use a separate aggregated table updated hourly (idempotent upsert). That’s basically what we’re doing already, just need to fix the dimension explosion.

Logan Wright: My vote: avoid MV unless we need it. Start with better CORE table + clustering + daily rollup. Measure. Add MV only if still slow.

Chloe Martin: +1. We already cache API responses for common views. If the base query goes from 15s to ~1–2s, caching will cover the rest.

DataOps Bot: Reminder: Warehouse maintenance window tonight 02:00–03:00 PT. Heavy backfills should be avoided during that period.

Aisha Bello: If we’re rebuilding tables, can we schedule backfill throttling? Last time the backfill saturated the warehouse and we paged for unrelated latency.

Mei Lin: Yes. Backfill plan:
- backfill CORE_FACT in 7-day chunks
- cap warehouse to medium, use task concurrency=2
- run outside 02:00–03:00 PT
I’ll coordinate with Sean.

Sean Gallagher: Works. If you post the run commands + expected runtime, we can keep an eye on query queueing.

Mei Lin: Concrete next steps:
1) I’ll open PR for schema split + clustering (CORE/DEBUG) and remove route_id from dashboard path.
2) Add DAILY rollup job derived from CORE.
3) Chloe/Felix: update API to use daily for >14d by default + add UI note.
4) Caleb/Aisha: add DQ checks: (a) hourly completeness by (region_norm,tier) (b) daily==sum(hourly) validation.

Felix Schneider: Sounds good. Please tag me on the PR so I can test the dashboard queries against the new views.

Logan Wright: +1, and please also ping Nadia if you touch metering dims. Removing route_id from the agg should reduce cardinality, but want to ensure we still can debug via DEBUG_FACT.

Mei Lin: Will do. I’ll post a doc snippet w/ new table definitions + sample queries here once the PR is up.

Allison Grant: When you have it, share `EXPLAIN` plans for the worst dashboard query before/after. That’ll tell us if pruning is actually happening.

Mei Lin: Will grab before/after EXPLAIN + query profile screenshots. Thanks all.

Mei Lin: Heads up: drilldowns on the internal Unit Economics dashboard are still spiky (p95 8–15s) when Finance runs 30d + group_by=model,region,tier. We need a partitioning/materialization plan before we scale dogfood. Can folks weigh in on the warehouse side?

Felix Schneider: +1. The API endpoint `GET /internal/unit-econ/timeseries?group_by=model,region,tier&window=30d` is the main offender. Console is basically doing top-N + timeseries in the same page load. If the underlying view is scanning too much, we’re toast.

Allison Grant: What’s the current layout? Are we still querying the "raw" metering rollup table (the one that has request_id-level rows) or are we on the hourly agg already?

Mei Lin: We’re on hourly agg, but it’s a wide table and not partitioned in a useful way. Current objects:
- `ANALYTICS.UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_FACT` (hourly aggregated, still ~1.2B rows because of extra dims)
- views: `ANALYTICS.VW_UNIT_ECON_DASHBOARD_*`
The fact table is keyed by `hour_ts`, `model_key`, `region_norm`, `customer_tier`, `quant_profile`, `streaming`, `route_id` (route_id is killing cardinality even with guardrails).

Logan Wright: To be clear: route_id should NOT be in the dashboard-facing cube. We added it for debugging but it should be separate (or sampled). Otherwise you can’t cluster effectively.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also route_id coverage isn’t stable. It’ll tank our completeness checks because it flips when we deploy gateway routes.

Chloe Martin: Agree. From API side we do not expose route_id. We only filter on model/region/tier/quant/streaming. Route was just “nice-to-have” for eng.

Mei Lin: Ok then proposal: split the fact table into:
1) `UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_CORE_FACT` (dims: hour_ts, model_key, region_norm, customer_tier, quant_profile, streaming)
2) `UNIT_ECONOMICS_HOURLY_DEBUG_FACT` (includes route_id + extra tags) with shorter retention
And we materialize dashboard views exclusively from CORE.

Sean Gallagher: Before we create more tables, can we sanity-check expected query patterns? Is it basically always bounded by time + group_by among those dims?

Felix Schneider: Yep. Typical patterns:
- time series over N days (hourly buckets) for blended $/1M output tokens
- top-N tables (e.g. top 10 models by $/1M output tokens, optionally filtered by region/tier)
- compare two ranges (last 7d vs prev 7d) which becomes two scans
No one is doing per-customer or per-route.

Allison Grant: What warehouse are we assuming? If this is Snowflake, “partitioning” isn’t a thing but CLUSTER BY + pruning is.

Mei Lin: Snowflake.

source.content:
Caleb Johnson: Heads up: data quality issue for Usage Insights. We’re missing `route_canonical` / `api_route` tags for *streaming* requests in **eu-west-1**.

Seeing a spike in `unknown_route` bucket starting ~yesterday 18:00 UTC. Non-streaming looks normal. This will make endpoint breakdown misleading.

Quick numbers from the quality dashboard: ~11–14% of metering events in eu-west-1 have empty route tag, and the examples all look like `/v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true`.

Can someone from Telemetry/Platform help triage? I’ll paste a sample event payload if needed.

Logan Wright: Ugh, thanks for catching. Do you know if it’s only the OpenAI-compat routes or all streaming endpoints (including our `/v1/generate`)?

Caleb Johnson: So far I only sampled `/v1/chat/completions` + `/v1/completions` (OpenAI-compat). Didn’t check `/v1/generate` yet.

Thomas Baird: I can pull a warehouse slice to confirm scope. Any specific field name you’re checking? We’ve got `route_canonical` on the analytics side, but ingestion sometimes calls it `api_route`.

Caleb Johnson: For the raw metering stream I’m checking `api_route` and `http.route` (both empty). In the derived dataset, `route_canonical` ends up null -> mapped to `unknown_route`.

Mei Lin: I ran a quick query on `metering_events_v2` (last 6h).

```
select
  region,
  count(*) as total,
  sum(case when api_route is null or api_route = '' then 1 else 0 end) as missing_route,
  round(100.0 * sum(case when api_route is null or api_route = '' then 1 else 0 end) / count(*), 2) as missing_pct
from metering_events_v2
where ts >= dateadd('hour', -6, current_timestamp())
  and request_type = 'inference'
group by 1
order by missing_pct desc;
```

Result: eu-west-1 is ~12.7% missing. us-east-1/us-west-2 <0.2%.

If I add `and streaming = true` it becomes ~98% missing in eu-west-1. If `streaming = false` it’s basically zero missing.

Logan Wright: That’s super clear. So streaming code path in eu-west-1 isn’t attaching route tags.

Hypothesis: edge/gateway build skew in eu-west-1 OR the streaming proxy path bypasses the route normalization middleware.

Chloe Martin: +1 to “bypasses middleware”. We have a separate handler for streaming that does early flush. I vaguely remember we attach tags after response headers are written… which might never happen for stream.

But why only eu-west-1 then? That points to build skew / config flag.

Thomas Baird: I’ll check deploy versions. There’s a known thing where eu-west-1 is one release behind if the canary got stuck.

Felix Schneider: This will also explain why the Usage Insights query service is showing a higher-than-expected “Unknown endpoint” row for eu-west-1 when `stream=true` filters are used.

Dylan Brooks: From console POV, if we don’t fix soon we need a banner / partial-data warning for eu-west-1 streaming. That’s going to be a rough beta first impression.

Nadia Rahman: This is also a reconciliation risk. If route is missing we still have cost totals, but cost-by-endpoint attribution breaks (and customers will file “missing endpoint breakdown” tickets).

We should treat as beta-blocker for any EU accounts.

Allison Grant: Agree on blocker. Also we need an alert that keys off streaming+missing-route specifically. The generic “missing route tags” alert is too noisy / slow.

Logan Wright: Plan proposal:
1) Confirm where tags are added in streaming request lifecycle (gateway + streamer).
2) Check eu-west-1 gateway/edge versions + config.
3) Hotfix + rollout to eu-west-1.
4) Backfill? (at least for the beta evaluation window) if feasible.

Thomas Baird: I checked deploybot history: eu-west-1 edge is on `gateway@6d3a19f` while us-east-1 is `gateway@91b0c2a`. So yes, skew.

The diff includes a refactor to streaming handler. Could easily be it.

Mei Lin: I can pull one raw event from eu-west-1 streaming and one from us-east-1 streaming to compare tag fields.

Caleb Johnson: Yes please. That would help confirm it’s emitting path vs template.

Mei Lin: Comparison (sanitized) - eu-west-1 streaming event:
- `api_route`: ""
- `http.route`: null
- `http.path`: "/v1/chat/completions"
- `streaming`: true

us-east-1 streaming event:
- `api_route`: "/v1/chat/completions"
- `http.route`: "/v1/chat/completions"
- `http.path`: "/v1/chat/completions"
- `streaming`: true

So it’s purely missing tag emission, not normalization.

Chloe Martin: That lines up with middleware not running in eu-west-1 build.

If the tag is sourced from the router context, we might be dropping it when constructing the stream response handler.

Thomas Baird: I can roll eu-west-1 to match current gateway version today (assuming no other blockers). That might just fix it without code changes.

Logan Wright: Let’s do it, but I want a quick sanity check that the newer build doesn’t break anything else in EU.

Thomas: can you stage rollout (10% -> 50% -> 100%) and watch missing-route metric + error rate?

Thomas Baird: Yep. I’ll start canary in ~30 mins.

Dylan Brooks: If this isn’t resolved quickly, we should add a temporary guardrail in Insights UI: show a warning banner when missing-route > X% for selected region + streaming.

Felix Schneider: We already have a “data incomplete” banner plumbing in progress; I can add a condition based on the freshness/quality endpoint once we have a metric.

Allison Grant: +1 but please don’t ship UI logic based on ad hoc queries. We should expose a single quality signal: `missing_route_pct` per region + request_type + streaming.

Caleb Johnson: I can add it to the quality dashboard + alert rule today. Threshold proposal: warn at 1%, page at 5% for >15 min.

Nadia Rahman: For correctness: do we need to retroactively repair attribution? If we leave it, weekly “what changed” will show a fake jump in “Unknown endpoint” spend.

Logan Wright: Backfill depends on whether the raw events have enough info to reconstruct route tags. We have `http.path` but not normalized template. For OpenAI-compat routes, path is stable anyway (no IDs), so we can infer the route for those endpoints.

But we should be careful: if we infer, document it + limit to known stable routes.

Mei Lin: +1. For `/v1/chat/completions` it’s safe; for anything with IDs it’s not.

Chloe Martin: Given this is streaming + OpenAI-compat, it’s probably safe to map missing route to `http.path` for those specific paths during backfill. But long term we want the correct tag emission.

Thomas Baird: Canary update: eu-west-1 at 10% on `gateway@91b0c2a`.

Caleb Johnson: Watching the metric now. Missing-route for streaming in eu-west-1 dropped from ~98% to ~11% over last 5 min bucket. Looks promising.

(reaction :eyes: from Logan Wright)

Thomas Baird: That’s a strong signal it’s the build skew.

Rolling to 50%.

Felix Schneider: If this continues to drop, we can avoid UI warning work for beta (still add alert though).

Allison Grant: Still add the alert. This class of issue is exactly what will pop up again.

Logan Wright: Agreed.

Action items:
- Thomas: complete rollout to 100% in eu-west-1 + post final metrics
- Caleb: add dashboard panel + alert for `streaming_missing_route_pct` (warn 1%, page 5%)
- Mei: open a short postmortem note on why eu-west-1 got stuck behind (release process)
- Logan/Chloe: verify streaming handler path in gateway didn’t regress tag emission (add unit/integration test)

Also logging a ticket under ENG-3822 (route tag instrumentation) to add a regression test specifically for streaming.

Nadia Rahman: Please include a note for Finance/Support: if any customer asks about “Unknown endpoint” spike for EU streaming between ~18:00 UTC yesterday and rollout completion, we have the root cause.

Thomas Baird: Rollout complete (100%). Current 5-min bucket: streaming missing-route in eu-west-1 is 0.3% and trending down.

Caleb Johnson: Confirmed on my side. Unknown endpoint row is back to baseline.

(reaction :thumbsup: from Dylan Brooks)
(reaction :white_check_mark: from Allison Grant)

Logan Wright: Great. Next step is preventing recurrence: tightening region rollout parity + adding that regression test.

Thanks everyone.

source.messages:
Logan Wright: Question for the group re experiment metrics dims: what are we using as `route_id`? Right now I’m seeing a mix of raw path (`/v1/accounts/123/chat`) and templated (`/v1/accounts/{account_id}/chat`) depending on where the span is created.

Caleb Johnson: +1. In OTel we currently tag `http.route` when we have it, but gateway sometimes only has `http.target` (raw) unless the route matcher passes template down.

Allison Grant: Raw path is going to explode cardinality. We should not be emitting per-request metrics with raw path as a label, especially for anything under customers’ dynamic prefixes.

Jada Williams: Agree. For Console “Route Experiments” we already have a concept of “route” (policy route). That should map to a stable identifier, not raw URL.

Jordan Lee: From policy engine POV, we evaluate experiments after route match. We can attach a stable `route_uuid` (from route config) into request context. That’s the cleanest.

Mina Haddad: There’s also the usability angle: Console wants to show something human readable. If we only emit UUID, dashboards are useless without a join.

Mei Lin: For analytics joins are fine (we already join route table for rollouts views), but for oncall Grafana it’s painful if all you see is UUIDs.

Rafael Mendes: Oncall perspective: I want “which endpoint is burning” immediately. Route template is pretty good for that. Raw path is worst.

Logan Wright: So maybe we emit BOTH `route_id` (stable) and `route_template` (low-cardinality string)?

Allison Grant: Caution: “route_template” is only low-cardinality if it’s actually the *template*. If we accidentally pass raw, we’re back to cardinality hell. Needs normalization + validation.

Caleb Johnson: We can implement a guard: if `route_template` contains a long numeric segment or UUID-looking segment, drop/replace with `_` or just don’t set the label.

Dylan Brooks: What does Console use today for rollouts dashboards? Is it template or internal ID?

Mei Lin: Console rollouts uses internal route record (UUID) and then displays the saved “Route name” / “Path pattern” from config. The metrics pipeline currently keys by UUID for rollouts.

Jada Williams: For experiments we should align with that: canonical dimension is `route_id` (UUID) and optionally `route_pattern` for readability.

Jordan Lee: Implementation detail: gateway/router should set `route_id` as soon as route is matched (before experiment evaluator). Then experiment evaluator adds `{experiment_id, variant_id}`. That avoids re-parsing URL.

Logan Wright: What about customers who define multiple routes with same template but different methods/hosts? E.g. `POST /v1/chat/completions` vs `GET /v1/chat/completions`.

Caleb Johnson: Yeah, `http.route` alone doesn’t include method. We should include method either in the template string or as another label.

Allison Grant: Please don’t add more labels unless we have to. But method is tiny-cardinality (GET/POST/etc) so it’s acceptable. Host maybe not.

Rafael Mendes: Method matters for troubleshooting too. I’d be fine with `http.method` staying as-is, and `route_template` being path-only.

Mina Haddad: Another wrinkle: some customers mount routes with tenant prefixes (`/customer/acme/...`). Even if templated, that still adds cardinality per tenant.

Jada Williams: Those should not be treated as “route templates” from our perspective. Our route configs should use normalized patterns (we don’t recommend per-tenant path prefixes; they should use headers). But reality exists.

Allison Grant: We should define normalization rules:
- Prefer route config ID (UUID) as the canonical `route_id`
- For display labels, use route’s configured pattern *after* normalization (replace path params with `{param}` tokens)
- If the configured pattern itself is high-cardinality (contains literal tenant strings), we still emit route_id, and either (a) don’t emit route_template or (b) emit a coarse label like `custom_route`.

Logan Wright: Makes sense. But for Grafana, without route_template we lose quick triage.

Mei Lin: Could add a small lookup panel or a runbook link: “How to map route_id to route name in Console” / internal tool. But that’s slower.

Caleb Johnson: Another option: emit `route_group` derived from registry, like `chat-completions`, `embeddings`, etc. That’s stable + useful.

Dylan Brooks: Console already has “Route name” (user-provided) and “Endpoint” (system). We could encourage naming best practices in the wizard.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Oncall would rather have `route_name` than a path string anyway.

Jada Williams: Can we actually emit `route_name`? That’s user-controlled and might be long / PII / weird chars.

Allison Grant: Please no user-controlled freeform strings in metric labels.

Jordan Lee: From router we can safely emit:
- `route_id` (UUID)
- `route_pattern` (system-generated canonical pattern) if it’s from our registry, not user-supplied
- `http.method`

Logan Wright: OK, so where do we get `route_pattern`? Gateway match rules already know the configured pattern, right?

Caleb Johnson: Today: sometimes. In the API gateway we have it, but in some private deployments we’re missing the compiled route matcher metadata in the span exporter.

Mina Haddad: For hosted/dedicated we can plumb it through request context (same place we put experiment ids). For private we might need a fallback.

Allison Grant: Fallback should be: only emit `route_id` and method; do NOT try to derive from raw path.

Rafael Mendes: +1000. Deriving from raw path is how we got the cardinality spike last quarter.

Logan Wright: What about traces? Can we put raw path in traces (since sampled) and keep metrics normalized?

Caleb Johnson: Traces already have `http.target` (raw) in many cases. That’s OK because tracing backend can handle it better + sampling. The big risk is metrics labels.

Allison Grant: Exactly. Metrics: strict. Traces/logs: more flexible.

Dylan Brooks: So the concrete ask: define what we call it. `route_id` vs `route` vs `http.route`?

Logan Wright: ADR-048 uses `route_id` as canonical dimension. We can follow that and set `route_id` to UUID, and optionally set `http.route` to the pattern.

Jordan Lee: Works. In policy engine we can set `route_id` in context as `routing.route_id` and exporter maps to `route_id` tag.

Mei Lin: For analytics dataset, we’ll also store `route_id` and join to route registry to display name/pattern in Console results.

Rafael Mendes: Can we also update the runbook to call out “don’t use raw path as route id” and add a dashboard panel to show top route_ids by traffic so you can click into Console?

Allison Grant: Yes, and we should add an alert for “route_template cardinality anomaly” (e.g. too many distinct values) if we end up emitting template.

Caleb Johnson: I can add a sanity check in the OTel exporter: if `http.route` value count exceeds threshold in a window, drop `http.route` label and emit a counter like `route_label_dropped=1`.

Logan Wright: Love it. That also gives us a signal for misconfig.

Mina Haddad: One more: route normalization across regions. If route_id is UUID from config, it’s consistent across regions right?

Jordan Lee: Yep, config is global, route IDs are stable. That’s why UUID is best.

Allison Grant: Decision proposal:
1) Canonical dimension for experiments + metrics attribution: `route_id` (UUID from route config/registry)
2) Emit `http.route` ONLY when we have a system-generated canonical pattern; never derive it from raw request path
3) Keep `http.target` / raw path in traces/logs only
4) Include `http.method` as separate low-cardinality label
5) Add exporter guardrails to prevent accidental high-cardinality `http.route`

Dylan Brooks: +1

Rafael Mendes: +1

Mei Lin: +1

Caleb Johnson: +1, I’ll take the exporter guardrail action item.

Logan Wright: +1. I’ll update the metrics taxonomy doc + ping Console to make sure results view joins on route_id and shows pattern/name.

Jada Williams: I’ll make sure router sets `route_id` before experiment evaluation and that we propagate it through retries.

Allison Grant: Cool. I’ll summarize in the standards doc + link in the eng-sre thread where cardinality concerns came up.
